The Architect

A Blueprint for the Civilization That Survives Itself

Mircea S. Rusu
First Edition · 2026

The Architect
A Blueprint for the Civilization That Survives Itself

First Edition · 2026
ISBN: to be assigned

Written on Earth, for any world.

To the generations who will still be here.

The fault, dear Brutus, is not in our stars, but in ourselves, that we are underlings. — William Shakespeare, Julius Caesar (c. 1599)
We have to choose between a global market driven only by calculations of short-term profit, and one which has a human face. — Kofi Annan, address to the World Economic Forum (1999)
Look again at that dot. That’s here. That’s home. That’s us. — Carl Sagan, Pale Blue Dot (1994)

Preface

This book was written in a period when the architecture of human civilization began to visibly fail, in plain view of the very people it was built to serve. The institutions that govern our money, our laws, our work, our information, and our wars were designed for a world that no longer exists — a world of isolated communities, slow messengers, and modest tools. We now live inside a single, instantaneous, planetary network, and we are governing it with paperwork, with patronage, and with the inherited reflexes of empires that did not survive their own contradictions.

The argument of these pages is simple, and it is severe. Either humanity designs, in this century, an operating system worthy of its capacities, or it will lose the chance to design anything at all. The window is not abstract. It is measured in decades, not in centuries, and it is shrinking under the combined weight of ecological pressure, weapons capable of ending us in an afternoon, intelligences that already exceed us in narrow domains, and political systems incapable of acting at the speed of the problems they have created.

What follows is not a prophecy and not a manifesto. It is a blueprint — a long, patient, technically honest attempt to describe how a civilization of eight billion people, on one planet today and on many tomorrow, could be governed in a way that preserves dignity, makes corruption structurally impossible, places intelligence under human authority, returns work to human purpose, and points the species toward a single shared horizon. None of it requires saints. All of it requires architecture.

The reader should know, at the outset, what this book is not. It is not the design of a world government; it proposes a federation that any community may join, adapt, or leave. It is not anti-technological; it treats technology as the substrate on which the next civilization will either be built well or be built carelessly. It is not utopian; every claim made here is measured against the evidence we already have, and every failure mode is named in the open. It is not the property of any party, religion, or nation. It is offered, in the most literal sense, to whoever will use it.

A word about the form. This is written as an old book because the questions it asks are old. How shall we live together? How shall we be governed? What is owed to those who come after us? The new answer requires new instruments, but the question is the oldest question we have. I have therefore allowed myself the liberty of writing in the voice of an architect addressing an apprentice — patient, declarative, unhurried, and unwilling to flatter.

If you have opened this book, you already suspect that the present arrangement of the world will not hold. You are not wrong. What follows is the case that, even now, there is something better available to us — not as a dream, but as a buildable structure, drawn here in full, so that any group of serious people, in any city or any country, can begin construction the day they decide to begin.

— M. S. R.

Part One

The Diagnosis

The world as it is, examined honestly, before any proposal is made for what it might become.

Chapter One

The Civilization Built on Old Code

Every civilization is, in the end, a piece of code. Not in the narrow sense of machines and silicon, but in the older and more durable sense: a set of rules executed by a population, day after day, until the rules become indistinguishable from reality. A border is a rule. A currency is a rule. A court, a tax, a vote, a marriage, a passport, a contract, a frontier post — each is a line of code that human beings have agreed to run inside their own minds and inside the minds of their neighbors. So long as enough of us continue to execute the rule, it has the force of nature. When too few of us continue, it dissolves overnight, and we wonder how we ever believed in it.

The civilization we have inherited is running on code written, for the most part, between the seventeenth and the mid-twentieth century. The sovereign nation-state, fixed by the Peace of Westphalia in 1648, was an engineering solution to the wars of religion in central Europe (Kissinger 2014). The joint-stock corporation, generalized in English and Dutch law during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, was an engineering solution to the problem of pooling capital for ventures too large for any single household. The modern bureaucracy, codified by Weber at the turn of the twentieth century, was an engineering solution to the problem of administering populations measured in tens of millions (Weber 1922). The post-war international order — the United Nations, the Bretton Woods institutions, the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights — was an engineering solution to the moral catastrophe of two world wars in a single generation (Mazower 2012; United Nations General Assembly 1948).

These were, in their time, brilliant pieces of design. They achieved exactly what they were built to achieve. They ended the Thirty Years’ War. They financed the construction of railways and the laying of submarine cables. They produced a century of life expectancy gains unprecedented in human history. They contained — if barely — the appetites of two superpowers armed with weapons that could have ended the species in an afternoon. None of what follows in this book should be read as contempt for the builders of the old code. The argument is far more uncomfortable than contempt. The argument is that the code worked, and that the world it was written for no longer exists.

The world it was written for

Consider, for a moment, the assumptions baked into the institutions we inherited. They assume that information travels slowly — at the speed of a horse, a ship, or, at best, a telegraph wire — so that a parliament can deliberate for weeks while events wait politely outside the chamber. They assume that capital is local — that a bank in Hamburg or a mill in Lancashire is taxable by the polity in which it physically stands. They assume that violence is symmetrical — that an army must be assembled, fed, transported, and paid, so that war is the prerogative of states large enough to bear its cost. They assume that the planet is large — that what is dumped into a river in one country will not, within a human lifetime, alter the chemistry of an ocean on the other side of the world. They assume that human beings are the apex of cognition on Earth — that no other agent will compose treaties, draft contracts, persuade voters, or design weapons.

Every one of these assumptions has now failed. Information travels at the speed of light, and a rumor circulates the globe before a cabinet has finished its breakfast. Capital is no longer local; a few keystrokes move sums larger than the annual budget of a middle-income country between jurisdictions that compete to ask the fewest questions. Violence is no longer symmetrical; a single individual with a laptop can disable a hospital network, and a small group with the right code can interrupt the electricity, the fuel, the water, or the financial settlement of a nation (Perlroth 2021). The planet is no longer large; we have altered the composition of its atmosphere measurably, and the consequences are now legible in fires, in floods, in crop failures, and in the migration of tens of millions of people (IPCC 2023). And human beings are no longer the only authors in the room; systems we have built ourselves now write text, code, contracts, music, and military doctrine at a speed we cannot match, and in volumes we cannot read.

An honest observer, looking at this list, would conclude that the operating system of our civilization is being asked to perform tasks for which it was never specified. The wonder is not that it strains. The wonder is that it has not yet crashed.

The symptoms of obsolescence

What does obsolescence look like, in a civilization? It does not look like collapse. Collapse is the final frame. Long before collapse, the symptoms are subtler and far more familiar. They look like this.

They look like a public that has stopped believing the official numbers, even when the numbers are correct, because it has been lied to about other numbers often enough that trust has been spent. They look like elections in which the population votes against incumbents in cycle after cycle, in country after country, regardless of ideology, because no incumbent of any persuasion seems capable of solving the problems the population can see. They look like the steady accumulation of laws nobody reads, regulations nobody enforces, and treaties nobody honors, until the legal order itself becomes a kind of liturgy — recited, respected, and ignored. They look like billionaires whose private fortunes exceed the budgets of the states that nominally regulate them (UBS 2024). They look like wars that begin without declaration and end without treaty, in places nobody can find on a map, fought by drones nobody has met, against enemies nobody has named. They look like a generation of young people who have concluded, not loudly but quietly, that the future is something to be survived rather than built.

None of these symptoms, taken alone, is fatal. Taken together, they describe a system that has lost the capacity to adapt at the rate the environment is changing. In engineering, this condition has a name. It is called technical debt. It accumulates whenever a system is patched rather than redesigned, whenever a workaround is preferred to a fix, whenever the cost of refactoring is deferred until the cost of refactoring exceeds the cost of starting again. Every civilization that has ever fallen, fell first into technical debt. The lesson of the archaeological record is not that great societies are destroyed from outside. It is that they exhaust their capacity to maintain the systems on which they depend, and then a smaller shock — a drought, a plague, a war on the frontier — finishes what their own neglect began (Tainter 1988).

Five failures, in summary

The chapters that follow this one will name, in turn, the five failures that constitute the central diagnosis of this book. They are sketched here only briefly, so that the reader knows where the argument is going.

The first is the failure of trust. Public institutions, in nearly every measured democracy, are trusted by a smaller fraction of their citizens than at any point since reliable measurement began (OECD 2024). This is not because citizens have become more cynical. It is because the institutions have become less legible. A modern citizen cannot, even in principle, verify how a tax dollar is spent, how a vote is counted, how a regulator is appointed, or how a court reaches its conclusions. The machinery is sealed, and the public is asked to take its operation on faith. Faith, in such conditions, predictably erodes.

The second is the failure of distribution. The global economy now produces more than enough food, energy, housing, medicine, and information to permit every human being to live in dignity (World Bank 2024). It does not do so. A larger share of global wealth is held by a smaller fraction of the population than at any point in the modern era (UBS 2024). This is not the predictable outcome of a market. It is the predictable outcome of a market layered on top of legal, tax, and ownership rules written for an economy of land and factories, applied without revision to an economy of data, intellectual property, and global financial flows.

The third is the failure of work. The connection between human effort and human dignity, which industrial society took for granted, has been quietly dissolving for two generations and is now being severed at speed by automation, by platform capitalism, and by intelligent systems capable of performing entire categories of skilled labor for the marginal cost of electricity. The institutions that were built to manage this transition — labor law, social insurance, professional licensure — were designed for a world in which the worker was a person, the employer was a firm, and both were located in the same country. Two of those three assumptions are now optional, and the third is becoming so (Graeber 2018).

The fourth is the failure of law. National legal systems, written by national legislatures for national populations, cannot regulate planetary phenomena. They cannot, on their own, tax a corporation that exists in twelve jurisdictions and books its profits in none. They cannot prosecute a fraud committed in one country, against citizens of a second, using infrastructure rented in a third, by a defendant who lives in a fourth. They cannot constrain weapons of mass destruction held by states that have not signed the relevant treaties. They cannot, in the current arrangement, do any of these things, and the gap between what national law can do and what global activity requires has become the working environment of organized crime, of tax evasion, of human trafficking, and, increasingly, of states themselves.

The fifth is the failure of foresight. We have constructed a civilization whose decisions are made on horizons of months — the electoral cycle, the fiscal quarter, the news cycle — and whose consequences play out on horizons of decades or centuries. Climate, biodiversity, infrastructure, pensions, debt, scientific research, education, and the long, slow work of cultural transmission all operate on timescales that no existing political institution is structurally equipped to consider. We are, collectively, a species with the technological reach of a mature civilization and the planning horizon of an adolescent. That mismatch, more than any other, is what makes the present moment dangerous.

Why this book exists

It would be possible to write a far longer book about each of these failures, and many such books have been written. The reader who wishes to dwell on the diagnosis will find a vast literature waiting, in economics, in political science, in history, in sociology, in climate science, in the philosophy of technology. The original contribution this book attempts is different. It assumes that the diagnosis, in its broad outline, is now established. It assumes that the reader, having lived in the world for some number of decades, requires no extended demonstration that the world is unwell. It moves instead to the question that the diagnostic literature, with notable exceptions, has been reluctant to engage. That question is the engineering question. If the operating system is obsolete, what would a new one actually look like?

Not in metaphor. Not in vision. In architecture. With named components, named interfaces, named failure modes, named transitions from the present arrangement to the proposed one. With the dignity of being wrong in public, in detail, so that the design can be inspected, criticized, improved, replaced. The chapters that follow are an attempt to answer that question — not as the final word, but as a first draft serious enough to be argued with.

The argument proceeds in six parts. Part One, of which this chapter is the opening, sets out the diagnosis. Part Two states the seven principles on which any successor system must be built if it is to be worthy of the name. Part Three presents the constitutional text — the founding document of a federation that any community on Earth may join — together with the reasoning behind each of its provisions. Part Four describes the architecture in technical detail: how the public ledger works, how the governance layer reaches decisions, how intelligence is placed under human authority, how identity is held by the person rather than the platform, how the economic instruments distribute abundance without destroying initiative, how labor is restored to human purpose, how law is harmonized across jurisdictions without coercion, how enterprises are kept honest without being crushed. Part Five describes the transition: how a civilization moves from the present arrangement to the proposed one, year by year, without civil war and without utopia. Part Six asks what comes after — what becomes possible, on this planet and beyond it, once the operating system is no longer the bottleneck.

This is the program. The reader who finds it audacious is not wrong. The reader who finds it premature is invited to compare the audacity of the proposal to the audacity of continuing on the present course. The cost of building a new system is large. The cost of not building one is larger, and is already being paid, in installments, by people who never signed the bill.

The diagnosis continues in Chapter Two, which examines the failure of trust in detail, and asks what trust is actually made of — and how, structurally, it could be rebuilt.

Chapter Two

The Failure of Trust

Trust, in the structural sense relevant to this argument, is not a feeling. It is a balance sheet. On one side stand the things a population is asked to take on faith: that the vote was counted as cast, that the tax was spent as voted, that the medicine was tested as labeled, that the judge was independent, that the regulator was not bought, that the official number is the actual number. On the other side stand the instruments by which any of these claims can be verified by an ordinary person, without privileged access, without specialized training, in a finite amount of time. When the first side is much larger than the second, the difference is borrowed. We have, in the late industrial democracies, been borrowing for a very long time.

The arithmetic is no longer disputed. In the United States, the share of the public expressing trust in the federal government to do what is right “most of the time or always” stood at roughly seventy-three percent in 1958 and at sixteen percent in 2024 (Pew Research Center 2024). The trajectory in the United Kingdom, France, Italy, and Japan is structurally identical: a slow erosion punctuated by sharp drops around financial crises, surveillance scandals, and pandemics, with no sustained recovery in any of them (OECD 2024). The Edelman Trust Barometer, which surveys twenty-eight countries annually, has for several years described “government” and “media” as distrusted by the median respondent globally, with business and non-governmental organizations slightly above the waterline (Edelman 2024). This is not a national mood. It is a planetary signal.

It is tempting, and politically convenient, to attribute the signal to the disinformation environment. The argument runs: the platforms broke the public sphere, conspiracy displaced consensus, and trust collapsed because attention was hijacked. This is half true and therefore dangerous, because it implies that trust can be restored by better content moderation. It cannot. The disinformation environment is downstream of a more basic fact. Citizens have, on the whole, correctly inferred that they cannot verify the operations of authority, and they have rationally concluded that institutions which cannot be verified should not be trusted. The platforms did not cause this. They simply made the gap audible.

What trust is structurally made of

Begin with a small example. A citizen in any modern democracy pays a tax. The money disappears into a general fund. From the general fund it flows, by appropriations, to ministries; from ministries, by contracts, to suppliers; from suppliers, by subcontracts, to the actual hands that build the road or treat the patient. Each handoff is documented in some register. None of the registers is connected. None is, in any practical sense, queryable by the person who supplied the money. The citizen is told, in aggregate annual statements, what the expenditure was. The citizen has no instrument by which to check.

This is not a complaint about secrecy. The numbers are, in most jurisdictions, public. The complaint is about resolution. Public information at the level of the aggregate is structurally indistinguishable, for the citizen, from no information at all. To verify that a road was built for the amount claimed, the citizen would need: the contract; the subcontracts; the timestamps of payments; the engineering specifications; the inspection reports; the bills of material; the salaries paid; the variances explained. These exist. They are scattered across a dozen agencies, in incompatible formats, behind freedom-of-information procedures whose timeline exceeds the political relevance of the question. The information is technically public and operationally private.

What this produces, over decades, is a public that has learned to act as if the books were closed, because the cost of opening them exceeds the cost of giving up. Once that learning is general, two further things follow. First, the segment of the public most committed to verification — the press, the auditors, the activists, the curious — is itself perceived as partisan, because the act of looking has become unusual enough to look ideological. Second, the segment of the public least committed to verification — the casual majority — becomes susceptible to whatever explanation of opacity is loudest, whether that explanation is corruption, conspiracy, foreign influence, or theological. None of these dynamics requires malice on the part of officials. They follow inevitably from a regime in which the cost of verification exceeds the cost of belief.

The legibility ladder

It is useful to distinguish four rungs on the ladder of public legibility, because policy debates routinely confuse them.

The first rung is disclosure. The state publishes a document. This is the minimum that any modern administration provides, and it satisfies the legal definition of openness while satisfying no practical demand for trust. The second rung is retrieval. A citizen who wishes to inspect a specific decision can find the relevant document without bureaucratic obstruction, in a reasonable time, in a format that does not require specialist software. Few governments are reliably at the second rung. The third rung is structured access. The relevant data is published in machine-readable form, joined across agencies by stable identifiers, queryable through standard interfaces, so that a journalist, an auditor, a student, or an automated agent can answer questions that the publishing agency did not anticipate. Almost no government is at the third rung, except in narrow domains such as company registries, contract awards, or court filings. The fourth rung is continuous verification. The state’s operations are exposed as a live stream of events — every disbursement, every appointment, every regulatory decision, every contract amendment — anchored in tamper-evident records, against which any party can independently reconstruct the state’s books in real time. No state has achieved the fourth rung. It is technically possible, and it is the only rung at which institutional trust becomes self-renewing.

The conventional wisdom holds that the fourth rung is unattainable for reasons of national security, commercial confidentiality, and personal privacy. Each of these reasons is partly correct and substantially overstated. National security demands secrecy over a small fraction of state activity, not over the disbursement of social benefits or the awarding of road contracts. Commercial confidentiality is a legitimate interest of suppliers, but it does not require concealment from the public of what the public paid for what; it requires only that proprietary technical specifications remain protected. Personal privacy is a genuine constraint on raw data, but it is well understood, in modern cryptography, how to publish aggregates and verifiable proofs without exposing the underlying records. The barrier to the fourth rung is not technical. It is institutional reluctance to be that legible.

Why opacity is structurally preferred

An honest analysis must concede that opacity is not an accident. It is preferred by the operators of the system because it preserves discretion. A regulator who is observed in real time loses the latitude to grant a quiet exception. A procurement officer whose every signature is traceable forfeits the flexibility to favor a familiar bidder. A central bank whose deliberations are continuously visible may find its policy options narrowed by anticipation. A minister whose travel schedule is published cannot meet whom she chooses, where she chooses. Some of this discretion is corrupt. Most of it is not. Most of it is the lubricant by which large bureaucracies function — informal favors, tactical ambiguity, plausible deniability, the room to change one’s mind without explaining why. This lubricant is what legibility threatens, and this is why every step up the ladder is resisted with the same arguments — privacy, security, complexity — regardless of which agency is involved.

The historical record shows the resistance can be overcome, and shows how. Court rulings have forced disclosure of police misconduct records (O’Neil 2016). International journalism consortia have forced disclosure of offshore financial structures, exposing the use of jurisdictions whose principal export is concealment (International Consortium of Investigative Journalists 2021). Open-data movements have compelled the publication of public-procurement awards in dozens of countries. In each case, the resistance was strong, the disclosure occurred, and the institutions that adopted higher legibility ended the cycle with greater, not lesser, public confidence. Legibility, once delivered, is rewarded. This is the empirical answer to the official’s perennial worry that more openness will only feed more cynicism.

What the disinformation environment actually does

It is necessary, before proposing a reconstruction, to be precise about the role of the disinformation environment. The platforms did not invent distrust. They industrialized its expression. In the older media regime, a citizen who suspected the books had been cooked could mutter to a neighbor; in the new regime, the same citizen broadcasts to a network of strangers selected by a recommendation engine that rewards the most arresting version of the suspicion. The result is not, as is often claimed, that the public was made paranoid by the platforms. The result is that a pre-existing, accurately grounded suspicion was given a delivery mechanism with vastly higher reach than the official rebuttal. Distrust is now public in a way that, for most of modern history, it was not.

This has two consequences for any program of reconstruction. First, the suppression of disinformation, however well intentioned, will be perceived as the suppression of suspicion as such, and will deepen the very crisis it tries to address. The history of broadcasting law in the late twentieth century is a slow record of this lesson being learned, forgotten, and relearned. Second, the only durable response to a public distrust that has finally found its voice is to give that voice something verifiable to inspect. Trust is restored by exposure, not by reassurance.

The architecture of restored trust

The remainder of this book will return repeatedly to a single architectural commitment, of which trust is the first beneficiary: every operation of public authority must be recorded on a ledger that is readable by any literate citizen, in real time, in a form that can be independently reconstructed. The ledger is the precondition for verification. Verification is the precondition for trust. Trust is the precondition for the consent on which the legitimacy of any system rests (Habermas 1989b).

What the ledger contains, how it is maintained, by whom it is audited, how it preserves privacy where privacy is warranted, and how it deals with information of genuine sensitivity — all of this is the work of Part Four. The present chapter is content to establish the diagnosis. The institutions of the late industrial order are no longer legible to their citizens; their citizens have correctly responded by withdrawing trust; the withdrawal of trust has been mistaken for a cultural defect of the citizens, when it is in fact a structural feature of institutions that were never designed to be inspected at this scale, by this many people, at this speed. The disease is not in the population. It is in the architecture.

A civilization that wishes to be trusted must accept the cost of being seen. That cost is real. Many decisions that were once made comfortably in private will, in a legible system, have to be defended in public. Many alliances that were once tacit will have to be explicit. Many compromises that depended on no one looking too closely will have to find new form. The transition will be uncomfortable for the people in charge. It will be far less uncomfortable than the alternative, which is the slow rotting of the social contract that we are presently watching in slow motion in every measured country in the world.

The diagnosis continues in Chapter Three, which examines the failure of distribution: why a planet that produces enough for everyone delivers enough to fewer than it did a generation ago, and what the structural reasons are.

Chapter Three

The Failure of Distribution

There is, on this planet, enough food. The Food and Agriculture Organization, an agency that has no interest in overstatement, has reported for decades that global agricultural production exceeds human nutritional requirement by a comfortable margin. There is enough housing material, enough fresh water, enough generative capacity in clean energy to keep every household lit, enough productive medicine to cover the world’s curable disease burden several times over, and an unprecedented surplus of information, instruction, and entertainment. The premise of the modern age — that human ingenuity, properly organized, would produce sufficiency — has, by the metric of total output, already been delivered. What has not been delivered is the second half of the promise: that the sufficiency would reach the people for whose sake it was produced.

The shape of the failure is not subtle. The 2024 Global Wealth Report places the bottom half of the adult population in possession of slightly under one percent of global household wealth, and the top one percent in possession of nearly half (UBS 2024). The same report describes the share of the top decile as having grown, with regional variation, in almost every economy that publishes data, across the past four decades. These are not the numbers of a transient business cycle. They are the numbers of a structural arrangement working as designed.

Two clarifications are necessary. First, the proposition that distribution has failed is not the proposition that absolute poverty has failed to decline. It has declined, substantially, particularly in East and South Asia. The World Bank reports that the share of the global population living below the international poverty line of $2.15 per day has fallen from roughly thirty-eight percent in 1990 to under nine percent in the most recent measurements (World Bank 2024). This is one of the few pieces of unambiguously good news of the late twentieth century, and any honest argument must include it. Second, the claim of distributional failure is therefore not that the floor has not risen. It is that the floor has risen far less than the ceiling, that the gap between them is now wider than at any documented point in modern history, and that the gap is widening in directions that no political process now functioning is structurally able to slow (Piketty 2014, 2020).

The two economies inside the same currency

The most useful frame for understanding the late industrial economy is to recognize that it now contains two distinct economies operating under the same legal currency. The first is the economy of labor and goods: people work, firms produce, households consume, the velocity of money is measurable, and the indicators that economics inherited from the nineteenth century continue to behave roughly as the textbooks predict. The second is the economy of asset prices: equities, real estate, fine art, intellectual property, sovereign debt, derivatives, and, most recently, programmable digital instruments. In the second economy, prices are determined less by present labor than by the discounted expectation of future income streams, and the principal driver of those streams is the global supply of liquidity rather than the productivity of the underlying real assets (Stiglitz 2012).

For most of the twentieth century, the two economies were tied together by the institutions of national finance. Wages tracked productivity; productivity tracked output; output tracked the stock of capital in the country that produced it. By the early twenty-first century, the tie has loosened almost to vanishing. Capital is mobile; labor is not. Capital is taxed at rates that compete downward across jurisdictions; labor is taxed at rates that compete upward as fiscal pressure rises. Capital appreciates when central banks expand their balance sheets to manage crises; labor’s claim on consumption is eroded by the same expansion. The two economies share a name, a flag, and a currency. They do not share a fate.

The consequence is a structural decoupling between the macroeconomic indicators that governments report and the lived experience of the median household. National output rises; median wages stagnate. Unemployment falls; housing affordability collapses. Inflation is reported as moderate while the prices most relevant to younger households — rent, education, healthcare, childcare — rise at multiples of the headline rate. The official measurements are not lies. They are simply describing one of the two economies, while the other economy, in which most of the population lives, has detached.

Why old redistributive instruments no longer redistribute

The mid-twentieth-century welfare state was the great success of distributive policy. By the standards of any earlier era, it was an astonishing achievement: universal pensions, public health, mass education, unemployment insurance, progressive income tax, inheritance duty. It rested on a set of preconditions that were specific to its time. The most important of these were that capital was largely national; that the principal form of wealth was the operating firm rather than the financial claim; that the working population was large relative to the dependent population; and that the political authority capable of taxing was coextensive with the geographical area where most economic activity occurred.

Each of those preconditions has weakened. Multinational firms book their profits in jurisdictions chosen for tax minimization rather than for production location, and the largest firms now do this with such efficiency that effective corporate tax rates on the top fifty global firms are, by several reasonable measurements, in low single digits (Saez and Zucman 2019; Tax Justice Network 2023). The principal form of wealth in advanced economies is no longer the operating firm but the financial claim — equity, bond, real estate, intellectual property — and these claims, particularly in their most aggressive forms, are held in legal vehicles whose ownership is structurally obscured (International Consortium of Investigative Journalists 2021). The working-age population has stopped growing in much of the advanced world and has begun to shrink in some of it; the ratio of contributors to claimants in pay-as-you-go pension systems has crossed thresholds that those systems were not designed to survive. And political authority remains national, while the activity it must regulate is planetary.

The honest summary is that the welfare state was an instrument calibrated to a configuration of capital, labor, and territory that no longer obtains, and the calibration cannot be restored by any sequence of national reforms. Reforms can slow the decline. They cannot reverse it. The reforms that would suffice, if undertaken at the planetary level — a global minimum corporate tax with teeth, a global registry of beneficial ownership, a wealth tax on the top fraction of a percent across jurisdictions — exceed what national legislatures, individually, are constituted to deliver (Piketty 2020).

The new sources of unearned income

Three relatively new structural features of the global economy concentrate income upward without requiring corresponding contribution. They are worth naming because they are not visible in the standard categories of nineteenth-century economic thought.

The first is the winner-take-most platform. A digital service whose unit cost approaches zero, whose value to each user rises with the number of other users, and whose underlying network effect protects the incumbent from substitution, is structurally a monopoly. The largest platforms — for search, for retail, for social communication, for cloud computing — are not monopolies because they are corrupt; they are monopolies because the technology of the service produces monopoly as its equilibrium. Antitrust frameworks designed to dissolve corrupt monopolies have proven structurally unable to act on these. The result is a transfer of economic surplus, on a scale measured in trillions, from users and small producers to platform operators, with little corresponding increase in productive employment (Zuboff 2019; UNCTAD 2023).

The second is the intellectual-property estate. The duration and reach of patent and copyright law, originally calibrated to give inventors a finite period of exclusivity sufficient to recoup their investment, have been extended in successive rounds to durations approaching a century, and to subject matters approaching the entire informational substrate of cultural and scientific life. The recipients of these extensions are predominantly not the original inventors and authors, who are dead or have assigned their rights, but the corporate estates that purchased the rights downstream. This is rent collection by another name, and the rent is collected from every consumer of culture, medicine, software, and seed (Mazzucato 2018).

The third is the publicly subsidized private return. A great deal of the technology that powers the most valuable contemporary firms originated in publicly funded research — the internet, satellite positioning, large-scale machine learning, the human genome project, lithium-ion battery chemistry, mRNA platforms. The public bore the risk; the private sector captures the return. There is no inherent injustice in private commercialization of public research, provided the public retains a proportionate claim. In the present arrangement, it does not (Mazzucato 2013).

The political consequence

A population that observes its productivity rising while its share of the product falls is a population that will, in time, withdraw its consent. The withdrawal can take many forms. It can take the form of populism on the right, with its narrative of betrayal by elites and its hostility to immigration as the visible face of a more general dispossession. It can take the form of populism on the left, with its narrative of class war and its demand for confiscatory remedies. It can take the form of withdrawal from the political process altogether, with declining electoral participation, declining union membership, declining civic association (Putnam 2000). It can take, in extreme cases, the form of separatism, of political violence, or of the slow conversion of the formal democracy into something else under the same name.

None of these outcomes is the considered choice of an irrational public. Each is a structurally rational response to an arrangement in which the rules visibly favor those who already have. A serious distributive remedy must therefore do more than transfer money. It must repair the perception, which is also the reality, that the rules themselves are tilted. The chapters of Part Four will propose specific instruments — a public dividend on commonly produced data and natural resource flows, a planetary minimum on corporate income, a beneficial-ownership ledger, a sunset on intellectual-property monopolies that have outlived their justification, and an architecture of public investment that retains a public claim on the upside it finances. Whether the instruments here proposed are exactly the right ones is a question for the argument, not the diagnosis. The diagnosis is settled: the present distribution is the predictable output of the present rules, and the rules require redesign rather than enforcement.

A note on growth

It is sometimes argued that distribution will resolve itself if growth is restored, and that the moral imperative is therefore growth rather than redistribution. The argument has a noble pedigree and a hollow record. The advanced economies have grown, in real terms, throughout the period in which their distributive position has worsened. Growth has not in itself produced distribution. There is no plausible mechanism by which it would, in an arrangement in which the rules of distribution are themselves tilted (Wilkinson and Pickett 2009). Growth remains valuable, particularly in low-income economies, where additional output translates more directly into additional welfare. In high-income economies, the marginal welfare yield of additional growth is now small relative to the welfare yield of repaired distribution and repaired ecological balance (Raworth 2017). To insist on growth as the prior condition for distributive repair is, in 2026, an argument made by people who do not wish the repair to occur.

The diagnosis continues in Chapter Four, which examines the failure of work — what happens to a civilization when the instrument by which dignity has been earned for two centuries no longer reliably produces it.

Chapter Four

The Failure of Work

For roughly two hundred years, the industrial democracies operated on a tacit settlement between citizen and society. The settlement read approximately as follows: in exchange for participation in productive labor, the citizen received income, status, social belonging, the structure of a daily routine, and entry to the institutions of collective bargaining and political voice. The settlement was never universal — it excluded the unpaid, the disabled, the colonized, and large portions of the female population for most of its history — but where it functioned, it produced the most stable and prosperous mass societies in recorded experience. It is now coming apart, on three fronts simultaneously, and the institutions built on the assumption of its persistence are visibly straining.

The first front is the changed character of the employment relation. Across the advanced economies, an expanding share of the working population now earns income through arrangements that resemble traditional employment in their economic effect but not in their legal form: independent contracting, platform work, on-demand assignment, project-based engagement, multi-employer holdings, and informal labor. The proportions vary by country and by sector, but the direction is consistent and durable (International Labour Organization 2024). These arrangements transfer to the worker a category of risk — the risk of finding the next job, the risk of insurance, the risk of training, the risk of pension, the risk of legal protection — that the twentieth-century employment contract had transferred to the firm. The transfer is uncompensated. Workers in these arrangements are not paid more in nominal terms; they are paid the same or less, and required additionally to absorb risks that previously belonged to the firm.

The second front is the structural pressure on middle-skill labor from automation and intelligent systems. The early industrial revolution displaced low-skill manual labor and complemented mid-skill and high-skill work. The automation of the late twentieth century, narrowly construed, did the same. The current wave of automation is structurally different: it complements high-end specialist labor, displaces routinized cognitive labor, and applies pressure to a range of professions that the previous waves left untouched — paralegal work, mid-tier accountancy, customer service, copywriting, junior-level coding, technical translation, basic medical diagnostics, and a growing fraction of the analytic work historically performed by university-trained employees (Brynjolfsson and McAfee 2014; Susskind 2020; Acemoglu and Restrepo 2020).

The third front is the cultural one. A generation has now been raised in proximity to platforms whose central proposition is that visibility, attention, and self-presentation are themselves productive activities — that the line between leisure and labor has dissolved. The same generation has watched its labor be characterized as content, its consumer preferences as data, and its private communication as signal. The relation of self to work, which was already strained by the platform employment economy, has become philosophically unstable. The young, in survey after survey, report a connection to work that is at once more demanding and less remunerative than the connection their parents reported a generation earlier (Graeber 2018; Standing 2011a).

What work was for

To grasp why the unraveling is so consequential, it helps to recall what the industrial settlement actually delivered, beyond income. The job, in the strong twentieth-century sense, performed at least four social functions. It produced a recognized identity, by which a person could be located in the social order. It produced a temporal structure, by which a life was organized into days, weeks, and years. It produced a circle of association, in which a worker met other workers and acquired the habits of cooperation, conflict, and solidarity. And it produced a route to political voice, through unions, professional bodies, and the implicit standing that came from being a contributor to the collective product (Arendt 1958). Income was the most measurable of these functions, and is therefore the one most often discussed, but it was not necessarily the most important. A society that retains the income but loses the other three has not preserved the settlement; it has only preserved its accounting trace.

This is why the conventional response to automation — increase income transfers; the rest will sort itself out — is structurally inadequate. A universal basic income that delivers economic floor without the other functions of work delivers a kind of well-fed loneliness. The political and cultural costs of such an arrangement, observed in early experiments and in regions of high structural unemployment, are not trivial. The replacement of the settlement requires not the replacement of the wage but the replacement of the wage plus the identity, structure, association, and voice that the wage carried with it.

The two productivity paradoxes

Two paradoxes recur in the data and deserve brief notice. The first is that aggregate productivity growth, in most advanced economies, has been surprisingly slow during the period of most aggressive deployment of digital and intelligent systems. The puzzle has been studied for thirty years, and the explanations are partial and contested. The most coherent answers point to the lag between the deployment of a general-purpose technology and the organizational redesign required to capture its productivity gains; to mismeasurement of output in service-heavy economies; and to the concentration of gains within a small number of leading firms whose practices do not diffuse (Brynjolfsson and McAfee 2014). Whatever the resolution, the empirical pattern is clear: the productivity gains promised by automation have not, to date, shown up uniformly in the aggregate, and have shown up unevenly across firms and across workers. Those workers whose tasks are most exposed to automation experience real wage pressure even where the aggregate productivity numbers are flat.

The second paradox is that the labor market in many advanced economies has, in recent years, displayed simultaneous signals of distress and of tightness — high job vacancy rates beside high underemployment, persistent labor shortages in some sectors beside structural unemployment in others. The standard explanation invokes mismatch: the jobs that exist do not match the people available; the people available do not have the skills the jobs require; the geographical and credentialing mobility required to close the gap is more costly than the wage premium of moving. All of these are partial truths. The deeper structural fact is that the labor market is bifurcating into jobs that pay well above subsistence and demand specialized human capital, and jobs that pay below or near subsistence and serve as residual employment for people displaced from middle-skill careers (Piketty 2014). The middle of the historical labor distribution is hollowing.

What is at stake

If the diagnosis stops at “people will need new jobs”, it has missed the magnitude of the change. The capacities of intelligent systems are not, on present evidence, leveling off. The sectors hitherto considered safe — medicine, law, engineering, education, scientific research itself — are being progressively augmented and, in their routine parts, replaced. The argument that “new jobs will appear, as they did after every previous wave” is a hope, not a forecast. The argument has held for previous waves because previous waves automated narrow capacities; the present wave automates general cognition. Its end state is not knowable with confidence. The responsible posture is to plan for an arrangement in which a substantial fraction of the population is, durably, not required by the production process for the form of labor that production used to need.

That arrangement is not without precedent at smaller scale. Ancient societies that achieved agricultural surpluses developed leisured classes whose contribution to society was cultural, philosophical, religious, or ceremonial. Industrial societies developed the institution of formal retirement, in which older citizens are supported in dignity to perform unwaged contributions to family and community. What is unprecedented is the prospect of extending such arrangements to a substantial share of the working-age population, indefinitely, at scale, without the traditional moral discomfort that accompanied non-employment. The discomfort is itself a cultural artifact of a specific industrial period. It is not eternal. It can, with effort, be redesigned.

Three legitimate fears

Any serious proposal in this domain must reckon with three fears that recur in the political conversation and that are not foolish.

The first is that universal income transfers, financed by taxation of capital and automation, will hollow the dignity of those they support. The fear is grounded in real evidence that long-term unemployment, even when adequately compensated, correlates with declines in mental health, civic participation, and family stability. Any successor settlement must therefore couple income with structured opportunity for contribution — community work, public service, education, care, cultural production — and must do so in a way that confers status, not stigma.

The second is that aggressive redistribution will undermine the productive activity that finances it. The fear is grounded in the empirical record of badly designed transfers and badly designed taxes. It is not grounded in the record of well-designed ones. The Nordic economies, Germany, the Netherlands, and several others maintain substantial transfers without measurable harm to long-run productivity. The constraint is design, not direction.

The third is that intelligent systems will not, in the end, displace as much labor as the alarmists predict, and that policy designed for the alarmist case will produce dependency where independence would have been possible. The fear is reasonable insofar as forecasts in this domain have a long history of error. The prudent response is not to wait for certainty, but to design an arrangement that adjusts gracefully to a range of automation outcomes, including the optimistic ones.

The shape of the answer

The architecture proposed in Part Four does not promise the restoration of full employment in the twentieth-century sense. It promises something different: an unconditional floor, a contributive ladder, and a recognized portfolio of activities — paid, unpaid, voluntary, civic, creative — that together constitute a life of acknowledged worth in a society that no longer requires every adult to spend forty hours a week producing marketable output. The instruments include a planetary basic income financed by taxation of automated production and natural resource flows; an open-access continuous learning entitlement; a recognized status for unpaid care, ecological stewardship, civic service, and cultural production; and a refactored taxation of capital that distinguishes productive investment from rent extraction.

The full design is for later. The diagnostic point is the one the chapter has now made. The institution of work, as the industrial democracies inherited it, was a particular configuration of three things — productive contribution, identity, and income — bound together by the structure of the firm and the regulatory framework around it. The configuration is unbinding. The income can, with effort, be replaced. The identity and the framework of contribution have to be redesigned, deliberately, before they are reconstituted by accident, in forms — gig economies, attention markets, parasocial labor — that few people would have chosen had the choice been made openly.

The diagnosis continues in Chapter Five, which examines the failure of law: why national legal systems built for national populations cannot govern the planetary phenomena that now dominate every meaningful policy question.

Chapter Five

The Failure of Law

Law, in the sense the modern era inherited it, is a sovereign instrument: a body of rules issued by a political authority, applied to a population located within a defined territory, enforced by officers who answer to that authority, interpreted by courts whose jurisdiction is fixed by the same authority. The whole apparatus presumes that the activity it regulates fits inside the territory in which the authority operates. For most of human history, that presumption was approximately true. A market was local, a contract was performed locally, a fraud was committed locally, a violence was inflicted locally, a corporation was a local body, a bank was a local institution. Law, like the activity it governed, was a national thing because everything it cared about was a national thing.

That presumption is no longer reliable, and the consequence is the slow conversion of large categories of consequential human activity into a domain that is, in operational terms, ungoverned. The activity has not vanished into anarchy. It has migrated into the seams between jurisdictions, where the rules of two or three or twelve sovereigns simultaneously apply, contradict, and fail to enforce. To call this domain “international law” is to use a term whose principal meaning, in 2026, is the absence of effective regulation. To call it “global governance” is to invoke a structure that does not, in any robust sense, exist.

The categories of escape

The escape from national law is most visible in five domains, and the diagnosis is best made through them in turn.

The first is capital. A multinational firm is not, in any meaningful operational sense, a single firm. It is a portfolio of legal entities, incorporated in jurisdictions chosen for tax, regulatory, and disclosure characteristics; tied together by inter-company contracts that allocate cost and revenue across borders; and managed by a leadership that has no special obligation to the population of any specific jurisdiction. The legal techniques are well documented. They include transfer pricing of internal services and intellectual-property licenses; the use of holding companies in low-tax jurisdictions; the routing of royalties through tax-haven subsidiaries; the strategic allocation of debt to high-tax jurisdictions to deduct interest from taxable income; and, increasingly, the use of jurisdictions whose principal export is corporate secrecy (Saez and Zucman 2019; International Consortium of Investigative Journalists 2021; Tax Justice Network 2023). The result is that a substantial fraction of the largest firms pay corporate tax at effective rates in low single digits, while the citizens of the countries in which they sell pay much higher rates on labor income that is, in all practical senses, captive.

The second is data. Personal data is generated by individuals located in one country, processed on servers located in a second, controlled by a corporation incorporated in a third, governed by a contract whose enforcement clause names a court in a fourth. When a privacy violation occurs, the question of which law applies is not merely contested; it is structurally underdetermined. The European Union’s General Data Protection Regulation is the most ambitious attempt yet to project national-style law onto a planetary substrate, and even it operates by the indirect mechanism of penalizing entities that wish to do business with European users (European Parliament and Council 2016). The reach is real, but the framework is patchwork; in jurisdictions with weaker protection or active state surveillance, the same data flows under different rules, and the individual has no instrument to know which apply (Zuboff 2019).

The third is information warfare and cybercrime. A criminal who interrupts the electricity, water, or hospital network of a country is not necessarily a citizen of that country, does not necessarily reside there, and may operate from a jurisdiction whose authorities are unwilling, unable, or politically disinclined to cooperate. Treaties governing extradition exist; they apply in narrow circumstances and frequently fail in the most consequential cases. State-sponsored cyber operations occupy a category that international law does not yet recognize as a discrete instrument of war. There is no equivalent of the Geneva Conventions for code (Perlroth 2021). The result is that critical infrastructure is, in 2026, attacked routinely, with attribution typically delayed by months, and prosecution typically impossible.

The fourth is environment. The atmosphere, the oceans, the migratory species, and the climate system are common-pool resources of the planet. Their use is regulated, where at all, by the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change and its successor instruments, most notably the Paris Agreement (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change 2015). The architecture of the Agreement is itself an admission of the problem: targets are nationally determined and largely unenforceable, monitoring is voluntary, sanctions are political. The atmosphere is the most consequential commons humanity shares; the legal instrument by which it is protected is, by design, the weakest treaty regime in existence. Compliance has been correspondingly partial (IPCC 2023, 2022).

The fifth is arms. The Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons depends on the voluntary discipline of states that have always retained the technical capacity to break it (United Nations 1968). Conventions on chemical and biological weapons are stronger in form than in enforcement. The proliferation of armed unmanned systems, of long-range precision strike, of autonomous targeting, and of cyber-enabled kinetic operations has occurred largely outside any treaty framework calibrated to the technologies involved (SIPRI 2024). The principal instrument of restraint has been the threat of mutual retaliation, which is a form of equilibrium rather than a form of law.

Why national law cannot, on its own, fix this

The temptation, in each of the five domains, is to argue that the problem is one of insufficient national resolve, and that a sufficiently determined national government could close each gap by extending its own law extraterritorially. The argument has been tried, and its limits are now visible.

Extraterritorial enforcement works only where the enforcing state has economic or military leverage over the target, and only in proportion to that leverage. The United States has, by the size of its market and the role of its currency, succeeded in extending some of its laws — sanctions, securities, anti-bribery, certain aspects of tax — to actors with no other connection to its territory. The European Union has done the same with privacy and competition law. No other jurisdiction has comparable reach, and the reach of the two that do is itself contested by every other state and by a growing fraction of the firms it touches. Extraterritorial law, when it works, works as power; it does not work as law in the proper sense, which requires consent of the governed.

A second temptation is to argue that the gap will be closed, eventually, by treaty. Treaties take decades to negotiate, frequently exclude the actors most relevant to the regulated activity, are interpreted by domestic courts with diverging methods, and are abandoned by signatories whose governments change. The history of the past sixty years includes several attempts at planetary regulation by treaty — for the seas, for the atmosphere, for the seabed, for outer space, for chemical weapons — with results that range from partial to ornamental. Treaties remain useful instruments. They are, by themselves, structurally insufficient to the speed and scope of the activity they would have to govern.

What law actually requires to function

It is worth restating, plainly, what conditions any system of law must satisfy to perform its work. The conditions are old; they are not, in 2026, controversial.

The first is jurisdiction: there must exist an authority whose writ runs over the activity to be governed. The second is publication: the rules must be knowable in advance by those they bind. The third is generality: the rules must apply uniformly to similarly situated subjects. The fourth is impartiality: the body that adjudicates must be capable of ruling against any party, including the authority that issued the rules. The fifth is enforcement: there must exist an instrument by which the rule, once decided, is given effect against parties who would prefer to ignore it. The sixth is review: there must be procedures by which a wrongful decision can be corrected. The seventh, derivable from the others, is legitimacy: the population subject to the law must, on the whole, accept its authority — not because they like every rule, but because the procedure by which the rules are made and enforced is one in which they have, in principle, voice (Rawls 1971a; Habermas 1989b).

The international order does not satisfy these conditions in the domains that now matter most. There is no body whose jurisdiction is universally recognized over capital flows, data flows, the global atmosphere, planetary cybersecurity, or the use of advanced weapons. There are bodies that approximate fragments of these things — the World Trade Organization, the International Court of Justice, the International Criminal Court, the Financial Action Task Force, the International Atomic Energy Agency, the conferences of the parties to the various climate and chemical weapons conventions (United Nations Diplomatic Conference 1998) — and each operates within a constrained mandate, with constrained membership, under constrained enforcement. None has the standing of a court of universal jurisdiction. None has the budget of a major national administration. None can compel.

The legal void as a habitat

What lives in the void? The honest answer is that organized crime lives there; that the most aggressive forms of capital live there; that the most coercive surveillance practices live there; that the cyberweapons industry lives there; that human trafficking, illicit finance, sanctions evasion, and the more sophisticated forms of disinformation operations live there. The void is not empty. It is a productive habitat for activities that find national law inconvenient.

The political consequence is severe. A citizen who watches the legitimate authorities of his country fail, year after year, to address activities that visibly damage him — fraud he cannot prosecute, pollution he cannot prevent, capital flight he cannot stop, foreign cyber attacks he cannot punish — will, in time, conclude that those authorities are either complicit or incapable. Both conclusions, sustained, undermine the consent on which legitimate government rests. The crisis of legitimacy that animates much of contemporary politics is not unconnected to the legal void; it is, in substantial part, downstream of it.

What a successor system must offer

A serious successor system must accept that some domains of human activity have escaped the national level and will not, on any realistic horizon, be coaxed back. For those domains, it must provide a planetary tier of law: a tier that is genuinely public in its standing, genuinely universal in its membership, genuinely reviewable in its procedures, and — most controversially — genuinely capable of enforcement. The instruments by which such a tier can be constructed without becoming the very tyranny that national sovereignty was originally devised to prevent are the principal subject of Parts Three and Four. The architecture proposed there relies on three commitments: subsidiarity, so that the planetary tier is restricted to what truly cannot be done at lower levels; transparency, so that the tier itself can always be inspected; and reversibility, so that decisions made at the planetary level can be revisited as circumstance changes.

The diagnostic point of the present chapter is narrow and sufficient. The legal architecture inherited from the industrial era cannot govern the activity that now exists. The choice is not between national law and global law. The choice is between an unfilled void, in which the activities that escape national law operate by their own logic, and a constructed planetary tier, in which those activities are brought into the discipline of public rules. The first choice is the present trajectory. Its outcomes are visible. The second is the work the next chapters undertake.

The diagnosis closes in Chapter Six, which examines the failure of foresight: why a civilization with the technological reach of an adult species makes its decisions on the planning horizon of an adolescent.

Chapter Six

The Failure of Foresight

A civilization can be diagnosed, with some accuracy, by the time horizon over which its decisions are made. Hunter-gatherer bands operated on a horizon of seasons. Agrarian societies operated on a horizon of harvests, which is to say of years. Empires that built lasting infrastructure operated on a horizon of generations; the Roman aqueducts, the Chinese canals, the medieval cathedrals took longer to build than any single elite could expect to oversee, and the institutions that built them accepted this as a cost of doing serious work. Modern industrial democracies, despite the unprecedented technological reach of the populations they govern, operate on a horizon of months: the electoral cycle for politicians, the fiscal quarter for firms, the news cycle for the public mind. The mismatch between the scale of the consequences our actions produce and the scale of the horizon over which we deliberate is, by any measure, the most distinctive structural pathology of the late industrial age.

The mismatch is not subtle. The greenhouse gas emissions of any given year alter atmospheric chemistry on a timescale measured in centuries, with consequences that compound for thousands of years (IPCC 2023). The ecological loads imposed on soils, aquifers, fisheries, and forests are repaid, where at all, on timescales measured in decades. Pension obligations are written today against demographic projections that resolve thirty and forty years hence (International Monetary Fund 2024). The half-life of long-lived radioactive waste from civilian nuclear programs is measured in tens of thousands of years, far longer than any continuous political institution that has ever existed. The cumulative cost of the carbon emitted in any one year of advanced-economy growth, calculated honestly against the climate damage it imposes, exceeds — by most estimates — the income gains of that growth (Stern 2006). The decisions are made on quarterly accounting; their consequences keep accruing on geological accounting. There is no political or economic instrument, in routine use, that translates between the two.

Why the present is over-represented

Several mechanisms produce the overweighting of the present in ordinary politics. They are worth listing because each must be addressed in any successor design.

The first is the electoral cycle. A government chosen on a four- or five-year horizon optimizes, almost without exception, for outcomes whose visibility falls within that horizon. Long-horizon projects — infrastructure, education, scientific research, ecological restoration — yield benefits whose attribution is unclear by the time they ripen, and yield costs that are entirely visible in the present. A rational politician concerned with reelection therefore underinvests in such projects relative to what the population’s own intertemporal preference would, on reflection, support. The phenomenon is not corruption; it is the structurally rational response to the institutional design (Fukuyama 2014).

The second is the fiscal-quarter cycle. The publicly traded firm, on which the contemporary economy depends for its allocation of capital, is required to report quarterly to its shareholders. Compensation of senior management is keyed, with rare exceptions, to indices whose horizon is a fiscal year or two. Investments whose payoff is more than five years out are systematically penalized in the share price, and managers who pursue them are systematically displaced. The result is the familiar pattern in which firms with vast retained earnings pursue stock buybacks rather than capital investment, even where the underlying productive opportunity is real (Mazzucato 2018).

The third is the news cycle. Public attention is allocated by media organizations whose competitive position depends on the freshness, salience, and emotional charge of the information they distribute. Long-horizon developments, by their nature, are slow, undramatic, and distributed across many actors; they cannot compete for attention against acute events. The public therefore receives a continuous stream of immediate stimulus interleaved with occasional, and quickly forgotten, references to the long horizon. The cumulative effect is that the long horizon is structurally underrepresented in the conversation from which political will is formed (Putnam 2000).

The fourth is the economic discount rate. Standard economic appraisal applies a positive discount rate to future costs and benefits, on the rationale that consumption later is worth less than consumption now. The principle is reasonable for short horizons; it becomes morally and practically dubious when applied to consequences that fall on populations not yet born and that did not consent to the discount. The choice of discount rate is itself a political decision dressed as a technical one, and the rates routinely chosen — three percent, five percent, seven percent — render any cost more than fifty years out essentially invisible to the calculation (Stern 2006).

Each of these mechanisms is independent. Each is reinforcing. Together they produce a political economy in which the long horizon is, with rare exceptions, neglected by every actor whose incentives the system has bothered to consider, and represented only by those whose incentives the system has not yet learned to value: scientists, archivists, teachers, farmers planting trees they will not live to harvest, parents thinking about their grandchildren.

The structural neglect of catastrophe

The most serious consequence of foreshortened deliberation is the structural neglect of low-probability, high-consequence events — the events that, integrated over time, dominate the expected loss profile of a civilization. The same logic that underweights the slow accumulation of climate damage underweights the small probability of pandemic, of major financial collapse, of nuclear use, of large-scale infrastructure failure, of misalignment in advanced artificial systems. Each of these events, in any given year, is unlikely. Each, integrated over a half-century, is well within the range of plausible outcomes. The expected damage from any one of them, properly accounted, exceeds the cost of the preparatory measures by orders of magnitude. The preparatory measures are nevertheless underfunded in nearly every advanced country, because their funding requires payment now against a benefit that, in any given year, will probably not be needed (World Economic Forum 2024).

The pandemic of the early 2020s is the clearest recent illustration. The capacity of national health systems to absorb a respiratory pandemic had been studied for decades, with broadly consistent conclusions that capacity was insufficient. Stockpiles of personal protective equipment, the contingency manufacturing of vaccines, the design of surveillance networks for emerging pathogens, and the legal frameworks for rapid public-health response had each been the subject of serious official report. The reports were filed; the recommendations were not, in most countries, implemented at the necessary scale. The cost of implementation would have been a small fraction of the cost of the pandemic when it arrived. The institutions that issued the warnings had no mechanism to compel action against a contingent harm. The institutions that allocated budgets had no mechanism to weigh contingent harm against present priorities.

The same pattern applies, with adjustment of detail, to climate, to financial stability, to the security of large concentrations of fissile material, and to the governance of advanced computational systems. Each is a low-frequency, high-consequence risk; each is structurally underweighted by institutions whose incentive structures cannot see past the electoral cycle (Bostrom 2014). The pattern is not anyone’s fault, in the narrow sense. It is the architecture’s fault.

What foresight institutions look like, and why we lack them

It is sometimes assumed that long-horizon thinking requires only the will to engage in it, and that the absence of such thinking is therefore a failure of character. This is mistaken. Long-horizon thinking, where it has functioned in human history, has functioned because specific institutions were designed to perform it, with budgets, mandates, and standing equal to the institutions of short-horizon decision. The medieval cathedral chapters, the royal forestry commissions of the early modern European states, the standing scientific academies of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, the public health corps of the late nineteenth, and the strategic planning bodies of the post-war reconstruction governments — these were institutions deliberately constructed to operate on horizons their political masters could not match. Most of them have been weakened, defunded, politicized, or dissolved during the past four decades, in the name of efficiency, agility, or fiscal restraint. The cumulative effect is a public sector that has lost, sector by sector, the institutional memory and authority by which long-horizon work is done (Mazzucato 2013).

A successor system must rebuild this lost capacity. The instruments are well known and have been proposed, in various forms, by writers from Burke to Aldo Leopold to Jonas to Elinor Ostrom: standing intergenerational councils with formal voice in legislation; office of the future commissioner with subpoena power over agencies’ long-horizon policies; constitutional clauses recognizing duties to populations not yet born; budgetary categories ring-fenced from short-term political reallocation; courts whose mandate explicitly includes the standing of future persons. Several of these have been implemented at small scale — Wales has a Future Generations Commissioner; Finland has a Committee for the Future in its parliament; the German Federal Constitutional Court has read intergenerational obligation into its constitutional doctrine on climate. None has been implemented at planetary scale, with planetary mandate, on the questions that demand it. That is the architectural gap.

The deepest version of the failure

Beneath the institutional analysis lies a philosophical fact that is rarely named. The political and economic frameworks of the modern age were constructed at a moment when human intervention in nature was small enough that the planet could be modeled, for most decision purposes, as an unchanging backdrop against which finite human projects played out. That model is now false. Human action has become a planetary geological force. The atmospheric chemistry, the biological diversity, the hydrological cycles, the carbon and nitrogen flows, and even the biogeochemistry of the oceans now bear the visible signature of human industry. Geologists discuss whether to formalize this period as a distinct epoch under the name Anthropocene; the political institutions of the period have not yet absorbed what such a designation would mean (IPCC 2023; Raworth 2017).

What it means is that human collective action has acquired effects on a timescale on which no human institution has ever before been required to operate. To exercise a power without an institution adequate to it is to exercise it irresponsibly. We are, in this sense, the first species to acquire planetary instruments without acquiring planetary institutions. The asymmetry will not be sustainable. Either the institutions are built, or the instruments are misused, or, in time, both.

Closing the diagnosis

The five chapters of Part One have set out the diagnosis. The institutions of the modern age are structurally illegible to their citizens; they distribute the product of an unprecedentedly capable economy in ways that no informed population would, on reflection, endorse; they have lost the link between work and dignity on which two centuries of social peace depended; they cannot regulate the activity that now most consequentially affects them, because that activity has migrated beyond the reach of any single state; and they make their decisions on horizons grossly inadequate to the consequences of the decisions they make. None of this is the fault of the inheritors. It is the cost of inheriting.
