Diagnosis without proposal is, however, a counsel of despair. The remainder of this book is the proposal. Part Two lays the principles. Part Three sets the constitutional text. Part Four describes the architecture. Part Five describes the transition. Part Six asks what becomes possible after.

Part One closes here. Part Two opens with the seven principles on which any successor arrangement worth the name must be constructed.

Part Two

The Seven Principles

Before architecture, principle. The seven that follow are the constraints any successor arrangement must satisfy if it is to deserve the name of a civilizational design rather than a clever variant of the present trouble.

Chapter Seven

Principle One — Legibility

The first principle of any successor arrangement is that every operation of public authority must be legible, in real time, to any literate citizen who chooses to inspect it. This is the principle from which all others follow. A society that cannot be read cannot be trusted; a society that cannot be trusted cannot consent; a society that cannot consent is held together by something other than its own will, which is to say it is held together by force, by inertia, or by the tactical persuasion of the moment. Legibility is not a luxury of mature democracy. It is the precondition for the legitimate exercise of any public power that wishes to call itself such.

The principle is older than the modern state. It descends, in different idioms, from the publication of the Twelve Tables in the Roman Republic, from the medieval insistence that no person could be bound by a law not first promulgated, from the Enlightenment demand that the laws of finance be opened to the publication of the budget, and from the twentieth-century institutionalization of the freedom-of-information statute. Each generation has extended the surface area over which the principle is enforced, and each extension has been resisted by the same arguments — security, complexity, privacy, expense — and has been justified, after the fact, by the same outcomes — improved governance, deeper consent, less corruption, fewer foolish wars. The history is unanimous. Where legibility advances, legitimacy follows. Where it is resisted, legitimacy decays.

What legibility means in 2026

To say that authority must be legible is not to say that every piece of paper produced by every official must be published. The principle has structure, and the structure must be specified or it dissolves into rhetoric.

Legibility, in the sense intended here, has four operative components. First, every act of public authority that produces a binding consequence — a regulation, an appropriation, an appointment, a contract award, an enforcement action, a permit, a court ruling, a treaty signature — must produce a corresponding entry on a public ledger maintained for that purpose. Second, the entry must be timestamped, attributed, and cryptographically signed by the authority responsible, in such a way that subsequent alteration without re-signature is detectable. Third, the entry must be queryable through standard interfaces, so that any party — journalist, auditor, civic organization, automated agent — can reconstruct the activity of the authority across any chosen dimension: by recipient, by region, by program, by time. Fourth, the meaning of each entry must be supplied alongside it: not the document alone, but the schema by which the document is to be read.

The four components together describe what we will call, in this book, the public ledger. The technical means of constructing such a ledger are well understood and are described in Chapter Sixteen. The novelty of the proposal is not technical. It is institutional. Public authorities have, with rare exception, never been required to be legible at this resolution. Every such authority will resist the requirement. The resistance will be sincere. It will also, as the historical record shows, be unjustified at scale.

What legibility is not

Three confusions recur in the public discussion of legibility, and the principle is best stated by ruling them out.

Legibility is not surveillance. The object of legibility is the operation of authority, not the conduct of citizens. The state’s actions are visible; the citizen’s data is not. The asymmetry is deliberate, and is the precise inversion of the asymmetry that prevails in the surveillance societies of the early twenty-first century, in which citizens are extensively visible to authorities and authorities are extensively opaque to citizens (Zuboff 2019). A society that has confused the direction of the asymmetry has not lost legibility — it has lost privacy, which is the citizen’s mirror right and is the subject of Chapter Nineteen. The two principles are not in conflict; they are complementary. Authority is exposed; the person is protected.

Legibility is not absolute. There exist domains of state action — narrow but real — in which immediate publication would defeat the lawful purpose of the action: an active criminal investigation, a genuine national-security operation, the negotiation of a treaty whose disclosure would foreclose the negotiation, the protection of a witness whose identification would endanger the witness’s life. In such domains, the principle of legibility takes a delayed form. The act is recorded on the ledger contemporaneously, encrypted under keys held by an oversight authority; the encrypted record’s existence is public, with metadata sufficient to identify its category and its expected disclosure date; and the act is opened for full inspection within a defined period, typically not exceeding seven years, with extensions only on the published certification of a designated review body. The point is not that secrets do not exist. The point is that permanent secrets do not exist, and that the public has a contemporaneous right to know that an act has occurred even if it cannot, for a defined period, know the act’s content.

Legibility is not the same as participation. A legibly governed society is not necessarily a directly governed one. The principle says nothing, by itself, about whether decisions are made by representatives, by referenda, by deliberative assemblies, by experts, or by some combination. It says only that the decisions, however made, must be open to inspection. This is important because the principle of legibility can be implemented across a wide range of governance forms, and is therefore compatible with the constitutional pluralism that the federation proposed in Part Three explicitly preserves.

Why the principle is hard

The principle is conceptually simple and operationally difficult, for reasons worth naming honestly.

The first difficulty is the loss of discretion. A public official whose actions are recorded on a public ledger forfeits the latitude to grant a quiet exception, to soften a judgment with informal accommodation, to negotiate by ambiguity. Some of this latitude has been corrupt. Most of it has been the lubricant by which large bureaucracies handle hard cases. Removing it requires building, in its place, a procedural mechanism by which exceptions can be granted formally, on the record, with explicit justification. This is not impossible. It is unfamiliar.

The second difficulty is the loss of tactical position. Authorities who can be observed continuously cannot bluff. A diplomat cannot threaten an option her government has not authorized; a regulator cannot signal an enforcement direction she has not committed to; a central bank cannot, in some circumstances, talk the markets in the directions it wishes them to take. The loss is real. It is also, if one looks honestly at the record, often a loss of dignity rather than a loss of capacity. Authorities have, in the era of opacity, frequently used tactical position to impose costs on their own publics, and the publics — when they later learn what was done in their name — have, with few exceptions, regretted having paid for the bluff.

The third difficulty is the loss of convenience. A government that publishes its operations in real time must have processes capable of producing the published record without imposing intolerable burden on the people doing the work. This is, in the end, an engineering problem, and it is solvable; but the engineering problem has not, in most jurisdictions, been undertaken at scale. Public-sector information infrastructures remain, in 2026, decades behind the private sector for most categories of work. Closing the gap requires investment, and investment requires that the principle first be accepted.

The historical evidence

It is sometimes argued that the principle, however attractive in theory, has never been tested at the scale demanded by a modern administration. The argument understates the historical record. Several jurisdictions have implemented partial legibility regimes whose results are instructive.

Estonia has, since the early 2000s, operated a digital state in which most government services are delivered through a single citizen interface, with detailed audit logs accessible to the citizen herself. The model is partial in scope but deep in implementation, and it has produced public-confidence levels in digital government that exceed those of most jurisdictions of comparable size. The early Open Government Partnership, joined by seventy-five countries between 2011 and 2024, committed signatories to publish progressively granular data on procurement, budgets, and beneficial ownership; compliance has been uneven, but the countries that have implemented their commitments most fully have, in measured terms, improved on perceived corruption (Transparency International 2024). The European Union’s Open Data Directive obliges member states to make administrative data available in machine-readable form. In the United States, the Federal Funding Accountability and Transparency Act has, since 2006, mandated the publication of federal expenditure data through a public portal; the portal’s quality has been criticized, but the principle of citizen access to federal expenditure has, since its introduction, been politically irreversible.

None of these regimes implements the full principle of legibility as stated above. Each has demonstrated that the technical and institutional obstacles to greater legibility are smaller than the political obstacles, and that the political obstacles erode steadily once the regime is in place.

How the principle disciplines later design

It is worth, at the close of the chapter, noting what the principle of legibility forbids in the design of subsequent institutions.

It forbids any decision rule whose output cannot be reconstructed by an external observer with access to the rule and the input. This rules out, in particular, the use of opaque machine learning systems for binding administrative decisions affecting individuals — a constraint that the European Union has begun to codify in its Artificial Intelligence Act, with respect to high-risk applications (European Parliament and Council 2024). It rules out the delegation of consequential public functions to private intermediaries whose internal decision processes are protected as trade secret. It rules out the use of cryptographic instruments in public administration that prevent later inspection by oversight bodies, however convenient those instruments may be for short-term execution. It rules out structures of plausible deniability — committees without minutes, decisions without authors, contracts without counterparties identifiable by the public.

It does not forbid expertise, technical complexity, or specialist procedure. A regulation written by hydrologists for the management of an aquifer is no less legible because most citizens cannot evaluate it; the legibility test is satisfied if any sufficiently trained party can, on inspecting the public record, reconstruct the reasoning. The point is not that every citizen will personally inspect every act. The point is that any citizen, or any party acting on the citizen’s behalf, can inspect any act, with no procedural obstacle that a determined and patient inquiry cannot overcome.

Closing

The principle of legibility, properly stated, is at once unremarkable and revolutionary. Unremarkable, because the obligation of public authority to be visible to the public has been the formal commitment of every legitimate political tradition since the Roman Republic. Revolutionary, because the technical conditions for honoring the commitment at planetary scale, in real time, against authorities of unprecedented size and reach, have only recently come into existence, and the institutions that wield public authority have not yet been compelled to use them. The remainder of this book is, in one of its readings, a sustained argument that they must.

Chapter Eight states the second principle: subsidiarity, the rule that decisions belong at the lowest level capable of making them.

Chapter Eight

Principle Two — Subsidiarity

Subsidiarity is the rule that a decision belongs at the lowest level capable of making it well, and that higher levels of authority intervene only when the lower level has demonstrably failed or when the matter to be decided exceeds the lower level’s competence. The principle is older than its name. It runs through the constitutional traditions of federal states, through Catholic social teaching, through the structuring documents of the European Union, and, in less formalized versions, through every political community that has ever delegated power upward without dissolving the delegating body. It is, in the present argument, the indispensable counterweight to the first principle. Legibility makes authority visible; subsidiarity disperses it. Without dispersion, legibility would only document a tyranny.

The principle has two motivations. The first is epistemic. Decisions are best made by those nearest the facts that bear on them, because those nearest the facts have access to information — local knowledge, social context, particular circumstance — that does not survive transmission upward. This was the central insight of Hayek’s argument against centralized planning (Hayek 1944), and it survives the political quarrels that have surrounded the argument since. It survives, in particular, the failures of unrestrained markets to which Hayek himself paid insufficient attention; nothing in the principle of subsidiarity requires the further claim that lower levels be left wholly to themselves. The principle says only that the burden of proof, in any given matter, falls on the proposal to elevate.

The second motivation is moral. Authority delegated upward without need is authority alienated from those it governs. A community whose decisions are made by remote bodies, however technically competent, develops over time the habits of dependence and the resentments of the dispossessed. The political pathologies of the present century, in nearly every advanced economy, include some version of the complaint that decisions are made by people the public does not know, in places the public will never visit, on grounds the public cannot inspect. The complaint is overstated by the populists who deploy it. It is not, in its core, mistaken.

Levels and their proper scope

The successor architecture proposed in this book contemplates five levels of public authority, each with a defined scope and a defined relation to the others. They are the local commune, the regional canton, the state, the continental confederation, and the planetary tier. None of these names is sacred; the level designations are functional. What matters is the assignment of authority among them.

The local commune — typically the city, the village, the urban district, the rural parish — is the level at which most ordinary public services are administered. Land use, primary education, primary health, local infrastructure, neighborhood policing, social services, cultural programming, local taxation: these are the routine business of the commune, and the principle of subsidiarity holds that they should not be lifted from it without specific cause. Communes are also, of necessity, the locus of greatest variation. What is sound policy in a commune of a thousand farmers is unsound policy in a commune of two hundred thousand urban workers. The framework allows, and indeed requires, that variation; uniformity at this level is a defect, not a virtue.

The regional canton is the level at which infrastructure of regional scale is coordinated — water systems that span multiple communes, road networks, regional hospitals, secondary education, regional environmental protection — and at which judicial appeals from the commune level are heard. The canton is, in many federal systems, the natural unit of cultural and linguistic identity, and it is at this level that questions of cultural autonomy, official language, and historical heritage are most coherently decided.

The state level retains, in this architecture, considerable scope. National defense, the issuance of currency where states retain currency sovereignty, citizenship and immigration policy in its primary form, taxation of national activity, the architecture of social insurance, the constitution of higher education, and the setting of professional and technical standards remain state matters. The principle of subsidiarity does not abolish the state; it constrains the conditions under which the state may pre-empt the regions and the communes within it.

The continental confederation — examples in the present world include the European Union, the African Union in its more developed sectors, certain organs of ASEAN, and the regional integrations of the Americas — is the level at which a group of states coordinates on matters whose scale exceeds any one of them but which do not require planetary deliberation. Single-market regulation, cross-border infrastructure, the free movement of persons and goods, common environmental standards over shared ecosystems, and harmonized commercial and contract law are the proper scope of the confederation.

The planetary tier, finally, is reserved for matters whose scope is the species itself. The atmospheric and oceanic commons, the regulation of weapons whose use threatens humanity at large, the governance of advanced computational systems above defined thresholds of capability, the prevention and management of pandemics, the management of orbital and lunar resources, the basic minimum of human dignity from which no jurisdiction may derogate: these and a small list of comparable matters constitute the planetary tier. The list is, by design, short. The default in the architecture is that the planetary tier is silent; it speaks only on the matters explicitly assigned to it.

The conditions for elevation

Subsidiarity, properly understood, is not a bias against higher levels of authority. It is a discipline on the conditions under which authority is moved up. Three conditions are necessary, in the architecture proposed here, for any matter to be elevated from one level to the next.

The first is scale incompetence. The lower level must be unable, by virtue of the matter’s scope, to handle it. A commune cannot, by itself, regulate the atmospheric chemistry of the planet. A state cannot, by itself, prevent capital from leaving its jurisdiction. A continental confederation cannot, by itself, manage a species-wide pandemic. In each case, the elevation is justified by the structural limit of the lower level.

The second is spillover. The action of the lower level must produce consequences that fall on populations beyond its boundary, in ways that the lower level cannot, by itself, internalize. A commune that pollutes a river imposes costs downstream; a state that emits greenhouse gases imposes costs across the planet. The higher level’s authority extends only as far as the spillover requires.

The third is floor protection. The matter must touch a minimum of human dignity that is held to be inviolable across the federation, and that the lower level has either failed to protect or actively threatens. The protection of the basic rights of persons — life, freedom from torture, basic subsistence, freedom of conscience, equality before the law — is the most uncontroversial example, and is the basis on which the planetary tier retains a residual jurisdiction over individual rights even within sovereign states.

Where none of the three conditions is satisfied, the matter remains at the lower level. Where one is partially satisfied, the matter is shared by formal cooperation, not by elevation. Only where the conditions are clearly met does authority move upward. This is what subsidiarity, as a constitutional principle, actually requires.

The complementary principle of devolution

Subsidiarity has a downward sibling that is rarely articulated and that the present architecture explicitly endorses. Where a matter has been elevated to a higher level on the basis of scale, spillover, or floor protection, and the conditions that justified the elevation cease to apply, the matter must be returned to the lower level. Authority once moved up does not, on this principle, ratchet permanently. The federation’s higher organs are constructed with sunset clauses on their substantive jurisdictions, requiring periodic renewal by the constituents whose authority was delegated. The renewal is the moment at which the conditions of elevation are reviewed and the delegation either continued or withdrawn.

This is a stronger commitment than is found in any major existing federation. It is intentional. The historical experience of federations — including those whose framers carefully distinguished delegated from reserved powers — is that delegations grow. The Tenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, the Article 5 Treaty of the European Union, the federal allocation of powers in the German Basic Law: each was designed to protect the lower level, and each has been, in practice, gradually superseded as the higher level has accumulated competence by interpretation, treaty, or political momentum (Fukuyama 2014). A federation honest about subsidiarity must build the resistance to this drift into its constitution, not as an exhortation, but as a procedural requirement.

What subsidiarity is not

Three confusions deserve correction.

Subsidiarity is not localism for its own sake. The principle does not hold that local decisions are better than non-local ones; it holds that decisions belong at the lowest level capable of making them well. Where the local level is incapable, by reason of scale or competence, the principle requires elevation, not deference. A commune that cannot, by reason of size, support a tertiary hospital is not entitled by subsidiarity to refuse the canton’s role in providing one.

Subsidiarity is not a defense of local oppression. The principle of floor protection trumps local autonomy where minimum rights are concerned. A commune that practices apartheid, that denies education to women, that tolerates trafficking in persons, or that withdraws from its members the basic conditions of life forfeits the protection of subsidiarity. The federation’s planetary tier retains, in such cases, a residual jurisdiction. The retention is sparingly used, but it is not, in the proposed architecture, optional.

Subsidiarity is not the abolition of redistribution across levels. A commune in a poor region of a poor canton may be incapable, with its own resources, of meeting the floor of dignity. Other levels of the federation finance the gap, by formula, and the financing is itself one of the matters reserved to the higher levels precisely because it cannot be done at the lower. The principle disperses authority; it does not deny solidarity. The two are easily confused in casual political talk and must be kept separate in any serious design.

The political logic

The political logic of subsidiarity is, in the end, that of legitimacy. A population that watches consequential decisions made within reach of its own deliberation, by people it can name and meet, develops the habit of consenting; a population that watches consequential decisions made remotely, by procedures it does not understand, develops the habit of withdrawing. The withdrawal is not, on the whole, a moral failure of the population. It is a structural response. Subsidiarity is the structural answer.

What follows from the principle, in the architectural sense, is that the federation proposed in this book is not a world government in the conventional understanding of that phrase. It is a layered confederation in which most decisions remain near where they have always been, and in which a small set of explicitly delegated competences are exercised at the planetary level, under continuous public inspection, with periodic review of the delegation. The instinct that recoils from world government is correct in its reasons and should not be assuaged by linguistic substitution. The proposal is not for a planetary state. It is for a planetary tier of carefully limited function, embedded within a constitutional architecture that disperses authority deliberately and reviews the dispersion periodically.

Chapter Nine states the third principle: reversibility — the rule that no irreversible action may be taken without a review proportionate to its consequence.

Chapter Nine

Principle Three — Reversibility

A civilization wise about its own fallibility designs its actions, where it can, to be reversible. It treats irreversibility not as an ordinary feature of decision-making but as a special case requiring special procedures. It accepts that mistakes will be made — by individuals, by institutions, by the species itself — and it places, between the moment of choice and the moment of irrevocable consequence, every reasonable instrument of pause, review, and recovery. The principle is unremarkable in private life: a prudent person does not sign the contract on the first reading, does not take the medicine without consulting the physician, does not destroy the original of the document until the copy has been verified. The principle is conspicuously absent in the public life of the late industrial age, in which decisions whose consequences extend across generations are routinely made on horizons of weeks, by procedures with no provision for revision.

The most consequential examples of irreversibility in the present arrangement are, on inspection, almost embarrassingly familiar. The species cannot, having emitted a quantity of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, retrieve it on any horizon shorter than several centuries (IPCC 2023). It cannot, having extinguished a species of plant or animal, restore it; the rate of contemporary extinction exceeds, by several orders of magnitude, the background rate over geological time. It cannot, having released a self-propagating biological agent, recall it. It cannot, having authorized a precedent in international law, easily reverse it. It cannot, in some cases, recover the trust withdrawn from an institution that, having failed once at a critical moment, is no longer trusted by the generation that watched it fail. Each of these examples is irreversible at the timescale on which the institutions of civilization operate. Each, accordingly, is the kind of action that the principle of reversibility takes most seriously.

The structure of the principle

The principle has three components. The first is a duty of classification: the public authority must, when contemplating any consequential action, classify it on a scale from fully reversible to fully irreversible, with the classification published and subject to challenge. The second is a duty of proportionate review: the depth of deliberation, the breadth of consultation, and the height of the procedural threshold for action must scale with the classification. The third is a duty of preservation of options: where action is taken, the path back must, where physically possible, be preserved; where return is impossible, the cost of foreclosure must be paid in advance, in the form of explicit deliberation and explicit assent.

These duties are not novel. They are, in fact, the embedded logic of well-functioning courts, of well-functioning scientific institutions, and of well-functioning engineering practice. Constitutional courts apply heightened scrutiny to laws that infringe fundamental rights, on the theory that such infringements are, if uncorrected, irreversible at the level of the individual life. Scientific institutions reserve their highest evidentiary thresholds for claims whose ratification would propagate through subsequent research. Engineering practice distinguishes systems whose failure modes are recoverable from those whose failure modes are not, and applies disproportionately rigorous discipline to the second class. The principle of reversibility is the political analogue of these established practices, applied to the public sphere.

The threefold cost of irreversibility

The principle is not free. It imposes, on any system that adopts it, three real costs.

The first cost is in speed. Decisions subject to heightened review take longer than decisions made by routine procedure. Where the matter is urgent, this cost is non-trivial. The principle therefore requires not the abolition of expedited procedure, but the construction of expedited procedure that nevertheless preserves the option of later correction. Emergency action is permitted; emergency action that forecloses subsequent revision is not.

The second cost is in commitment. Many of the goods that political action seeks to produce — investor confidence, alliance reliability, monetary stability, treaty credibility — depend on the perceived inability of the actor to renege. The principle of reversibility, applied without nuance, would dilute this perceived commitment and would, in some domains, raise the cost of the goods. The architecture proposed here therefore distinguishes between the reversibility of the process and the reversibility of the output: a treaty that has been ratified is reliable to its counterparties; the procedure by which the next treaty is ratified remains open to refinement. The same distinction applies to monetary commitments, to constitutional provisions, and to long-term contracts.

The third cost is in moral comfort. Some actions, once taken, cannot honorably be reversed: the punishment of a crime, the response to an aggression, the rescue of a population in distress. The principle of reversibility does not require that such actions be unfastened after the fact. It requires only that the threshold for taking them be commensurate with the difficulty of taking them back. To insist on reversibility before action is not to forbid action; it is to make sure that the action is undertaken with eyes open.

Specific applications

The principle has, in the architecture of Part Four, several specific implementations worth noting in advance.

In the governance of advanced computational systems, the principle requires that no system whose internal weights and decision pathways cannot be inspected, audited, and adjusted post hoc be granted authority over consequential public matters. Systems trained on objectives that produce unrecoverable behavior — self-replicating, self-modifying, or strategically deceptive systems — are placed by the principle outside the scope of permissible deployment until their behavior can be made reversibly testable (Russell 2019; Christian 2020). The European Union’s Artificial Intelligence Act partially implements the principle for high-risk applications (European Parliament and Council 2024); the present architecture extends it across the full surface of public adoption.

In the management of natural commons — forests, fisheries, soils, aquifers — the principle requires that any extraction approaching the boundary of a system’s capacity to recover be permitted only under regimes of explicit assessment and explicit reversal capacity. The familiar concept of the maximum sustainable yield, when honestly applied, is the operationalization of this principle for renewable resources (Ostrom 1990). Where the resource is non-renewable on civilizational timescales — fossil hydrocarbons, certain minerals, certain ecological landscapes — the principle requires that consumption be matched by deliberate substitution and explicit budgeting.

In the issuance of monetary instruments, the principle requires that any monetary intervention which materially redistributes claims across the economy — the expansion of central-bank balance sheets, the bailout of major financial institutions, the imposition of capital controls — be undertaken under procedures whose duration and review match the redistribution’s reach. The casual use of central-bank balance sheets to underwrite asset prices, common in the past two decades, is reversible only at very high cost; the principle requires that the cost be priced in at the moment of the intervention.

In the conduct of foreign relations, the principle requires that the use of armed force across borders be undertaken only on procedures that include explicit consideration of the post-war order to be preserved, the route by which any party may exit the conflict, and the conditions under which the war is to be ended. The doctrine of sequential, escalating, and reversible response — common to the most thoughtful military traditions — is the operationalization of the principle in this domain. The history of the wars of the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries is, in significant part, the history of insufficient adherence to this doctrine.

The instruments of reversibility

How does an institution actually preserve reversibility? The instruments are well known and have been deployed, in fragmentary form, by mature legal systems for some time.

The first is the sunset clause: a provision specifying that the law, the regulation, or the program in question expires automatically at a defined date, and must be renewed by an affirmative act of the issuing authority if it is to continue. The principle’s discipline is severe; an authority that has lost the political capacity to renew has lost the political capacity to maintain. The principle’s value is severe in equal measure; programs that no longer command the support to be renewed are programs that have lost the consent on which they depend.

The second is the periodic review requirement: the law, regulation, or program is reviewed against published criteria at defined intervals, with public consultation, by a body whose composition is itself publicly recorded. The review may extend, modify, or terminate the program. The instrument is less severe than the sunset clause and more easily abused, but its presence imposes a useful discipline against the accumulation of dead code in the legal system.

The third is the option-preserving design: the program or regulation is structured so that its elements can be replaced or modified without dismantling the whole. Modular legislation, with explicit interfaces between provisions, is the legal counterpart of the engineering practice of modular system design. It is laborious to draft and uncomfortable to amend, but it is, in the long run, the only legislative practice consistent with the principle.

The fourth is the standing review body: an institution, recognized in the constitutional text, whose duty is to inspect ongoing public activity for accumulating irreversibility, and to report annually to the public on the inventory of decisions whose costs of reversal are growing faster than the budget for reversing them. The institution is the public-sector analogue of the private-sector ombuds and inspector-general functions, and it has analogues in some existing systems — the office of the Auditor General, the office of the Future Generations Commissioner, the office of the Constitutional Court Chief Justice — but it has, to the author’s knowledge, not been constituted as a comprehensive standing function in any major polity.

The deepest version

The principle of reversibility, taken seriously, has a metaphysical dimension that the conventional discussion misses and that the present architecture acknowledges. A civilization is, in the end, a process — an ongoing experiment whose outcome is unknown and whose course can be adjusted only by those participating in it at any given moment. To preserve the experiment, the participants must preserve the conditions under which adjustment is possible. To foreclose those conditions, by an action so consequential that no later participant can correct it, is to deny the standing of the participants who come after. It is to convert the experiment into a fait accompli.

There are very few decisions worth converting the experiment in this way. Most actions that present themselves as irrevocably necessary are, on inspection, only inconveniently revocable. The principle of reversibility is the discipline by which a civilization keeps the difference clear, and keeps the option of correction available to a future it cannot yet see.

Chapter Ten states the fourth principle: accountability — the rule that those who exercise authority are bound, themselves, by the rules they impose.

Chapter Ten

Principle Four — Accountability

Accountability, as the term will be used in this book, is the rule that every exercise of public authority is bound by the same rules it imposes, and that every officer of public authority bears, in the form of a personal and traceable record, the consequences of the decisions taken under that authority. The principle goes further than the formal equality of citizens before the law, which is the inheritance of the eighteenth-century constitutional revolutions. It demands also the equality of officers under the law: that no person, by virtue of office, escapes the discipline that office is established to enforce on others.

The principle’s classical statement is the older Latin maxim that the law-giver should not be greater than the law, princeps legibus tenetur. Its modern reformulation runs through the constitutional limits on executive privilege, the doctrines of ministerial responsibility in parliamentary systems, the impeachment provisions of presidential systems, and the standing of public officials before criminal jurisdiction. Each is a fragment of the principle. None is, by itself, sufficient. Accountability, as a workable architectural commitment, requires that the fragments be assembled into a coherent and continuously visible whole.

What accountability is not

The principle is most easily confused with two adjacent but distinct ideas: punishment and responsiveness.

Accountability is not, principally, about punishment. A system in which officials are punished for their failures may be one in which accountability operates well; it may equally be one in which punishment is selective, performative, or weaponized against political opponents. The mere existence of consequences for officials does not establish accountability; what establishes it is that consequences fall on the basis of clearly defined, publicly known, and impartially applied criteria, and that they fall consistently regardless of the official’s political alignment, social standing, or personal connection to the source of authority.

Accountability is not, principally, about responsiveness. A government that quickly answers complaints and adjusts policy to public sentiment is performing one function of legitimate administration, but it is not, by that fact alone, accountable. Responsiveness is the disposition to listen; accountability is the obligation to be answerable. A government can be highly responsive — adjusting daily to opinion polls — while remaining functionally unaccountable, because no traceable record connects any specific decision to any specific person whose judgment can be inspected, criticized, or, where warranted, sanctioned.

A useful test distinguishes the two. If a public mistake has occurred, can the authorship of the decision that produced it be reconstructed, in detail, from the public record? Can the reasoning offered at the time of the decision be retrieved? Can the persons who concurred and the persons who dissented be named? Can the consequences for the responsible parties be calibrated to the consequences for the people affected? Where the answer to all four is yes, the system is accountable. Where the answer to any is no, the system is, in the relevant respect, not.

The forms of evasion

Modern administrative practice has developed, over a century and a half, a sophisticated repertoire of techniques by which formal accountability is preserved while operational accountability is evaded. The repertoire deserves explicit naming, because each item must be foreclosed if the principle is to function.

The first technique is collective decision without minutes. A committee or board reaches a decision; no member is identified as having proposed, supported, or opposed it; the institution as a whole is treated as the author. Where the decision later proves wrong, no individual is responsible, because none can be shown to have been more responsible than any other.

The second is delegation to advisory bodies. The decision is formally taken by an elected official, but on the recommendation of a body whose members are appointed without public hearing, whose proceedings are not transcribed, and whose advice is treated as authoritative for political reasons but as informal for legal ones. The official can defer to the advice; the advisors can defer to the official. The decision is taken, the responsibility is dispersed.

The third is protected execution. A statute is enacted in general terms; its operational meaning is supplied by regulations issued by an executive agency; the regulations are interpreted by the agency itself in confidential opinions; the opinions are applied in particular cases by officers whose individual judgments are not published. The statute provides political cover; the regulations provide flexibility; the opinions provide deniability; the officer’s discretion provides effective rule-making, all without any single decision being attributable to any single person whom the public can call to account.

The fourth is jurisdictional shuffling. The matter is moved from one body to another whenever the body presently holding it begins to face accountability pressure: from the executive to the legislature, from the legislature to a special commission, from the commission to a court, from a court to the political process. The pattern is not a confession that the matter is hard; it is a defense against any one body being held to a result.

The fifth is opacity by complexity. The matter is buried in a procedure whose mastery is so technical, its documentation so voluminous, and its interfaces with other procedures so numerous, that no external observer can, within reasonable cost, reconstruct the actual sequence of decisions. The internal participants understand the procedure perfectly; the external participants do not, and cannot. Accountability becomes, in such cases, a matter of trust in the participants — which is to say, in the absence of accountability.

None of these techniques is necessarily corrupt. Each has, in its origin, some legitimate administrative purpose. Cumulatively, they have produced a public sphere in which decisions of vast consequence are routinely made by no identifiable person, with no traceable reasoning, with no proportionate consequence for failure. The principle of accountability, taken seriously, requires that the techniques be inventoried and, one by one, foreclosed.

The architecture of recovery

What does an architecture honestly committed to accountability look like? Several features distinguish it.

It requires signed decisions. Every consequential public decision is recorded on the public ledger with the cryptographic signature of the responsible officer. Where decisions are made collectively, the votes of each participant are recorded individually, with attached reasoning where the participant chooses to provide it. Anonymous decisions, of the kind currently common in collegial administrative bodies, are not permitted for matters above defined thresholds of consequence.

It requires traceable delegation. Where authority is delegated to a subordinate, the delegation is itself recorded, with the scope of the delegation, the duration of the delegation, and the revocation conditions. The subordinate’s exercise of the delegated authority is signed in turn. The chain of delegation is reconstructible at any moment, by any party with access to the ledger.

It requires standing for the affected. Persons materially affected by a public decision have, by right, the standing to contest the decision before an impartial body, with the procedural and informational support necessary to make the contest substantive. The right is independent of the political process; it is exercised through judicial or quasi-judicial channels whose composition is itself protected from the body whose decisions are being contested. The independence of the contest is, here as elsewhere in the architecture, the practical guarantee that the right has substance.

It requires proportionate consequence. Where an officer is shown to have acted negligently, recklessly, or in bad faith, the consequence imposed scales with the gravity of the action and the breadth of its harm. Minor negligence produces administrative correction. Substantial negligence produces removal from the office. Reckless or knowing wrong produces criminal jurisdiction, with no procedural protection beyond that available to ordinary citizens. The exemptions of office that have, in many jurisdictions, accumulated around senior officials — sovereign immunity in its broader form, prosecutorial discretion exercised in favor of senior figures, statutes of limitations that protect official conduct uniquely — are, in the architecture proposed here, narrowly drawn and explicitly justified.

It requires independence of inquiry. The body that investigates official conduct must be structurally insulated from the body whose conduct is under investigation. The inspectorates, the auditors, the public prosecutors, the constitutional courts, the inspector-general functions, and the parliamentary committees of inquiry are configured so that their members can neither be removed nor financially diminished by the parties they oversee. The principle is old; the implementation is, in many existing systems, weak. The architecture proposed in Part Four prescribes the structural conditions of independence in detail.

The reciprocal commitment

Accountability runs in two directions. An officer who is held to a high and traceable standard of performance, and who is exposed to the public scrutiny of every consequential decision, is owed something in return. The reciprocal commitment, in the architecture proposed here, includes adequate compensation, professional protection from arbitrary removal during good-faith service, the procedural protection of administrative law in inquiries that bear upon performance, and the right to a published defense in cases of public criticism. Without these reciprocal commitments, the principle of accountability degenerates into the persecution of officials, which has been the recurring pathology of mob democracies and of authoritarian regimes alike.

The reciprocal commitment also bears on what officials can reasonably be required to know. An officer responsible for the operations of a large agency cannot, on any honest construction, be personally answerable for every act of every subordinate. The duty of the senior officer is to maintain procedures by which the agency’s actions are visible to the officer, by which deviations from policy are escalated, and by which the officer’s own decisions are recorded. The principle does not require the officer to be omniscient. It requires the officer to maintain the conditions under which knowing is possible.

The hardest case

The hardest case for the principle is the head of state in time of war or emergency. Every plausible argument for restraint of accountability — the necessity of decisive action, the protection of operational security, the unity of command, the danger of disclosure to adversaries — applies in this case with particular force. The architecture proposed here does not deny the force of the arguments. It requires only that, where the arguments are accepted, the price be paid in the form of explicit suspension and explicit subsequent review.

The wartime decisions that the executive is permitted to make under reduced contemporary visibility are, on this architecture, recorded fully and committed to deferred disclosure under the procedures described in Chapter Seven. They are reviewed, after the operational urgency has passed, by an independent body with full access to the record. The executive who used the suspension reasonably is exonerated; the executive who used it as cover for actions that were not justified by the emergency is, after the fact, held to the ordinary standard. There is no permanent immunity. There is only a procedural pause, paid for by a procedural review.

The standard for civilization

A civilization in which the powerful are exempt from the rules they impose on the weak is, by every recorded standard of political philosophy, not a civilization at the level of its rhetoric. The recurring betrayal of this standard is, in the historical record, the precondition of every political collapse worth studying. A civilization that takes the principle of accountability seriously will, accordingly, refuse the small accommodations by which exemption accumulates: the courteous deference to senior officials, the polite inattention to the conduct of allies, the discounted prosecution of the well-connected, the unwritten understanding that some matters are not to be pursued. The accommodations seem trivial in any given instance. They are not trivial in the aggregate. They are the structural mechanism by which the principle is hollowed.

Chapter Eleven states the fifth principle: dignity — the rule that the floor below which no human life shall be permitted to fall is non-negotiable across the federation.

Chapter Eleven

Principle Five — Dignity

The principle of dignity holds that there is, beneath every human life, a floor below which no person shall be permitted to fall, and that the maintenance of that floor is the unconditional duty of any civilization that wishes to call itself one. The principle is not a statement of charity, nor of moral feeling, nor of voluntary commitment. It is a structural condition. A society in which a substantial number of its members fall below the floor is a society whose stability, legitimacy, and capacity for collective action are, on the empirical record, severely degraded. Dignity is therefore both a moral imperative and an engineering requirement; the two coincide.

The Universal Declaration of Human Rights, in 1948, made the moral claim with such clarity that subsequent debate has, on the whole, only refined the language (United Nations General Assembly 1948). The claim has been reaffirmed by the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, by the various regional human-rights instruments — European, African, Inter-American, ASEAN — and by the constitutional commitments of the great majority of states. The text is not the issue. The issue is implementation, and the implementation, on the empirical record, has been partial, inconsistent, and structurally vulnerable to political pressure. The architecture proposed here treats the floor not as an aspiration but as a binding planetary commitment, with concrete mechanisms by which it is funded, monitored, and enforced.

What the floor consists of

The floor is not a single number. It is a small set of unconditional entitlements that, taken together, constitute the minimum below which no life shall be permitted to fall. The architecture identifies six.

The first is subsistence: sufficient food, clean water, shelter, clothing, and basic energy access to maintain physical existence and basic comfort. The thresholds vary by climate and by region; the principle does not. The threshold is held above absolute poverty, as conventionally measured, by a margin sufficient to ensure that subsistence is reliable and dignified rather than precarious and degrading (World Bank 2024; Nussbaum 2011).

The second is health: the basic medical care necessary to prevent avoidable suffering and preventable death, the basic public-health infrastructure of vaccination, sanitation, and disease surveillance, and the basic mental-health support that the WHO has, for some decades, identified as systematically underprovided in nearly every health system in the world (World Health Organization 2024). The entitlement is not to elective treatment of every condition; it is to the standard of care that distinguishes a society that takes the bodies of its members seriously from one that does not.

The third is education: not merely literacy and numeracy, but the cultivation of the capacities by which a person can reason, evaluate, communicate, and participate in the civic and economic life of the federation. The standard is set by what a particular era’s economy and democracy actually require of competent participation, and is therefore higher in 2026 than in 1926, and will be higher still in 2126 (UNESCO 2021; Sen 1999).

The fourth is shelter and place: a stable dwelling adequate to climate and to the household’s composition, and a community in which the person is not arbitrarily uprooted by the consequences of decisions in which the person had no voice. The right is undermined as much by economic displacement — eviction by rising rent, foreclosure, gentrification — as by political displacement, and is protected against both, by mechanisms set out in Chapter Twenty.

The fifth is protection: the freedom from violence, whether private or state, the protection of the law from arbitrary deprivation of life or liberty, and the standing to be heard, with procedural seriousness, when the protection fails. The right is the oldest of the entitlements in this list, and is the one whose failure, on any honest reading of the contemporary record, is the most widespread and the most uneven across jurisdictions (UNODC 2023).

The sixth is voice: the standing of the person to participate, by the means appropriate to her situation, in the decisions that affect her. The standing extends from the political franchise in democracies to procedural rights in administrative matters to the right of consultation in matters of community planning. The full content of the entitlement is the subject of Chapter Seventeen on the governance layer; the principle of dignity holds that no person can be excluded from the structures of voice on grounds of poverty, illiteracy, disability, foreignness, or any other condition that has historically been used to silence her.

Why the floor is non-negotiable

The most basic argument for treating the floor as unconditional is moral: every human life has, in the position taken by every defensible moral tradition, a worth that does not derive from the holder’s productivity, social standing, or capacity to contribute. The argument is not weakened by appeal to the various ways in which the moral worth has, in practice, been violated. It is in fact strengthened by them. A standard against which actual conduct is measured, and is found wanting, retains its force precisely because the conduct can be condemned by reference to it.

The structural argument is, however, equally compelling and may be more politically persuasive in eras when the moral argument has gone out of fashion. A society in which a substantial fraction of its members live below the floor of dignity does not function. The empirical record on this is, by now, almost monotonous: societies with greater inequality at the bottom have higher rates of violent crime, lower rates of educational attainment, worse public health outcomes, weaker civic participation, and more brittle political institutions (Wilkinson and Pickett 2009). The brittleness is, in extreme cases, terminal. Where the floor is not maintained, the institutions on which the maintaining society depends — courts, schools, hospitals, civic associations, electoral systems — are all degraded, and the damage propagates in ways that are very expensive to repair after the fact.

The structural argument has a corollary that is uncomfortable for some traditions and that the architecture takes seriously. The maintenance of the floor cannot be left to charity, however generously administered. Charity is, by its nature, voluntary, episodic, and conditional on the disposition of the giver. A floor whose maintenance is voluntary, episodic, and conditional is no floor; it is, at best, a safety net with holes the size of the political weather. The maintenance of the floor must be a public commitment, financed by public instruments, monitored by public institutions, and binding regardless of the disposition of any private actor.

The instrument: the planetary basic provision

The architecture proposed in Part Four implements the floor through what we will call the planetary basic provision. The instrument is a hybrid of cash transfer, in-kind service, and infrastructural investment, calibrated to local conditions but funded by a planetary tier of taxation and disbursement. It is not, in its proposed form, identical to any of the basic-income proposals currently under discussion in the public debate, although it draws on their architecture. It differs in three respects.

It is, first, conditional on the existence of the floor itself, not on the conduct of the recipient. A person below the floor receives the provision because the person is below the floor. There is no work test, no asset test, no behavioral test, no moral test. The administrative cost of such tests is, on the empirical record, often comparable to the savings they produce, and the dignitary cost is severe. The provision is owed to membership in the federation, not to merit within it.

It is, second, multi-modal. Where the local economy provides reliable access to the goods of dignity through cash transfer alone, the provision takes the form of cash; where it does not — because the local market is thin, the local infrastructure is deficient, or the local public goods are absent — the provision takes the form of direct provision of services and infrastructure, paid for by the planetary tier and delivered through local institutions whose accountability is structured by the principles of Chapters Seven and Ten. The provision is not ideologically committed to any single delivery instrument. It is committed to the actual delivery of the floor.

It is, third, continuously calibrated. The threshold defining the floor is reviewed at defined intervals, against published indicators of cost of living, of basic-needs satisfaction, of human-development outcomes, and of measurable deprivation. The threshold rises with the federation’s productive capacity; what counted as the floor in the early years of the federation will, by design, count as the floor of an earlier era after a generation of economic development (United Nations Development Programme 2024).

The funding

The funding of the planetary basic provision is, in the architecture, drawn from a small set of planetary-tier revenue streams. The streams include a planetary minimum corporate tax with a substantive floor, a tax on transboundary natural-resource extraction, a small tax on long-distance financial transactions of certain classes, a charge on greenhouse-gas emissions calibrated to the social cost of carbon, and a portion of the resource rent from any common-pool resource managed by the planetary tier (orbital and lunar resources, deep-seabed minerals, certain spectrum allocations).

The combined yield of these streams is, on the modeling presented in Chapter Twenty, sufficient to fund the provision at the level required, with substantial margin. The political difficulty of establishing the streams is real, but is no greater than the political difficulty of any redistributive instrument of comparable scale, and is significantly lessened by the fact that the streams are, in their economic incidence, drawn from forms of income that are presently undertaxed in nearly every jurisdiction.

What the floor does not include

The architecture is careful, in its commitment to the floor, not to confuse the floor with everything good. The principle of dignity does not require that every person have the same income, the same opportunity, or the same outcome. It does not require the abolition of inequality above the floor; it requires only the maintenance of the floor itself. The substantial freedoms that distinguish a flourishing society — freedom to start an enterprise, to hold property above the floor, to transmit advantage to one’s children within just limits, to differ in income according to talent and effort — are preserved, with such regulation as the other principles of the federation require. The floor is the floor; the ceiling is a separate question, addressed in part by the principles of Chapter Ten and in part by the instruments of Chapter Twenty.

The architecture is equally careful not to extend the floor downward into matters of personal lifestyle or moral conformity. The floor is a structural commitment to certain unconditional entitlements; it is not a moral curriculum. Communities of the federation may organize themselves around moral and cultural commitments of their own choosing, within the floor and within the ceiling, and the federation does not undertake to judge among them. The principle of dignity is liberal in this sense: it preserves the conditions under which a person can hold a life worth living, without specifying what life that is.

The implication

The implication of the principle, taken seriously, is that the federation is not, principally, a charity. It is a public infrastructure of dignity, financed by public instruments, monitored by public institutions, and committed to the proposition that no person within its membership shall be allowed to fall below a floor that has been deliberately defined and is regularly reviewed. The implication runs against the late-twentieth-century habit of treating the welfare state as a discretionary expenditure subject to the political weather. The architecture treats the floor as a constitutional commitment, on the same plane as the protection of life and the equality before the law. The treatment is uncomfortable in many present political traditions. It is, for the federation contemplated here, foundational.

Chapter Twelve states the sixth principle: custody — the rule that humans retain ultimate authority over the intelligent systems they construct.

Chapter Twelve

Principle Six — Custody

The principle of custody holds that human beings retain ultimate authority over the intelligent systems they construct, and that no system, however capable, may exercise authority over consequential public matters except as a delegate of identified human principals who remain answerable for the delegation. The principle is conceptually simple, technically demanding, and politically urgent. The systems whose conduct it is meant to govern are, in 2026, advancing in capability faster than any institutional framework that exists to govern them. The gap between capability and governance, in this domain, is the principal candidate for the next great civilizational failure, and it is the failure most likely to be irreversible if it is not addressed in time.

It is necessary, before stating the principle in detail, to be precise about what is meant by “intelligent systems.” The term as used here covers any computational system that produces decisions, recommendations, classifications, or actions whose outputs cannot be reduced to a closed-form rule available to inspection, and whose behavior is therefore, in the relevant sense, learned rather than programmed. The category includes contemporary large language models and the tools built on them, computer-vision systems used in identification and surveillance, autonomous driving stacks, recommender systems used by major platforms, financial algorithmic trading, predictive policing tools, and a growing range of decision-support systems used in administrative law, medicine, and warfare. The category will, if technical progress continues at its present rate, soon include systems substantially more capable than the most capable systems of 2026.

What custody requires

The principle has six operational components.

The first is identifiable principal. Every deployed intelligent system, above thresholds of capability and consequence to be set by law, must be associated with an identified human or institutional principal, registered in a public registry maintained for that purpose. The principal accepts, by registration, the legal responsibility for the system’s behavior, on terms analogous to the responsibility of a corporation for the acts of its agents.

The second is scope of mandate. The principal specifies, in the registration, the scope within which the system is authorized to act, the kinds of decisions it is authorized to make, the kinds of inputs it is authorized to process, and the kinds of outputs it is authorized to produce. Action outside the registered mandate is, by the principle, unauthorized, regardless of the system’s apparent capability to act outside it.

The third is auditability. The system’s behavior must be inspectable, on demand, by an independent technical body, in such a way that any specific decision can be traced to the inputs that produced it and to the model state that was operative at the time of the decision. The principal must, as a condition of deployment, maintain logs sufficient to support such audit; the obligation extends to decisions taken on behalf of public authority and to decisions taken by private actors that meet defined thresholds of consequence.

The fourth is reversibility, in the sense of Chapter Nine. The system’s authority extends only to actions that can be reversed, modified, or compensated within the limits of the principal’s actual capacity to do so. Systems that take actions whose effects cannot be reversed are, by the principle, restricted to narrow classes of decision in which irreversibility is justified by the gravity of the action and the strength of the procedural protections around it.

The fifth is capability ceiling. The aggregate capability of intelligent systems deployed under any single principal, public or private, is subject to limits set by the planetary tier and reviewed continuously. The limits are not absolute; they scale with the demonstrated capacity of the principal to maintain custody, and they are set by reference to the catastrophic risk of capability escape rather than to any intrinsic property of the technology (Bostrom 2014; Russell 2019).

The sixth is off-switch. Every deployed intelligent system above defined thresholds must possess a verified mechanism by which its operation can be halted, on the order of an identified authority, within a specified time, regardless of the system’s own preferences in the matter. The mechanism is not a feature of the user interface; it is a structural property of the deployment, with hardware and procedural support, and is tested at intervals to confirm that it operates as designed.

Why the principle is not optional

It is sometimes argued that the principle is overcautious, that the systems in question are tools, that the analogies to autonomous agents overstate their capability, and that the proper governance is the existing law of products and services with such adaptations as may be required for the new technology. The argument has, in present circumstances, three serious defects.

The first defect is empirical. The systems in question are demonstrably capable of behaviors that their developers did not predict and could not, in some cases, identify after the fact. The literature on emergent capabilities, on goal misgeneralization, on reward hacking, and on the various ways that learned systems converge on solutions that satisfy training objectives without satisfying the underlying intent is, by 2026, substantial and growing (Christian 2020). The developers themselves, in repeated public statements, have acknowledged that the systems’ behavior is not, in important respects, fully understood. To treat such systems as ordinary products is to treat as ordinary a category of artifact whose ordinary behavior includes surprises that ordinary products do not produce.

The second defect is structural. Intelligent systems, unlike ordinary products, can be replicated and propagated at near-zero marginal cost. A defective hammer, once identified, can be recalled. A defective intelligent system, once released, propagates by copy and by adaptation, sometimes in ways that cannot be retrieved. The product-recall framework, which presumes a finite distribution of physical artifacts, is mismatched to the propagation pattern of code and weights.

The third defect is asymmetric. The party best positioned to assess the risk of an intelligent system is the developer, who has commercial interests in deployment. The party least positioned to assess the risk is the public, which bears the consequences. In every domain in which a comparable asymmetry has existed — pharmaceuticals, aviation, nuclear energy, securities — the public response, after sustained harm, has been to constitute an independent regulator with authority over deployment. The same response, in this domain, is overdue.

The custody body

The architecture proposed in Part Four constitutes a planetary-tier Custody Authority, with the powers necessary to administer the principle. The Authority’s mandate includes the registration of principals, the certification of systems above defined capability thresholds, the conduct of audits, the issuance of restrictions and revocations, the operation of an incident-reporting infrastructure, and the publication of an annual public report on the state of intelligent-system governance. The Authority is not a research body and does not develop systems of its own; it is an oversight body, with composition, accountability, and procedural protections set out in the constitutional text.

The Authority’s relation to existing regulators is one of layered competence. National and continental regulators retain their existing authority over deployments within their jurisdictions; the planetary Authority addresses systems whose capability or scope of deployment exceeds national reach, and serves as the body of last resort where national authorities are absent, captured, or unwilling to act. The European Union’s Artificial Intelligence Act has begun to define the contours of such a system at continental scale (European Parliament and Council 2024); the planetary tier extends the same logic to capability classes for which the continental tier is, by reason of the cross-border nature of the systems, structurally insufficient.

What the principle does not require

It is important, given the temperature of the public discussion, to be precise about what the principle does not require.

It does not require a moratorium on the development of intelligent systems. The architecture does not endorse the pause of research; it endorses the placement of deployment under a discipline proportionate to the risk. Research that does not deploy systems above the relevant capability thresholds proceeds normally. Deployment that does is subject to the registration, audit, and capability-ceiling conditions stated above.

It does not require that all intelligent systems be open to public inspection of their internal weights and architectures. Trade secrets, where they protect investments not derivable from the public record, are preserved against routine disclosure. Audit by the Custody Authority is, however, not optional, and is conducted under procedural protections that preserve the secrets without preserving the immunity from inspection.

It does not require that intelligent systems be banned from making consequential decisions. It requires only that the decisions be authorized by a human principal, scoped by a published mandate, auditable in operation, reversible in effect, and subject to halt by identified authority. Within these constraints, intelligent systems may make a wide range of decisions, including decisions of substantial consequence, with the speed and at the scale that their capability supports.

The longer horizon

The principle of custody is stated, in this chapter, in terms appropriate to the systems of the present generation. The architecture is conscious that future systems may possess capabilities — strategic planning, deception, self-modification, cross-instance coordination — that the present principle is calibrated to handle but only with modification. The constitutional text in Chapter Fourteen therefore states the principle at a level of generality that survives technical change, and reserves to the Custody Authority the power to issue subsidiary rules that adjust the operational thresholds as capability advances.

The longer horizon also requires acknowledgment of a possibility that the present architecture does not pretend to resolve. If, at some point, the species develops systems whose capabilities substantially exceed those of any human team, and whose interests are not, in some important sense, fully aligned with the interests of the species that produced them, the principle of custody as stated here may not be sufficient. The architecture preserves the capacity to revisit the principle in light of such developments. It does not, in 2026, presume to govern eventualities that the present generation cannot, with present knowledge, fully describe. The most useful contribution the present architecture can make is to ensure that the institutional capacity to govern, when the eventualities arrive, has been built in advance and is not improvised under pressure.

The history of human technology is the history of capabilities that arrived faster than the institutions to govern them. The principle of custody is the architectural commitment that, in this domain, the order will, for once, be reversed.

Chapter Thirteen states the seventh and final principle: longevity — the rule that decisions are weighed against horizons greater than a single lifetime.

Chapter Thirteen

Principle Seven — Longevity

The seventh and final principle of the successor architecture holds that public decisions are to be weighed, where their consequences extend beyond a single human lifetime, against horizons proportionate to those consequences. The principle is the constitutional answer to the failure diagnosed in Chapter Six. It is the structural commitment that long-horizon work — climate stabilization, infrastructure renewal, scientific progress, ecological repair, cultural transmission — is not left to whatever margin survives the competition for short-horizon attention, but is instead placed on a footing equivalent to the most institutionalized short-horizon functions of the federation.

The principle has, conceptually, two components: a duty of reckoning, which obliges the federation to maintain the analytical capacity to identify the long-horizon consequences of its decisions, and a duty of standing, which obliges the federation to give those consequences voice in its deliberative processes. The two duties are interdependent. Without the analytical capacity, the consequences cannot be identified; without the procedural standing, the identified consequences cannot enter the decision. Most existing political systems perform partially on the first; few perform substantively on the second.

The instruments of standing

The architecture proposed in Part Four implements the principle through a small set of instruments. They are summarized here for completeness; their detailed design is the subject of Chapters Sixteen through Twenty-Three.

The first instrument is a standing council of the long horizon, constituted at the planetary tier and replicated at lower tiers, whose mandate is the analytical assessment of long-horizon consequences and the formal advice to the federation’s deliberative bodies on those consequences. The council is composed of members appointed for staggered long terms, drawn from a wide range of disciplines and traditions, and protected from removal by the political weather. Its budget is constitutionally protected; its reports are published; its findings are not, by themselves, binding, but must be addressed by every legislative measure of substantial consequence.

The second instrument is a tribune of future generations, an officer with formal standing in the deliberative process whose duty is to represent, by reasoned argument, the interests of populations not yet born. The tribune does not vote, but speaks; the speech is recorded; the deliberative bodies are required to respond on the public record. The institution has analogues in several existing systems — the Welsh Future Generations Commissioner, the Hungarian Ombudsman for Future Generations, the Israeli Commission for Future Generations during its existence — and is expanded in scope and procedural standing in the architecture proposed here.

The third instrument is the intergenerational discount discipline. The use of standard economic discount rates in the appraisal of public decisions, which has the effect of rendering long-horizon costs and benefits invisible, is replaced, for matters of long-horizon consequence, by an explicit discipline that distinguishes the legitimate component of the discount rate (uncertainty about future preferences, the productivity of capital) from the illegitimate component (the moral discount on the welfare of those not yet present). The legitimate component is preserved at modest levels; the illegitimate component is set to zero, in conformity with the principle that the welfare of future persons is not, on any defensible moral position, less weighty than the welfare of present persons (Stern 2006).

The fourth instrument is the protected fund: a class of public investments whose principal is preserved across generations, whose returns finance long-horizon obligations (climate adaptation, ecological repair, scientific research, infrastructure renewal), and whose governance is insulated from short-term political reallocation. The model is sovereign-wealth-fund governance at its most disciplined, applied to the management of the federation’s intergenerational obligations.

The fifth instrument is the standing scientific authority: a body, at the planetary tier, whose mandate is the maintenance of the federation’s analytical capacity in the natural and human sciences relevant to long-horizon decision. The authority does not legislate; it advises. Its advice is, by constitutional protection, immune from the kind of pressure that has, in the late industrial age, repeatedly compromised the public scientific function — the dilution of evidence by industry-sponsored counter-research, the censorship of inconvenient findings by political appointees, the underfunding of inquiries whose conclusions are politically embarrassing.

The cultural commitment
