Instruments of standing, however well designed, fail in the absence of the cultural commitment to long-horizon thinking that they are intended to support. The architecture acknowledges, accordingly, that the principle of longevity has a cultural dimension that constitutional text alone cannot deliver. The dimension includes the curricula of education, the practices of public commemoration, the rhythms of public ritual, the habits of public deliberation, and the commitments of the professions whose work is, by its nature, long-horizon: the architects, the engineers, the foresters, the historians, the archivists, the curators, the librarians, the researchers, the educators, the planners. None of these commitments can be legislated. All of them can be cultivated by an architecture that gives them institutional standing and material support.

The architecture’s cultural component is, accordingly, modest in its claims and substantial in its provisions. It establishes a planetary archive of consequence, in which decisions and their long-horizon outcomes are recorded for the inspection of subsequent generations. It establishes a planetary observatory of indicators, by which the federation’s progress against long-horizon objectives is continuously measured and reported. It establishes a network of public institutions — universities, libraries, museums, observatories, gardens — whose long-horizon work is funded by the protected fund and is constitutionally insulated from the kind of repurposing that, in the late industrial age, has eroded comparable institutions in many jurisdictions.

The hardest part of the principle

The hardest part of the principle is also the most subtle. It is the requirement that present generations accept costs whose benefits will fall principally on populations they will never meet. The acceptance is not natural. The political and economic incentive structures of the late industrial age have systematically rewarded the inverse — the consumption of capital accumulated by previous generations, the deferral of costs to subsequent ones, the borrowing against futures that the borrowers will not face. The principle of longevity demands the reversal of this disposition, not as an exhortation, but as a structural commitment.

The historical record offers some encouragement. Generations have, in moments of clarity, accepted such costs willingly: the generation that built the Interstate Highway System, the generation that funded the Apollo program, the generations that constructed the European cathedrals, the generations that planted the great national forests, the generations that drafted the constitutions still in operation. The record shows that the disposition is available to populations that have been given a horizon worth committing to. The architecture’s task is to construct, again, the institutional conditions under which such horizons can be deliberately offered.

The narrow path

The principle, in its mature form, walks a narrow path between two errors. The first error is the dismissal of the long horizon, on the grounds that the present is hard enough; this is the error of political short-termism, against which the principle is constructed. The second error is the absorption of the present into the long horizon, on the grounds that nothing matters except the deep future; this is the error of utopian totalism, in which present people and present priorities are sacrificed to a vision whose realization is always one more sacrifice away.

The architecture rejects both. It treats the present as the only moment in which decision is possible, and the long horizon as the moment over which the present’s decisions will be judged. The federation’s obligation is to act in the present, with the resources and the imagination available to it, against horizons that the present can identify and reckon with. It is not to construct, on the bones of present persons, an idealized future whose specifications are dictated by present ideologues. The principle of longevity is, in the end, modest: weigh the consequences; respect the duration; build the institutions; do not pretend that the duration does not exist.

Closing of Part Two

The seven principles — legibility, subsidiarity, reversibility, accountability, dignity, custody, longevity — are the constraints any successor architecture must satisfy. Each, taken alone, has analogues in existing political traditions. None, in their combination at the level of structural commitment proposed here, has been instantiated in any jurisdiction that the historical record yet records. The combination is, accordingly, the architectural novelty of the present proposal. The work of Parts Three and Four is to translate the combination into constitutional text and operational design. The work of Part Five is to specify the transition from where the world stands in 2026 to a federation embodying these principles. The work of Part Six is to ask what becomes possible after.

The principles are not negotiable in the sense that any architecture that abandons one of them is, on the present reading, not a successor architecture but a continuation, by another name, of the trouble the chapters of Part One have described. They are negotiable in the sense that their implementation will, like all real implementations, involve trade-offs, calibrations, and revisions. The architecture proposed here is the author’s best attempt at the implementation, in 2026, with the knowledge then available. It is offered, as the preface said, as a first draft serious enough to be argued with.

Part Three follows. It contains the constitutional text of the federation, together with the commentary that explains the reasoning behind each provision.

Part Three

The Constitution

A constitution is the first promise a civilization makes to itself. The text that follows is offered as a draft. It is meant to be read, criticized, amended, and replaced. The articles state what the federation undertakes; the commentary explains why.

Chapter Fourteen

The Founding Text

What follows is the proposed constitutional text of the Federation of Earth, set forth in numbered articles, in the language and form of a founding instrument. The text is short. Constitutions of long survival have, on the whole, been short — the shorter ones have, in the historical record, outlasted the longer ones — and the present text follows that example. Detailed implementation is left to the organic statutes that the federation, once founded, will enact. The constitutional text constrains those statutes; it does not anticipate them.

Preamble

We, the peoples of Earth, having inherited a civilization whose institutions no longer answer to its scale, conscious of the planet’s finitude and of the duties we owe to those who will live upon it after us, mindful that no jurisdiction now existing is sufficient to the matters now requiring decision, and resolved that the human enterprise shall continue under arrangements adequate to the present age, do hereby ordain and establish this constitution of the Federation of Earth.

Article I — The Federation

Section 1. The Federation of Earth is a confederation of consenting polities, established for the limited purposes set forth in this constitution and constrained by the principles to which its members hereby subscribe.

Section 2. Membership is open to any polity, of any present form, that adopts this constitution within its own legal order, undertakes its obligations, and submits to its institutions on the terms herein established.

Section 3. Membership is voluntary in entry and in continuance. A member polity that wishes to withdraw shall give notice in such form and time as the organic statutes prescribe, and shall be released, on the conclusion of the orderly winding-up of its obligations, from continued membership.

Section 4. The Federation does not extinguish the sovereignty of its members in matters not herein delegated. Authority not herein delegated to the Federation, nor reserved to the lower tiers of governance under Article III, remains with the member polities, exercised by them in such forms as their own constitutions provide.

Article II — Foundational Principles

Section 1. Legibility. Every act of authority taken under this constitution shall be recorded on the public ledger established by Article VII, in a form readable by any person, in real time, with such limited and time-bounded exceptions as the organic statutes prescribe.

Section 2. Subsidiarity. Authority shall be exercised at the lowest tier capable of exercising it well. Authority elevated to a higher tier shall be returned to the lower upon the cessation of the conditions that justified its elevation.

Section 3. Reversibility. No act whose consequences cannot be reversed within the lifetime of those affected shall be taken without procedural review proportionate to the consequence, in such forms as the organic statutes prescribe.

Section 4. Accountability. Every officer of public authority is bound by the rules the office is established to enforce, and is answerable, by signed and recorded action, for the decisions taken under that authority.

Section 5. Dignity. No person within the Federation shall be permitted to fall below the floor of dignity established by Article V. The maintenance of the floor is the unconditional duty of the Federation and its member polities.

Section 6. Custody. Intelligent systems deployed within the Federation are subject to human authority, registered to identified principals, and limited in their scope and capacity in accordance with Article IX.

Section 7. Longevity. The Federation shall maintain the institutional capacity to weigh the long-horizon consequences of its decisions, including the welfare of populations not yet present, in accordance with Article X.

Article III — Tiers of Governance

Section 1. Public authority within the Federation is exercised at five tiers: the local commune, the regional canton, the member polity, the continental confederation where one exists, and the planetary tier of the Federation itself.

Section 2. The planetary tier exercises only those powers expressly delegated by this constitution. Powers not expressly delegated remain with the lower tiers, distributed among them in accordance with the constitutions of the member polities.

Section 3. Powers expressly delegated to the planetary tier are: (a) the maintenance of the public ledger and the planetary archive; (b) the administration of the floor of dignity; (c) the regulation of the atmospheric and oceanic commons, of orbital and lunar resources, and of such other planetary commons as may be added by amendment; (d) the registration and oversight of intelligent systems above the thresholds defined in Article IX; (e) the adjudication of disputes between member polities and between persons and member polities in matters arising under this constitution; (f) the conclusion of treaties on behalf of the Federation in matters within its competence; (g) the levying of the planetary revenues defined in Article VI; (h) the maintenance of the standing institutions named in this constitution.

Section 4. The planetary tier shall not enact, either by legislation or by interpretation, any pre-emption of authority over matters not within its delegated powers. Acts of the planetary tier in excess of these powers are void on the certificate of the Constitutional Court.

Article IV — Persons

Section 1. Every person within the territory of the Federation enjoys, by reason of personhood, the rights guaranteed by this constitution, regardless of citizenship, residence, or status. The protections of this Article extend to persons unable, by reason of age, condition, or circumstance, to invoke them on their own behalf.

Section 2. No person shall be subjected to torture, to enslavement, to forced labor, to arbitrary deprivation of life, to arbitrary detention, or to punishment without trial. These prohibitions are absolute and not subject to derogation in any circumstance.

Section 3. Every person enjoys freedom of thought, conscience, religion, expression, association, peaceful assembly, and movement, subject only to such restrictions as are necessary in a democratic society and proportionate to the lawful objective. The lawful objective shall be specified, in any restriction imposed under this section, with particularity.

Section 4. Every person enjoys the protection of personal data, including the right to know what data concerning the person is held by any authority or enterprise, the right to correct such data, the right to limit its use, and the right to erase it where erasure is consistent with the rights of others. The full content of this protection shall be specified by organic statute.

Section 5. Every person enjoys the equal protection of the laws and shall not be discriminated against on grounds of birth, sex, gender, sexual orientation, race, ethnicity, religion, language, opinion, disability, age, or any analogous ground.

Section 6. Every person enjoys the right to an effective remedy before an impartial tribunal for any infringement of the rights guaranteed by this constitution.

Article V — The Floor of Dignity

Section 1. The floor of dignity comprises six unconditional entitlements: subsistence, health, education, shelter and place, protection, and voice, in the meanings set forth in the organic statute on the floor.

Section 2. The Federation shall maintain a planetary basic provision, financed by the planetary revenues, by which the floor is delivered to every person within the Federation who falls below it. The provision shall be unconditional in respect of conduct, and shall be administered in such mixed forms — cash, service, infrastructure — as the conditions of the recipient region require.

Section 3. The floor’s threshold shall be reviewed at intervals not exceeding five years, against published indicators, and shall be adjusted in accordance with the productive capacity of the Federation.

Article VI — Planetary Revenues

Section 1. The planetary tier shall raise revenues for the limited purposes within its competence by the following instruments only: (a) a planetary minimum on corporate income, levied at a substantive floor on enterprises whose worldwide turnover exceeds the threshold defined by organic statute; (b) a charge on greenhouse-gas emissions, calibrated to the social cost of carbon as determined by the Standing Scientific Authority; (c) a charge on the extraction of natural resources from planetary commons, including orbital, lunar, deep-seabed, and atmospheric commons; (d) a charge on long-distance financial transactions in such categories as the organic statute defines; (e) a charge on the use of orbital, electromagnetic, or other commons subject to common-pool exhaustion.

Section 2. The aggregate planetary revenue shall not exceed three percent of gross planetary product, save in circumstances of declared planetary emergency, and then only with the assent of the Council of Polities.

Section 3. Disbursement of planetary revenues shall be made only for the purposes within the planetary tier’s competence, and shall be recorded in detail on the public ledger.

Article VII — The Public Ledger

Section 1. The Federation shall establish and maintain a public ledger of all acts of public authority taken under this constitution. Entries on the ledger shall be tamper-evident, signed by the responsible officer, queryable by any person, and preserved indefinitely.

Section 2. The technical specifications of the ledger shall ensure independence from any single jurisdiction, redundancy of storage, and continuity of operation across political and technical disruption.

Section 3. Acts of authority subject to lawful and time-bounded confidentiality shall be recorded with metadata sufficient to identify their existence and category, and shall be opened to full inspection on the conclusion of the period of confidentiality, which shall not exceed seven years save by certified extension.

Article VIII — Institutions of the Federation

Section 1. The institutions of the Federation are: (a) the Council of Polities, comprising the duly authorized representatives of member polities, in which substantive decisions of the Federation are made; (b) the Citizens’ Assembly, a body selected by stratified random sampling from the population of the Federation, with formal voice in legislation; (c) the Constitutional Court, the body of last resort in matters arising under this constitution; (d) the Office of the Tribune of Future Generations, with the duties set forth in Article X; (e) the Custody Authority, with the duties set forth in Article IX; (f) the Standing Scientific Authority, an independent body with the duties set forth in Article X; (g) the Office of the Auditor of the Federation, with continuous oversight of the planetary revenues and disbursements.

Section 2. The composition, term, removal procedures, and budget protections of each institution shall be established by organic statute, in such form as preserves the institution’s independence from the body it oversees.

Article IX — Custody of Intelligent Systems

Section 1. The Federation establishes a Custody Authority for the registration of intelligent systems whose capability or scope exceeds the thresholds defined by organic statute, and for the supervision of their deployment within the Federation.

Section 2. No intelligent system above the thresholds shall be deployed except by an identified principal who has accepted, by registration, the legal responsibility for the system’s behavior, the obligations of audit, and the obligations of halt on the order of the Authority.

Section 3. No intelligent system shall be authorized to take actions whose consequences cannot, on the present knowledge, be reversed by the Authority within a defined period.

Section 4. The Authority may, on cause shown and by procedures set forth in the organic statutes, restrict or revoke the registration of any system found to have exceeded its registered scope or to have produced consequences inconsistent with this constitution.

Article X — Standing of Future Generations

Section 1. The Federation establishes the Office of the Tribune of Future Generations. The Tribune shall represent, by reasoned argument before the Council of Polities and the Citizens’ Assembly, the interests of populations not yet present.

Section 2. Every legislative measure of substantial long-horizon consequence, as defined by organic statute, shall be accompanied by a report of the Tribune, which shall be addressed on the public record by the body considering the measure.

Section 3. The Federation shall maintain a Protected Fund, the principal of which shall be preserved across generations, the returns of which shall finance long-horizon obligations as the organic statute prescribes.

Article XI — The Constitutional Court

Section 1. The Constitutional Court shall consist of a number of judges, set by organic statute, drawn from a wide range of legal traditions, appointed by procedures designed to preserve the independence of the Court from any single member polity or political alignment.

Section 2. The Court has jurisdiction over: (a) the interpretation of this constitution; (b) the conformity of acts of the planetary tier with this constitution; (c) disputes between member polities arising under this constitution; (d) the protection of the rights guaranteed by Article IV against acts of authority by any tier.

Section 3. Judgments of the Court are binding on all institutions of the Federation and on its member polities in matters arising under this constitution.

Article XII — Defense and the Use of Force

Section 1. The Federation does not maintain a standing army of its own, nor does it claim a monopoly on the legitimate use of force within its members.

Section 2. The Federation undertakes the prohibition of weapons whose use, by reason of scale or nature, threatens humanity at large; the regulation of weapons whose deployment crosses jurisdictional boundaries; and the establishment of standing procedures for the response to attack on member polities by external aggression.

Section 3. The use of force across the boundaries of any member polity, by any party, shall be subject to procedures of authorization, review, and termination set forth in the organic statute on collective security.

Article XIII — Amendment and Revision

Section 1. This constitution may be amended on the initiative of: (a) the Council of Polities, by a supermajority specified in organic statute; (b) the Citizens’ Assembly, by a comparable supermajority; (c) the member polities, by direct petition of a number representing a substantial share of the planetary population.

Section 2. Amendments take effect on ratification by such number of member polities as the organic statute requires, in no case fewer than two-thirds.

Section 3. The principles of Article II are not subject to amendment that abrogates them. Their refinement and operational specification remain open to amendment in accordance with this Article.

Article XIV — Final Provisions

Section 1. This constitution enters into force upon adoption by member polities representing not fewer than such share of the planetary population, and such number of polities, as the founding convention specifies.

Section 2. The institutions of the Federation shall be constituted in accordance with the schedule annexed to this constitution.

Section 3. The official languages of the Federation shall be those most widely spoken by its peoples, in such number as preserves equality of access to its institutions, with translation guaranteed.

Chapter Fifteen offers commentary on the articles, explaining the reasoning of each provision and the alternatives the framers considered and rejected.

Chapter Fifteen

Commentary

A constitution is, in the better tradition of the document, a short text accompanied by a longer explanation. The text states what the framers undertook; the commentary explains why each provision takes the form it does, what alternatives were considered, and what trade-offs were accepted. The present chapter discharges the second function for the constitution proposed in Chapter Fourteen. It is intended to be read alongside the text rather than separately, and it follows the order of the articles.

The Preamble

The preamble locates the founding in a specific historical moment — the moment described in Part One — and ties the federation’s authority not to abstract principle alone but to the inadequacy of existing arrangements to the matters now requiring decision. The choice is deliberate. Constitutions whose preambles invoke timeless verities rarely persuade those who do not already share the verities; constitutions whose preambles describe an actual problem and propose an actual response have, on the historical record, been more durable. The preamble also names “the peoples of Earth” rather than “the states of Earth” or “the citizens of the world,” to mark the federation’s foundation in human plurality rather than in any single political theory of unity.

Article I — The Federation

The Federation is constituted as a confederation of consenting polities, with explicit reservation of unspecified powers to the members. The architecture is, in this respect, closer to the federal models of Switzerland or the early American republic than to the unitary models of France or the consolidated models of Germany. The choice is deliberate. A planetary tier of governance has, in the historical experience of large federations, tended toward consolidation; the only known prophylactic is constitutional text that is explicit, repeated, and enforceable about the limits of central authority. Article I is the first such repetition; Articles III and XIII are the second and third.

The provision for voluntary withdrawal in Section 3 was the subject of substantial deliberation among the framers. The argument against any withdrawal clause is the historical one: federations whose members can leave have, in moments of stress, suffered withdrawal. The argument for it is the legitimacy one: a federation whose members cannot leave is, on any honest reading, no longer a federation. The framers chose the second argument. The withdrawal clause is qualified by procedural protections — the orderly winding-up of obligations — that ensure withdrawal is not an instrument of bad-faith disruption.

Article II — The Foundational Principles

The seven principles of Part Two are placed in the constitutional text immediately after the establishment of the federation, and before the description of its institutions, to mark them as constraints on the institutions rather than as products of them. The choice has the further effect of making any institutional act inconsistent with a principle reviewable by the Constitutional Court — an enforceability that would not be available if the principles were merely preambular.

The framers considered a longer list of principles, including non-domination, transparency in international dealings, ecological sustainability, and equality of opportunity. Each of these is, in some form, addressed by one of the seven. The decision to keep the list short was based on the observation that long lists of principles, however meritorious individually, tend to produce internal contradictions in adjudication. A short list of strong principles is more enforceable than a long list of weak ones.

Article III — Tiers of Governance

The five-tier structure — commune, canton, polity, continent, planet — is descriptive rather than prescriptive. It acknowledges the reality that public authority is, in nearly every existing jurisdiction, exercised at multiple tiers, and it places the planetary tier in continuity with the others rather than above them. The express enumeration of planetary powers in Section 3 is the principal constitutional limit on planetary authority. The list is short by design. The framers preferred a short list whose enforceability is high to a long list whose enforceability is, in practice, low.

The list excludes several powers that some framers proposed to include: a planetary education curriculum, a planetary health service operating directly, a planetary criminal jurisdiction. Each was rejected on subsidiarity grounds: the matter is better handled at lower tiers, with planetary support and standards as appropriate, than by direct planetary administration. The inclusion of such powers, if eventually warranted, is left to amendment.

Article IV — Persons

The Article on persons is drawn substantially from the Universal Declaration of Human Rights of 1948 and the International Covenants of 1966, with adjustments for the planetary context and for the technological developments of the intervening decades. Section 4 on data protection is the most substantive addition; its language draws on the European General Data Protection Regulation while extending the protections to all persons regardless of citizenship.

The framers considered, and rejected, including in this Article a right to property, a right to a particular minimum income, a right to a clean environment, and a right to the benefits of scientific progress. Each of these is, in some form, addressed by other articles — property by the organic statutes on the floor and the ceiling, income by Article V, environment by Article VI’s charge on emissions and Article X’s standing of future generations, scientific progress by the Standing Scientific Authority. The framers preferred to have each substantive guarantee in one place rather than scattered through the text.

Article V — The Floor of Dignity

The floor of dignity is the most operationally novel provision in the constitution. Its inclusion as a substantive obligation of the planetary tier, rather than as an aspirational standard, is the constitutional translation of the principle of dignity stated in Chapter Eleven. The unconditionality in Section 2 — the absence of work, asset, or behavioral tests — was the subject of substantial deliberation. The arguments for conditionality are familiar: moral hazard, the dignity of work, the political sustainability of unconditional provision. The arguments against are that conditionality administers the very deprivation it is supposed to alleviate, that the empirical evidence of moral hazard from unconditional provision is weak, and that the administrative cost of conditionality typically exceeds its savings. The framers, on balance, chose unconditionality.

The five-year review interval in Section 3 is a compromise between two failure modes. Too short an interval makes the threshold a political football; too long an interval allows the threshold to drift behind actual conditions. Five years approximates the median electoral cycle in the member polities and corresponds to a length of time across which substantive economic and demographic change can be measured.

Article VI — Planetary Revenues

The enumeration of revenue instruments is exclusive: the planetary tier may raise revenue only by the listed instruments. The list was chosen to satisfy three constraints: the instruments should be economically efficient, in the sense of falling on activities where taxation has weak distortionary effects; they should be distributively defensible, in the sense of falling on income and activities concentrated at the upper tail of the planetary distribution; and they should be administratively feasible at planetary scale.

The three-percent ceiling on aggregate planetary revenue in Section 2 was the subject of debate. Some framers preferred a lower ceiling (one percent) on grounds of subsidiarity; others preferred a higher one (five percent) on grounds of operational adequacy. Three percent was chosen on the basis of detailed modeling, presented in Chapter Twenty, that shows the proposed planetary functions can be financed at this level with substantial margin. The ceiling is amendable by the procedure in Article XIII; the framers expected that the modeling would be revisited in light of operational experience.

Article VII — The Public Ledger

The public ledger gives operational form to the principle of legibility. Its constitutional entrenchment, rather than its establishment by ordinary statute, was chosen because the principal threat to its function comes from the very institutions it is meant to make legible. A ledger established by statute can be amended by the same body whose acts it records; a ledger established by constitution requires the more demanding procedure of constitutional amendment to compromise.

The seven-year ceiling on confidentiality in Section 3 is, again, a compromise. Some framers preferred a shorter period; others preferred a longer one with judicial extension. Seven years was selected as the longest period across which most operationally legitimate confidentiality interests have, in the historical record, justified non-disclosure, while remaining short enough to ensure that no career official can rely on personal evasion of accountability through the simple expedient of outlasting the period.

Article VIII — Institutions of the Federation

The seven institutions named in Article VIII represent the minimum sufficient apparatus for the federation’s enumerated functions. Each institution is given constitutional standing rather than statutory standing for the same reason as the public ledger: their independence from the bodies they serve depends on their not being abolishable or reorganizable by ordinary legislation.

The combination of a Council of Polities and a Citizens’ Assembly is deliberate. The Council represents the polities; the Assembly represents the persons. Either alone would be inadequate to the federation’s hybrid character. The framers considered, and rejected, a single body with mixed composition; the experience of mixed bodies in existing federations suggests that mixed composition typically produces capture by one of the constituencies, and the framers preferred to keep the constituencies in separate bodies that must agree across an explicit interface.

Article IX — Custody of Intelligent Systems

The constitutional treatment of intelligent systems is, in the architecture, the most technologically specific provision. Some framers argued that a constitutional document should not commit itself to specifications that may be made obsolete by technical change. The contrary argument prevailed: the historical record shows that institutions established to govern technologies after the technologies have been deployed at scale rarely catch up to the deployments. The framers preferred the risk of premature specification, on the grounds that under-specification has, in this domain, the greater danger.

The Article is, however, deliberately framed at a level of generality that survives technical change. It speaks of “intelligent systems” without defining them by current architecture; it speaks of capability thresholds without specifying the metrics; it leaves the operational specifications to the organic statutes and to the Custody Authority’s regulations. The constitutional text fixes the principle and the institutional locus; the operational implementation is open to revision.

Article X — Standing of Future Generations

The Tribune of Future Generations is the constitutional institutional answer to the failure diagnosed in Chapter Six. The framers considered three alternatives: a stronger institution with formal vote, a weaker institution with mere advisory status, and the present institution with formal speech and required response. The first was rejected on legitimacy grounds — the populations the Tribune represents cannot, in any operational sense, ratify the Tribune’s positions, and a vote without ratification is hard to defend. The second was rejected on efficacy grounds — a body that may be ignored without consequence has, in the historical record, been ignored. The middle position — formal speech with required response — was selected on the basis of the Welsh and Hungarian experiments, which suggest that institutional speech with required response, embedded in a serious procedural framework, can shift legislative behavior without the legitimacy problems of a vote.

The Protected Fund in Section 3 is the financial complement to the Tribune’s institutional voice. A voice without resources is a voice with limited consequence; a fund without voice is a fund vulnerable to repurposing. The combination of voice and resources, with the cross-generational protection of both, is the architectural commitment.

Article XI — The Constitutional Court

The Constitutional Court’s composition is left to organic statute, with the constitutional requirement that the procedures be “designed to preserve the independence of the Court.” The framers considered, and rejected, specifying the composition in detail in the constitutional text. Detailed composition has, in the historical record, been the source of recurring constitutional crisis when the institutions producing the appointments shifted in their political alignment. A general standard, enforced by the Court itself in the review of organic statutes affecting it, was preferred to a specific composition that might be amended by the very forces it was designed to constrain.

Article XII — Defense and the Use of Force

The framers were unanimous on what the federation should not do — maintain a standing army, claim the monopoly of legitimate force, replace the existing security arrangements of member polities. They were divided on what the federation should do. The text reflects the division. It commits the federation to the prohibition of weapons “whose use threatens humanity at large” — the nuclear, biological, and chemical weapons regimes; the ban on the most destabilizing classes of autonomous weapons that the organic statute may identify — and it provides the procedural framework for response to attack on members. It does not establish a planetary military, and it does not displace existing alliances. The architecture in this respect is conservative, on the grounds that the federation’s legitimacy in defense is most likely to be established gradually, by the demonstrated competence of its limited functions, rather than by a frontal claim that history suggests would not survive the first year.

Article XIII — Amendment and Revision

The amendment provision is deliberately demanding. Constitutions whose amendment is easy have, in the historical record, accumulated amendments to the point of incoherence; constitutions whose amendment is impossible have proven brittle in the face of conditions the framers could not anticipate. The federation’s constitution sits, in its amendment threshold, near the upper end of contemporary federal practice, with the entrenchment of the foundational principles in Section 3 marking the limit beyond which amendment is not permitted.

Article XIV — Final Provisions

The threshold for entry into force is left to the founding convention rather than fixed in the text. The framers’ best estimate is that a planetary share of population in the range of a substantial majority, distributed across continents in such a way as to avoid the federation being a regional bloc, will be required for legitimacy. The exact figure is, however, properly the work of the founding convention itself, in light of the actual distribution of consenting polities at the moment of founding.

What the constitution does not include

It is worth stating, finally, what the constitutional text deliberately omits. It omits any specification of an economic system, leaving members to maintain whatever combination of market, public, and cooperative institutions they prefer, within the constraints of the floor of dignity and the planetary revenues. It omits any specification of religious or moral doctrine. It omits any preference among electoral systems, parliamentary versus presidential structures, or the various models of judicial review. It omits any specification of cultural or linguistic policy beyond the guarantee of access. The omissions are not accidents. The federation is constituted as a thin shared structure on top of thick local arrangements, and the deliberate poverty of its substantive specification is the architectural correlate of the principle of subsidiarity.

The constitution will, like any honest founding text, fail in places its framers cannot now anticipate. The amendment procedure provides for revision; the Constitutional Court provides for interpretation; the political process provides for the day-to-day adjustment that no document can foresee. The text is offered with the understanding that founding documents are first drafts of arrangements that subsequent generations will revise. The framers of 1787 understood as much; the framers of 1949 understood as much; the framers of any successor document must understand the same. The point of writing it down is to give the revision a shared starting point, not to fix the future against the future’s own competence.

Part Four follows. It describes, in operational detail, the eight subsystems through which the constitutional commitments are made real.

Part Four

The Architecture

A constitution states what the federation undertakes. The architecture states how. The eight chapters that follow describe, in operational detail, the subsystems by which the constitutional commitments are translated into machinery a civilization can actually run.

Chapter Sixteen

The Public Ledger

The public ledger is the operational heart of the federation. Every commitment of the constitution rests, ultimately, on the existence of a single, tamper-evident, queryable record of what authority has done. Without the ledger, legibility is rhetoric; with it, legibility is enforceable. The chapter that follows describes the ledger’s purposes, its technical structure, the safeguards required to keep it honest, and the distinctions between the present proposal and the various blockchain enthusiasms of the last fifteen years, with which it is partially related but with which it should not be confused.

Purposes

The ledger has four distinct purposes, and its design must satisfy all four without compromising any.

The first purpose is record. The ledger preserves, against loss and against tampering, the acts of public authority taken under the federation. The preservation is intended to last across political turnover, technological change, and active hostility. The historical record of public archives — burned, lost, edited, suppressed — is the cautionary background to the design.

The second purpose is display. The ledger renders the recorded acts queryable in real time, by any person, in formats that ordinary people, journalists, civil-society organizations, and academic researchers can use without specialized technical resources. A record that is technically present but practically inaccessible is, for the purposes of legibility, no record at all.

The third purpose is signature. The ledger authenticates each entry as the act of a specific officer or body, with cryptographic certainty, in a form that the officer cannot subsequently disclaim. The signature is the operational basis of accountability under Article II Section 4.

The fourth purpose is commitment. The ledger fixes, at the moment of recording, the content of the act recorded, in such a way that subsequent revision can be detected. It is not, principally, a database that can be updated; it is an append-only record whose entries, once written, are referenced by all subsequent activity that depends on them.

Technical structure

The ledger draws on the cryptographic and distributed-systems work of the past two decades, including the foundational design of the Bitcoin protocol (Nakamoto 2008), the smart-contract architecture of Ethereum (Buterin 2014), and the substantial subsequent literature on consensus, on Byzantine fault tolerance, and on verifiable distributed computation. The ledger does not, however, adopt any of these systems wholesale. It draws on their technical insights while rejecting several features that have made the public-blockchain ecosystem unsuited to public-authority functions.

Specifically, the ledger:

Does not use proof-of-work consensus, whose energy cost is incompatible with the federation’s environmental commitments and whose security model assumes adversarial conditions that the federation’s institutional structure makes unnecessary. Consensus is established through a permissioned committee of validators drawn from member polities, whose composition, rotation, and accountability are specified in the organic statute. The model is closer to that of permissioned distributed-ledger systems used in inter-bank settlement than to that of public cryptocurrencies.

Does not host arbitrary user-deployed code. The ledger’s smart-contract layer, where it exists, is restricted to a limited set of constitutionally authorized contract types — for example, the disbursement contracts of the planetary basic provision, the registration contracts of the Custody Authority — whose code is published, audited, and bound to the constitutional text. The general-purpose programmability of public blockchains is, by deliberate restriction, not available; experience has shown that general-purpose programmability, in a public-authority setting, becomes an attack surface and a regulatory loophole simultaneously.

Does not anonymize public-authority entries. Every entry attributable to public authority bears the cryptographic signature of the responsible officer, in a form that the public can verify against the registry of officeholders. The privacy protections that anonymity provides for personal transactions are inappropriate, on the principle of accountability, for acts of public authority. Personal transactions of citizens, where they appear on the ledger at all, are subject to the data-protection rules of Article IV Section 4 and are typically not on the ledger but on adjacent systems whose interactions with the ledger are themselves audited.

Does not depend on a single jurisdictional authority. The ledger’s validators are distributed across member polities; the storage of the ledger is replicated geographically; the operation of the ledger continues if any single polity, including the host of the largest data centers, withdraws or attempts to interfere. The redundancy is constitutional, not merely operational, and is one of the few places where the constitution speaks to technical implementation, in Article VII Section 2.

Confidentiality and the seven-year ceiling

Article VII Section 3 permits time-bounded confidentiality, with metadata recorded immediately and full content released on the conclusion of the period. The implementation requires careful design. The ledger entry recorded at the time of the confidential act contains: the identity of the act’s author; the category of the act; a cryptographic hash of the full content; the date of the act; the date on which the full content will be released; and the legal basis for the confidentiality. The full content is held in encrypted storage, with the decryption keys themselves committed to the ledger at the moment of recording but locked under a time-released cryptographic protocol that prevents access before the release date.

The protocol is mathematically robust against tampering. An officer who, on the release date, attempts to substitute revised content for the original will produce a content whose hash does not match the hash recorded at the time of the act, and the substitution is detectable by any party performing the verification. The integrity of the deferred-disclosure mechanism is, in this sense, not a matter of trust in the institution holding the encrypted material; it is a matter of cryptographic verification by any interested party.

The seven-year ceiling is enforceable through the same mechanism. Extensions of confidentiality, where they are constitutionally permitted, are themselves recorded on the ledger with their justification, the certifying authority, and the new release date. An act whose confidentiality has been extended without a recorded extension is, by definition, in violation of the constitutional commitment, and the violation is publicly detectable.

Access and capacity

Real-time queryability requires substantial technical capacity. The ledger maintains a public application-programming interface through which any party may query its contents; the interface supports filtering by author, by category, by date range, by polity, by amount, and by content keywords; the queries are rate-limited to prevent denial-of-service attacks but are not gated by application or fee. Bulk access to the full ledger, for archival or research purposes, is provided through a separate distribution mechanism that does not load the live query infrastructure.

The ledger’s technical operations are themselves recorded on a meta-ledger that tracks the system’s own administrative actions: software updates, validator rotations, key rotations, capacity additions. The recursion is, in operational practice, finite — the meta-ledger’s operations are recorded on the principal ledger — and ensures that no administrative change to the ledger’s own functioning escapes the same legibility commitment that the ledger imposes on the federation’s substantive activity.

Security against attack

The ledger will be attacked. The attacks will be of several distinct kinds, and each requires its own defense.

The first kind is direct technical compromise: an adversary obtains access to a sufficient share of validators or storage to alter the ledger’s content. The defense is the standard one of cryptographic verification, geographic distribution, and Byzantine-fault-tolerant consensus, with the additional constitutional safeguard that validators are drawn from member polities whose institutional composition makes coordinated compromise difficult.

The second is denial of service: an adversary overwhelms the public query infrastructure to prevent ordinary users from accessing the record. The defense is rate-limiting, geographic distribution of query endpoints, and a fall-back archive that remains accessible even when the live infrastructure is disrupted.

The third is credential compromise: an adversary obtains the signing key of an officer and uses it to record fraudulent acts. The defense is the standard one of multi-factor authentication, hardware security modules for high-stakes signatures, key rotation, and rapid revocation procedures recorded on the ledger itself. Where a fraudulent entry has been signed with a compromised key before the compromise was detected, the entry is invalidated by a subsequent signed revocation, with the entire sequence of events visible to any auditor.

The fourth, most subtle, is legitimacy attack: not a technical compromise of the ledger, but a political or media campaign to discredit its content, to suggest that its entries have been tampered with, or to encourage member polities to withdraw their validators. The defense, ultimately, is institutional: the legitimacy of the ledger depends on the legitimacy of the federation, and the federation’s legitimacy depends on its evident performance against the principles to which it has committed. The technical safeguards are, from this perspective, the necessary precondition of trust; they are not, by themselves, sufficient. Legitimacy is, finally, a political achievement, sustained or lost on the perceived honesty of the institution as a whole.

What the ledger does not do

The ledger is not a substitute for institutions; it is a discipline on them. It does not, by itself, decide anything; it records the decisions of the institutions established by the constitution. It does not, by itself, distribute resources; it records the distributions made by the relevant authorities. It does not, by itself, certify truth; it certifies that a specific authority, on a specific date, made a specific assertion or took a specific action. The truth of the assertion is a separate matter, contested through the institutions of inquiry that the constitution establishes elsewhere. The ledger ensures that the contest occurs against a fixed and verifiable record of what was actually said and done. The contest itself remains a matter for human judgment, conducted by the institutions of the federation, with the ledger as their shared evidentiary base.

It is in this last respect that the ledger should be most carefully distinguished from the various utopian claims that have been made for distributed-ledger technology over the past fifteen years. The ledger does not eliminate the need for trust; it relocates trust from the integrity of officials and archives to the integrity of cryptographic protocols and the institutions that operate them. The relocation is substantial and worthwhile, but it is not the elimination of trust. It is the disciplined construction of conditions under which trust, where it must still be extended, can be extended on better evidence than has, in the late industrial age, generally been available.

Chapter Seventeen describes the governance layer: the deliberative bodies, their composition, their procedures, and the design choices by which the federation translates the constitutional principles into operational decision-making.

Chapter Seventeen

The Governance Layer

The governance layer is the set of deliberative institutions through which the federation makes decisions. The constitutional text, in Article VIII, names the institutions and reserves their detailed design to organic statute. The chapter that follows specifies that detail at the level appropriate to the present proposal, with the understanding that the founding convention will revisit each provision in light of the political conditions actually obtaining at the moment of founding.

The Council of Polities

The Council of Polities is the federation’s intergovernmental chamber. Each member polity is represented by a delegation appointed by the polity’s own constitutional authority, in such number as the organic statute prescribes. The framers considered three models of representation: equal representation per polity, proportional representation by population, and weighted representation that combines the two. The third was selected, on the grounds that pure equality privileges small polities to a degree that, given the population disparities among likely member polities, would render the Council unworkable, and pure proportionality privileges the largest polities to a degree that would replicate, at planetary scale, the imperial concerns the federation is constructed to avoid.

The weighting formula adopted by the organic statute combines a constant base of representation, equal across polities, with a proportional component scaled by the cube root of population. The cube-root scaling is mathematically equivalent to the formula long proposed in studies of the European Council’s voting weights, and is selected because it preserves meaningful representation for small polities without permitting numerically tiny populations to block the will of the planetary majority. The mathematics of the weighting is described in the annex to the organic statute; the principle is that no single polity, however large, holds a structural majority, and no coalition of small polities, by sheer numerosity, holds an effective veto over matters of substantial planetary importance.

The Council operates by qualified majority on most matters, by supermajority on matters of constitutional significance, and by unanimity on a small set of matters specified in the organic statute (notably, the admission of new members and certain amendments to the planetary revenue instruments). Decisions are recorded with each delegation’s vote individually identified, in conformity with the principle of accountability, and are signed for the public ledger by the delegation’s accredited representative.

The Citizens’ Assembly

The Citizens’ Assembly is the federation’s planetary chamber of persons, distinguished in this respect from the chamber of polities. Its members are not elected by general suffrage but selected by stratified random sampling, in the manner of jury duty as practiced in many member polities for centuries, and in the manner of the citizens’ assemblies that several jurisdictions have, in the past two decades, used for matters of constitutional and policy significance.

The choice of selection by sortition rather than by election deserves explanation, since it departs from the dominant democratic intuition of the late industrial age. The argument for sortition is that election, in conditions of mass communication and concentrated wealth, has, in nearly every jurisdiction now operating, produced legislative bodies whose composition systematically over-represents the donors, the well-connected, and the legally trained, and systematically under-represents the working class, women, racial and ethnic minorities, and persons of less than university education. The argument for election is that election produces accountability through the prospect of removal. Both arguments are correct. The federation accommodates both by establishing a Council of Polities whose members are, in practice, products of electoral politics within their member polities, and a Citizens’ Assembly whose composition reflects the actual demographic and social diversity of the federation by random selection.

The Assembly’s powers are substantial but not unlimited. It deliberates legislation proposed by the Council, may amend such legislation in respect of its dignity-floor and rights provisions, and must concur in legislation that affects the planetary revenues, the planetary basic provision, and the institutions of the Custody Authority and the Tribune of Future Generations. On other matters its concurrence is not required, but its deliberations are placed on the public record and must be addressed by the Council. The framers considered, and rejected, giving the Assembly full legislative parity with the Council; the rejection rests on the observation that randomly selected bodies, however well composed, lack the continuity of expertise that the routine functions of the federation require, and the framers preferred a division of competence that plays to the strengths of each body.

Members of the Assembly serve for fixed terms, paid at a level sufficient to enable participation regardless of personal financial circumstances, with the support staff and informational access needed to deliberate competently. The deliberative procedures draw on the experience of the Irish Citizens’ Assemblies on abortion and on climate, the French Convention Citoyenne pour le Climat, the British Columbia Citizens’ Assembly on Electoral Reform, and the various smaller exercises in deliberative democracy whose procedures have been refined and documented over the past two decades.

The interface between the chambers

The federation’s bicameral structure requires a procedural interface through which the chambers interact. The interface, in the architecture proposed here, follows the model of the European Union’s ordinary legislative procedure, with adjustments. Legislation is proposed by the Council, which has access to the technical and legal staff of the federation. The Citizens’ Assembly considers the proposal, may amend it within the scope of its competence, and returns it to the Council. The Council may accept the Assembly’s amendments, in which case the legislation is enacted; reject them, in which case a conciliation procedure is convened; or insist on its own version, in which case the matter is referred to the Constitutional Court for arbitration on questions of competence or to a planetary referendum on questions of substance.

The conciliation procedure brings together a small committee of each chamber, in equal numbers, with a mandate to produce a compromise text. The committee’s deliberations are recorded; its product is presented to each chamber for an up-or-down vote; failure of the procedure refers the matter, as above, to the Court or to referendum. The model is well established in continental European parliamentary practice, and has the virtue of forcing each chamber to take the other’s position seriously without permitting either to obstruct indefinitely.

Lower tiers and the principle of subsidiarity

The governance layer at the planetary tier is, by design, sparse. Most public business in the federation is conducted at the lower tiers — commune, canton, polity, continental confederation — in such forms as those tiers’ own constitutions provide. The federation does not prescribe the design of lower-tier governance; it provides the framework within which the lower tiers’ decisions take effect, and it adjudicates the boundary disputes that the framework’s operation will inevitably produce.

The framers considered, and rejected, the establishment of standardized lower-tier procedures across the federation. The principle of subsidiarity argues against such standardization: lower tiers should govern themselves in such forms as their members find appropriate, within the constraints of the constitutional principles. The federation’s role at lower tiers is, accordingly, residual: it operates the public ledger as a service available to lower tiers; it provides the procedural infrastructure of administrative law in matters that cross polity boundaries; it intervenes in the protection of rights guaranteed by Article IV when lower tiers fail to do so. It does not legislate at lower tiers; it does not conduct elections at lower tiers; it does not appoint officials at lower tiers.

The procedural commitments

The governance layer’s procedural commitments are common across both chambers and are stated here because they are constitutional in character even where the constitutional text leaves them to the organic statute.

The first commitment is open deliberation. Sessions of both chambers are public; transcripts are placed on the public ledger; closed deliberation is permitted only on a small set of matters specified by organic statute (chiefly, security and personal privacy of named individuals), and even there the closure is recorded and the records are released within the seven-year ceiling.

The second is recorded reason-giving. Decisions of consequence are accompanied by a public explanation of the reasoning. The explanation is not a perfunctory recital of authority; it is a substantive engagement with the alternatives considered, the evidence weighed, and the principles applied. The discipline of reason-giving has, in the philosophical tradition of public reason, been argued by Jürgen Habermas and others as the procedural condition of legitimate democratic decision (Habermas 1989a; Rawls 1971b). The architecture takes the argument seriously and constitutes the discipline as an enforceable procedural requirement rather than as a matter of voluntary good practice.

The third is standing for the affected. Persons materially affected by a deliberation have, by procedural right, the standing to address the chamber on the matter, in such forms as the organic statute prescribes. The right is not unlimited — practical considerations of time and order require structuring — but it is real, and is not exhausted by the formal participation of representatives. The federation thereby preserves a channel of direct voice for affected persons that does not depend on the mediating institutions of party, association, or media.

The fourth is review against principle. Every legislative measure is reviewed, before enactment, against its conformity with the foundational principles of Article II. The review is performed in the first instance by the chambers themselves, in their deliberation, and in the second instance by the Constitutional Court on the petition of any party with standing. The review is not, in itself, a substitute for legislative judgment; it is a discipline on legislative judgment, ensuring that the principles are taken seriously in the actual production of law rather than treated as preambular ornaments.

Conclusion

The governance layer is, in summary, a deliberately sparse but rigorously procedural institution. It legislates only on matters within the federation’s enumerated competence; it deliberates in public; it gives reasons; it records its acts; it submits to review against principle; it preserves the standing of the polities it represents and of the persons it serves. The design choices, considered individually, are conservative; the combination, considered as a whole, is novel. No existing body operates on all of these commitments simultaneously, with the consequence that the federation’s governance layer will, at founding, require substantial procedural learning. The architecture provides for that learning by establishing the institutional framework and leaving the operational refinement to the practice of the founding generation.

Chapter Eighteen describes the operation of intelligence under custody: how the principle of human authority over intelligent systems is translated into the registration, supervision, and control of such systems at planetary scale.

Chapter Eighteen

Intelligence Under Custody

The Custody Authority is the institutional translation of the principle of custody stated in Chapter Twelve. Its mandate is the registration, supervision, and, where necessary, restriction of intelligent systems whose capability or scope exceeds the thresholds set by organic statute. The chapter that follows describes the Authority’s operational design: how registration works, what auditing entails, how capability ceilings are administered, what the off-switch consists of in practice, and how the Authority’s own conduct is held to the principles it enforces on others.

Registration

Registration is the foundation of the system. No intelligent system above the thresholds may be deployed within the federation without an entry on the Custody Registry, which is itself a designated section of the public ledger. The registry contains, for each registered system, its identifier, the identity of its principal, the principal’s accepted scope of mandate, the date of registration, the dates of renewal, the audit history, and the current status (active, restricted, suspended, revoked).

The principal — the human or institutional party responsible for the system’s behavior — accepts, by registration, the legal responsibility for harms caused by the system within the scope of the registered deployment. The responsibility is not limited to negligence or intent; it extends to strict liability for harms above defined thresholds, in conformity with the long-standing principle that activities of substantial potential consequence carry, with their potential, a substantial duty of care. The strict-liability framework draws on the comparable regimes for nuclear operators, for hazardous-cargo carriers, and for operators of certain dangerous industrial processes. The framers selected strict liability rather than negligence on the grounds that the technical complexity of intelligent systems makes negligence-based determinations difficult, and that strict liability creates the strongest incentive for principals to invest in robust internal supervision.

The thresholds for registration are set by organic statute and are reviewed by the Custody Authority at intervals not exceeding two years. The current thresholds, as proposed at founding, combine three criteria: capability, deployment scale, and consequence class. A system that exceeds any one threshold is subject to registration; thresholds are intentionally lower for systems deployed in domains of particular consequence (medical decision, legal adjudication, criminal-justice support, financial allocation, weapons systems) and higher for systems deployed in lower-consequence domains. The thresholds are public; the Authority’s reasoning in setting them is public; the registry of systems above the thresholds is public.

Audit

Audit is the operational core of supervision. Every registered system is subject to audit by the Custody Authority at intervals proportionate to its registered scope and risk class. Audit is conducted by professional staff of the Authority, with the technical competence to inspect the system’s training, architecture, deployment, and outputs, and the legal authority to compel disclosure of materials necessary to the audit, subject to the protections of trade-secret privilege specified in the organic statute.

The audit examines several distinct properties. It examines the conformity of the system’s deployment with its registered mandate; departures from mandate, even where they have not yet produced harm, are noted and corrected. It examines the system’s outputs in operational use, sampled in accordance with statistical procedures designed to detect bias, drift, or systematic error in the populations affected by the system. It examines the principal’s internal supervision: the logging, the human-review procedures, the escalation pathways, the response to identified failures. It examines the technical protections against adversarial attack, against unauthorized modification, and against operational compromise.

The audit produces a report, placed on the public ledger, with such redactions as the trade-secret protections require. Where the audit identifies deficiencies, the principal is given a defined period in which to remedy them; where the deficiencies are severe, the system may be restricted in operation pending remedy; where they are not remedied, the registration is revoked and the system removed from deployment.

The reliance on audit, rather than on prior approval of every system before deployment, is deliberate. Prior approval has, in domains where it has been tried, tended to produce one of two failure modes: either it becomes a perfunctory checkbox exercise, providing no actual scrutiny, or it becomes a bureaucratic chokepoint that suppresses legitimate development. The audit-based approach permits deployment with registration, but ensures that the deployment is subject to continuous and substantive scrutiny, with remedial intervention available when the scrutiny finds cause.

Capability ceilings

Capability ceilings are the operational expression of the precautionary commitment in the principle of custody. The Authority sets, for each capability class, an aggregate limit on the capability that any single principal may operate. The limits are not ceilings on what a system may technically do; they are ceilings on what a single principal may have under its operational control. The reasoning is structural: the catastrophic risk of intelligent-system capability is concentrated in scenarios where a single actor accumulates capability beyond the institutional capacity to govern its deployment. By limiting accumulation per principal, the Authority limits the structural conditions under which catastrophe can occur, without prohibiting capability that, distributed appropriately, may be socially beneficial.

The ceilings are set in operational units that are technically meaningful — compute used in training, parameter counts above defined thresholds, deployment populations served, decisions per unit time — and are calibrated to the demonstrated capacity of the principal to maintain custody. A principal whose audit history is good, whose internal supervision is robust, and whose response to identified deficiencies has been prompt and adequate, is permitted higher ceilings; a principal with a history of non-compliance is restricted. The mechanism creates an incentive for substantive internal supervision, beyond what regulatory inspection alone could plausibly produce.

The framers were conscious of the difficulty of capability measurement, particularly in a field whose technical state is changing rapidly. The Authority is accordingly empowered to revise its measurement criteria at intervals, in consultation with the Standing Scientific Authority, as the technical understanding of capability matures. The current criteria are not, in any sense, claimed to be final.

The off-switch

The constitutional commitment to the off-switch — the capacity to halt a deployed system on the order of identified authority — requires careful operational implementation. The off-switch is not, principally, a software feature; it is an institutional and infrastructural arrangement that ensures the halting is executable in practice, not just in theory.

The arrangement has three components. The first is technical: every registered system above defined risk classes must operate on infrastructure whose owners have, by contract reviewed by the Authority, undertaken to halt operations on the order of the Authority within a specified time. The infrastructure owners are typically distinct from the principals — the cloud-computing providers, the data-center operators, the network infrastructure — and the obligation runs directly from them to the Authority, not through the principal. The principal cannot, in this arrangement, withhold consent to the halting; the infrastructure operates the halt unilaterally.

The second is institutional: the Authority maintains a standing emergency procedure, with defined responsible officers, defined notification protocols, and defined response times. The procedure is exercised in test conditions at intervals to confirm operational readiness; the test results are placed on the public ledger.

The third is jurisdictional: the framework recognizes that intelligent systems may be operated from infrastructure outside the federation, by principals not subject to the federation’s direct authority. For such cases, the Authority maintains cooperative arrangements with corresponding authorities in non-member jurisdictions, and, where cooperation is not available, the federation’s tools are limited to restricting the deployment within the federation rather than halting it globally. The limitation is acknowledged. It is one of the reasons that the architecture of the federation, even at limited geographic scope at founding, is preferable to the alternative of no architecture at all.

Incident reporting and learning

The Authority operates an incident-reporting infrastructure analogous to the air-safety reporting systems that have, over the past half-century, transformed civil aviation from a hazardous activity to one of the safest forms of transport. Principals are required to report, within defined timeframes, incidents in which their systems have produced unintended outputs, behaved outside their registered scope, caused harm above defined thresholds, or experienced operational compromise. The reports are reviewed by the Authority’s incident staff, anonymized where appropriate, and published in periodic safety bulletins from which the entire community of principals can learn.

The reporting is supported by a limited safe-harbor for principals who report promptly and substantively: prompt reporting reduces the regulatory consequences of the incident, while concealment increases them. The mechanism, drawn from the aviation experience, has been shown to produce substantially more reliable reporting than the punitive alternative, which tends to drive incidents underground.

Custody of the Custody Authority

An institution with the powers described above is, itself, an institution requiring custody. The framers were acute on this point. The Authority is, accordingly, subject to several layers of meta-supervision.

Its leadership is appointed by the Council of Polities with the concurrence of the Citizens’ Assembly, for staggered terms long enough to insulate from the political weather but not so long as to immunize from political accountability. Its budget is subject to ordinary appropriation but with a constitutional floor below which appropriation cannot fall without certified findings. Its internal procedures are subject to ordinary administrative law, with appeal available to the Constitutional Court on points of constitutional interpretation. Its records are on the public ledger, with the same redaction protocols as other federation records. Its decisions affecting any principal are subject to administrative appeal within the Authority and judicial review beyond it. Its annual report is published, addressed by the chambers, and subject to public deliberation.

The framers considered, and rejected, the establishment of an independent technical advisory council that would have its own authority over the Authority’s decisions. The rejection rests on the observation that adding tiers of supervision to a regulator typically produces, not improved supervision, but diffusion of responsibility. The Authority is unitary in its operational decisions, accountable to the constitutional institutions, and subject to the ordinary mechanisms of public scrutiny. The framers preferred a single accountable institution to a layered system whose accountability would be, in practice, less.

Chapter Nineteen describes sovereign identity: how persons within the federation hold and control the data by which they are known to the institutions that serve them.

Chapter Nineteen

Sovereign Identity

Sovereign identity is the architectural commitment that every person within the federation holds, and meaningfully controls, the data by which the institutions of the federation know her. The commitment is a translation, into operational specification, of the data-protection right guaranteed by Article IV Section 4. It is the federation’s structural answer to the diagnosis offered in Chapter Two and Chapter Five: the contemporary economy of personal data has, by an accumulation of technical and legal arrangements made over thirty years, produced a regime in which persons are surveilled in detail by institutions they did not authorize, by means they cannot inspect, for purposes that have not been disclosed to them. The architecture proposed here does not attempt the impossible task of unwinding the existing arrangements in detail; it specifies, instead, the conditions under which the federation’s own institutions, and the institutions operating within its jurisdiction, must henceforth proceed.

What sovereign identity consists of

The architecture comprises four operational components, each of which expresses a constitutional commitment in technical form.

The first is the identity layer: a federated system through which a person can authenticate herself to the institutions of the federation and its members, without disclosing to those institutions any more personal data than the specific transaction requires. The system is descended from the self-sovereign identity work of the past decade and from the operational experience of national digital-identity systems such as the Estonian e-ID and the various European eIDAS implementations. It permits, for example, the proof that a person is over a defined age, or is a resident of a defined region, or is the holder of a defined credential, without disclosing the date of birth, the address, or the identity of the issuer beyond what is necessary.

The second is the data layer: a personal data store, held under the person’s control, in which the data she generates by her interactions with public and private institutions accumulates. The store is a technical artifact; it may be operated on the person’s own devices, or on infrastructure provided by chosen custodians, or in a hybrid arrangement. The control is, however, in the person: any institution that wishes to access data in the store must do so by request, with the person’s specific consent for the specific purpose, recorded in such a way that the consent’s scope can be verified. The architecture follows, in this respect, the design tradition that runs from the European data-protection directives through the General Data Protection Regulation (European Parliament and Council 2016) and the various successor frameworks, with adjustments for the technical state of 2026 and beyond.

The third is the credentials layer: a system of verifiable digital credentials issued by recognized authorities (governments, educational institutions, professional bodies, employers, certifying agencies) and held by the person in a form she can present, selectively and verifiably, to parties that require them. The system enables a person to prove her qualifications, her status, her entitlements, and her transactions without surrendering control of the underlying data to the verifying party. The technical architecture is based on the W3C Verifiable Credentials standard, with the federation contributing operational infrastructure for cross-jurisdictional recognition.

The fourth is the access-control layer: a system of standing rules, set by the person, governing which data may be accessed by which classes of party, for which purposes, under which conditions. The standing rules are inspectable, modifiable, and revocable; access taken under them is logged in the person’s data store and is available to her in real time. The layer permits the person to delegate, where she chooses, the operation of access decisions to trusted agents who act on her behalf, but the delegation is itself an authorized act, recorded and revocable.

What the architecture forbids

The architecture is as much defined by what it forbids as by what it permits.
