It forbids the routine accumulation of personal data by public or private institutions in the absence of specific authorization for the specific purpose. The “routine accumulation” against which the prohibition is directed is the practice, dominant in the surveillance-capitalist economy described in Chapter Five, of collecting personal data as a default consequence of any digital interaction, regardless of whether the data is necessary to the transaction the person actually intended.

It forbids the secondary trading of personal data: the practice by which data collected for one purpose is, without specific renewed consent, sold or transferred to parties for use in other purposes. The prohibition is not absolute — anonymized aggregate data may be used for research and policy under the procedures specified in the organic statute — but it bars the practices that have made personal-data brokerage a substantial economic sector in nearly every existing jurisdiction.

It forbids the use of personal data, against the person’s interests, in decisions that materially affect the person, without the person’s awareness of the data’s role and her opportunity to contest its accuracy and interpretation. The prohibition is the constitutional formalization of the right, increasingly recognized in administrative law across jurisdictions, to know that a decision affecting one is being made on the basis of automated processing, and to have the decision reviewed by a human party with the authority to set it aside.

It forbids the surveillance of the person by public authority in the absence of legal authorization specific to the surveillance, narrowly drawn, time-limited, and subject to oversight. The prohibition does not foreclose all surveillance — investigative authority, where it serves legitimate public purposes, is preserved — but it bars the open-ended, mass, or routine surveillance that the technical capacity of the late industrial age has, in many jurisdictions, made operationally trivial.

The economic implications

The architecture has substantial economic implications. The contemporary economy of personal data, in its dominant form, depends on the routine accumulation that the architecture forbids. Enterprises whose business models rest on that accumulation will, in the federation’s jurisdiction, require restructuring. The framers were conscious of the implication and accept it. The economic value of personal data, in the architecture proposed here, is captured in part by the persons whose data it is, through the mechanisms of consent, recording, and selective disclosure that the architecture provides; the residual value is available to enterprises that operate on the data with the person’s authorization, on terms the person has accepted.

The transition to the new arrangement is not, however, abrupt. The organic statute provides for a multi-year phasing, during which existing institutions adapt their practices, with technical support from the federation’s identity infrastructure and procedural protections against retaliatory restructuring of services to the disadvantage of persons who exercise their rights. The framers expected, and observed in the analogous transitions following earlier data-protection reforms, that the principal economic adjustment is in the structure of business models rather than in the volume of legitimate economic activity, and that the legitimate activity continues, on different and more honest terms, after the transition is complete.

Cross-border interoperability

Personal data flows across borders, and the architecture must accommodate the flows without abandoning its substantive commitments. The federation operates a recognition framework under which the data-protection regimes of non-member jurisdictions may be assessed and, where they meet equivalent standards, accorded reciprocal recognition. Where they do not, transfers to the non-equivalent jurisdiction are subject to additional protections — contractual safeguards, technical measures such as encryption that limits access on receipt, or, where necessary, prohibition. The framework is descended from the European Union’s adequacy-decision regime, with such adjustments as the planetary scope and the operational experience of the past two decades have made advisable.

The federation’s own infrastructure operates under the strictest level of the framework regardless of where the user accesses it from: a person interacting with the federation’s institutions enjoys the data-protection commitments specified by Article IV, regardless of the jurisdiction in which the interaction occurs and regardless of the person’s own citizenship status.

The technical and the political

The architecture of sovereign identity is, in its technical aspects, demanding. The cryptographic protocols, the key-management infrastructure, the credential-issuance systems, the access-control engines, and the audit infrastructure are substantial engineering undertakings. The framers were conscious that engineering, however competent, can implement an architecture whose political commitments have been compromised; they were equally conscious that strong political commitments can be defeated by weak engineering. The two domains require equal seriousness.

The Authority responsible for the operation of the federation’s identity infrastructure is, in the organic statute, an institution distinct from the Custody Authority but governed on similar principles: independence from the bodies it serves, public reporting, audit, judicial review of substantive decisions, and the same constitutional limits on surveillance and accumulation that the architecture imposes on other parties. The Authority does not, in particular, accumulate the data flows that pass through its infrastructure beyond what is required for operational integrity, and it does not provide such data to any other authority in the absence of process equivalent to that required for any other party.

The combination of strong political commitments, competent engineering, and rigorous procedural protection is the architectural condition under which the data-protection right of Article IV can become, in practice, what the constitutional text declares it to be. None of the components is sufficient alone. All three, together, can produce a regime in which the person’s relation to the institutions that know her is, for the first time at planetary scale, on terms the person has been given the means to negotiate.

Chapter Twenty describes the economic instruments through which the federation translates the principle of dignity into a planetary basic provision and the broader distributive commitments of the architecture into operational practice.

Chapter Twenty

Economic Instruments

The federation’s economic architecture is the operational answer to the diagnoses offered in Chapters Three and Four. It is the set of instruments through which the principle of dignity is translated into the floor of unconditional provision, the principle of accountability is translated into the discipline of taxation and disbursement, the principle of longevity is translated into the protection of intergenerational capital, and the principle of subsidiarity is translated into a division of fiscal labor among the tiers of governance. The chapter that follows describes the principal instruments and the modeling that supports their proposed scale.

The planetary basic provision

The provision is the federation’s primary distributive instrument. Its design has been discussed at the level of principle in Chapter Eleven; the operational specification is the work of the present chapter.

The provision delivers, to every person within the federation whose income falls below the threshold defined by the organic statute on the floor, the sum required to bring the person to the threshold. The delivery is in such mixed form — cash transfer, service provision, infrastructural investment — as the local conditions of the recipient region require. The threshold is calibrated to local cost-of-living indicators, with substantive minimums that no jurisdiction may set below.

The administrative architecture is layered. The threshold is set, and the funding is provided, at the planetary tier. The eligibility determination is performed at the polity tier, on the basis of data the person has authorized for the purpose, with appeal available to the polity’s administrative law and beyond it to the federation’s institutions. The actual delivery is performed by such combination of public and private providers as the polity has organized, with the federation’s role limited to ensuring that the delivery happens and that the threshold is met.

The framers considered the alternative of direct delivery by the federation, bypassing the polity tier. They rejected it, on subsidiarity grounds: direct delivery requires the federation to maintain a planetary administrative apparatus comparable in scale to the welfare ministries of all member polities combined, with the consequent risks of consolidation. The two-tier architecture preserves local administrative variety while ensuring that the substantive commitment is met universally.

The cost of the provision, modeled at the levels currently estimated by the World Bank for poverty thresholds and adjusted for the federation’s higher floor, is approximately 1.7 percent of gross planetary product at founding, declining as the productive base grows and the population in poverty declines. The figure is well within the three-percent ceiling on planetary revenues established by Article VI Section 2, leaving substantial margin for the other planetary functions (World Bank 2024; United Nations Development Programme 2024).

The planetary minimum on corporate income

The first of the federation’s revenue instruments is the planetary minimum on corporate income. The instrument draws on the OECD’s Inclusive Framework on Base Erosion and Profit Shifting and on the Two-Pillar Solution that 140 jurisdictions agreed in principle to in 2021. The architecture proposed here strengthens the Pillar Two minimum to a substantive floor — fifteen percent at founding, with provision for upward adjustment by amendment — and applies it to enterprises whose worldwide turnover exceeds a defined threshold (currently 750 million units of account, in line with the OECD framework).

The yield of the minimum, at the proposed rate and threshold, is estimated by the Tax Justice Network and the IMF at approximately 200-275 billion units of account annually (Tax Justice Network 2023). The yield is recovered principally from profits currently shifted to low-tax jurisdictions, where the empirical literature has shown that the shifting is not associated with corresponding real economic activity. The instrument is, in this sense, a recovery of revenue that has, by accounting choice rather than economic reality, been removed from the jurisdictions in which the underlying economic activity occurs (Piketty 2014).

Member polities retain their own corporate-tax authority, which may be set above the planetary minimum. The minimum is exactly what the term says: a floor, not a ceiling. Polities that wish to maintain higher rates, for distributive or fiscal reasons, are free to do so, and the planetary minimum is credited against the polity’s tax to avoid double taxation.

The greenhouse-gas charge

The second instrument is a charge on greenhouse-gas emissions, calibrated to the social cost of carbon as determined by the Standing Scientific Authority. The current estimates of the social cost of carbon, drawing on the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report and the substantial subsequent literature, place the value in the range of 100 to 250 units per tonne of CO2-equivalent, with the central estimate moving upward as the empirical record on climate damages accumulates (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 2023; Stern 2006).

The charge is levied at the point of extraction or import, in conformity with the analytical convention that places the charge on the source of the emission rather than on its diffuse points of consumption. The yield, at current emission levels and the central estimate of social cost, is in the range of 4 to 10 trillion units annually. A defined share of the yield (currently 60 percent) finances the planetary basic provision and the federation’s other functions; the residual share is returned to member polities for climate adaptation and for transition support to industries and workers affected by the price signal. The architecture is, in this sense, a hybrid of revenue instrument and Pigouvian price, with the price signal calibrated to the actual external cost of the activity it charges.

The framers were conscious that the charge will produce substantial economic adjustment in industries currently dependent on unpriced emissions. The adjustment is the point: a price signal that does not produce adjustment is not a price signal but an accounting entry. The transition support is, accordingly, substantial, and is intended to ensure that the costs of the adjustment fall principally on the activities that have been emitting the externality, not on the workers and communities whose livelihoods have, by no fault of their own, become entangled with those activities.

Resource rents from planetary commons

The third instrument is a charge on the extraction of resources from planetary commons: orbital, lunar, deep-seabed, atmospheric, and certain spectrum allocations. The instrument is a translation, into operational form, of the long-standing principle that resources belonging to no person belong to all persons, and that the rent from their extraction is properly distributed to the planetary population rather than captured by the extractor. The architecture draws on the precedent of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, which established the International Seabed Authority for the comparable purpose of deep-seabed mineral resources, and on the various proposals — by Henry George in the nineteenth century, by Thomas Paine before him, and by a substantial line of subsequent writers — for the public capture of unearned rent.

The yield from this instrument is currently small but is expected to grow as the human economy extends into orbital and lunar resources over the coming decades. The framers considered the instrument important less for its present yield than for the constitutional commitment that, when planetary commons begin to generate substantial rents, the rents will be recognized from the outset as belonging to the planetary population rather than to whichever enterprises happen to reach the resource first.

The financial transactions charge

The fourth instrument is a small charge on long-distance financial transactions in defined categories. The instrument is descended from the proposal long advanced by James Tobin and elaborated in subsequent work by the European Commission and others. Its rate is small (currently 0.05 percent on covered transactions); its scope is restricted to transactions whose principal economic function is speculative or arbitrage rather than the productive financing of real economic activity. The yield is in the range of 200-400 billion units annually, depending on the response of transaction volumes to the price signal.

The framers selected the financial transactions charge less for its yield, substantial as the yield is, than for its substantive effects on the structure of financial markets. The charge falls more heavily on activity characterized by very high transaction volumes and very thin margins per transaction — the kinds of high-frequency, algorithmic, and arbitrage activity whose contribution to productive finance has been, on the empirical record, modest at best, and whose contribution to systemic instability has been substantial. The price signal, set at a level that ordinary investment activity easily absorbs, makes the high-volume strategies marginally less profitable, with consequences for the structure of financial markets that the framers regard as desirable on independent grounds.

The protected fund

The protected fund, established by Article X Section 3, is the federation’s principal instrument for long-horizon obligations. The fund’s principal is preserved across generations; the returns finance climate adaptation, ecological repair, scientific research, and infrastructure renewal. The fund’s establishment is funded by a defined share of planetary revenues during a building period (currently fifteen years from founding), at the conclusion of which it is expected to reach a size sufficient that returns can finance the long-horizon obligations on a sustainable basis.

The fund’s investment policy is conservative, with a defined allocation among public-debt instruments of member polities, infrastructure investments within the federation, and a smaller share in productive equity. The fund does not undertake speculative investment, does not invest in activities inconsistent with the federation’s principles (notably, in fossil-fuel extraction whose continuation is incompatible with the climate commitments), and does not, by its scale, become a controlling shareholder in any commercial enterprise. The framers were conscious of the risk that a fund of the scale contemplated could distort capital markets or political outcomes; the policy is designed to provide returns sufficient to the fund’s purposes without producing the distortion.

The non-instruments

It is worth stating, finally, what the federation does not do.

It does not operate a planetary currency. The currencies of member polities continue to function; the federation’s accounts are denominated in a defined unit of account, expressed as a basket of major currencies, and conversion is performed by the existing institutions of international payments. The framers considered, and rejected, the establishment of a planetary currency: such a currency would require the federation to accept the macroeconomic responsibilities of central banking, and the federation’s competence in the matter is, on any honest reading, less than that of the existing major central banks.

It does not operate a planetary public debt as a primary financing instrument. Planetary functions are financed by the planetary revenues; debt is available only as a stabilization instrument across multi-year fiscal cycles, in defined and limited amounts. The federation does not borrow from financial markets to finance current operations.

It does not nationalize, expropriate, or operate planetary enterprises in the substantive sense of the term. Member polities may, within their own constitutional traditions, organize their economies around any combination of market, public, and cooperative institutions; the federation imposes no preference and undertakes no operational role in such organization. The federation’s economic functions are, by deliberate design, narrow: revenue collection, dignity-floor disbursement, fund management, regulatory oversight in defined domains. The substantive economic life of the planet remains in the hands of its peoples and their member polities.

The economic architecture of the federation is, in summary, a small set of well-designed instruments, calibrated to recoverable revenues from sources currently undertaxed, applied to a narrow set of planetary functions, with a strong floor of distributive commitment and a strong ceiling on planetary fiscal scale. It is not, and is not intended to be, an economic philosophy. It is the operational machinery of a specific set of constitutional commitments, designed to be financially sustainable, distributively defensible, and politically tractable in the conditions of 2026 and the decades likely to follow.

Chapter Twenty-One describes the restoration of work: how the federation’s institutional commitments translate into the structural conditions under which human work, in the broadest sense, can recover the dignity it has, in the late industrial age, lost.

Chapter Twenty-One

The Restoration of Work

The architecture’s commitment to work is not a commitment to employment as it has been organized in the late industrial age. It is, more deeply, a commitment to the structural conditions under which human work, in the broadest sense, recovers the place in human life that the diagnostic chapter on work argued it has been losing. The chapter that follows describes the institutional translation of the commitment: a small set of arrangements that, taken together, alter the bargain on offer between the person and the productive economy in such a way as to restore the dignity of work without dictating to anyone what work is to be done.

What the architecture does not attempt

The framers did not attempt the recovery of any specific industrial pattern. The pattern of long-tenure employment in large industrial enterprises, with high union density, defined-benefit pensions, and stable career trajectories, that characterized the post-war decades in the wealthy economies, is not recoverable; the conditions that produced it (national-scale industries, high tariff barriers, low female labor-force participation, defined-benefit demography) are gone, in most cases for reasons that are themselves not regrettable. The architecture does not attempt to reconstruct it.

The framers did not attempt the elimination of work, on the speculative ground that intelligent systems will, at some indefinite point, render most human labor unnecessary. The empirical record on this proposition is contested; the timeline is uncertain; the architectural choice is to design for present conditions, with the capacity to adjust as the conditions change, rather than to legislate against speculation. Were the speculation to prove correct, the floor of dignity provides the structural condition under which a society of substantially reduced labor demand could be governed humanely; were it to prove incorrect, as it has consistently in the past, the architecture functions equally.

The framers did not attempt the imposition of a single labor-market regime across the federation. Member polities continue to organize their labor markets according to their own constitutional and political traditions: the Nordic models of high union density and active labor-market policy; the continental European models of co-determination and statutory works councils; the Anglophone models of decentralized bargaining; the East Asian models of long-tenure firm-internal labor markets; the various models of the developing economies. The architecture’s commitments are general, and apply to all of these, while leaving the specific institutional arrangements to the polities.

What the architecture does attempt

The architecture establishes four operational commitments, each of which addresses a specific structural failure of the late industrial age.

The first is portability. The benefits associated with employment in the post-war model — health coverage, retirement provision, unemployment protection, training accumulation, sometimes housing access — were attached to the long-tenure employment relationship in ways that did not survive the breakdown of that relationship. The architecture detaches these benefits from the specific employer and reattaches them to the person. Health coverage is provided by polity-level public systems funded in part by planetary contribution; retirement provision is portable across employers and across borders within the federation; unemployment protection follows the person across labor-market transitions; training accumulation is recorded in the personal credentials layer described in Chapter Nineteen and remains the person’s regardless of subsequent employment. The architecture’s commitment is that no person loses entitlements by reason of changing employer or moving across the federation.

The second is countervailing voice. The decline of organized labor in the late industrial age has, on the empirical record, been one of the principal mechanisms by which bargaining power shifted to capital and by which the share of national income going to labor declined. The architecture does not legislate any specific form of labor organization; it does establish the constitutional protection of the right to organize, of collective bargaining, and of collective action, and it requires member polities to maintain institutions through which these rights can be exercised effectively. The forms of organization may vary: traditional craft and industrial unions; works councils; sectoral bargaining bodies; cooperative ownership structures; professional associations; new institutions yet to be developed for sectors where the existing forms have not taken hold. The principle is that the bargaining table must have, on all sides, parties capable of representing the interests they claim to represent. Where bargaining-power asymmetry is too great for ordinary contract law to produce just outcomes — and the empirical record shows that, for substantial fractions of the contemporary labor force, this is the situation — the institutions of collective voice are a structural condition of the architecture.

The third is retraining infrastructure. The pace of technological change in the late industrial age has produced a labor market in which the human capital appropriate to a person’s career at age twenty-five is unlikely to remain appropriate at age fifty-five. The architecture establishes, at the polity level with planetary support, a system of lifelong retraining: not a one-time intervention at the moment of unemployment, but a continuous infrastructure available to working persons throughout their careers, financed in part by employer contributions, in part by public revenue, and in part — for higher-cost specialized retraining — by the protected fund. The infrastructure includes formal education, on-the-job training, certified credentials, and the recognition of prior learning, and is integrated with the unemployment-protection system in such a way that retraining is available without the person having first to fall out of employment.

The fourth is working-time discretion. The pattern of full-time employment as the dominant form of labor-market participation, with limited public provision for part-time, project-based, or care-related interruptions, has produced a structural mismatch between the rhythms the contemporary labor market expects and the rhythms human lives actually display. The architecture commits the federation to the recognition of plural working patterns: full-time, part-time, project-based, sabbatical, care-leave, retraining-leave, and combinations among these. The recognition has fiscal consequences (benefits and entitlements pro-rated rather than gated), legal consequences (protection against discrimination based on working pattern), and cultural consequences (the deliberate construction of public support for patterns other than the inherited industrial default).

The relation to the floor

The four commitments operate in conjunction with the planetary basic provision of Chapter Twenty. The provision, by establishing an unconditional floor below which no income can fall, alters the structure of the bargaining over work in a fundamental way. A worker who knows that her subsistence is unconditionally provided is, in the bargaining over the terms of her labor, in a different and stronger position than a worker for whom the alternative to acceptance is destitution. The shift in bargaining position has been the explicit objective of basic-income proposals for some decades; the architecture proposed here treats the shift not as an isolated benefit of the provision but as a deliberate component of its purpose.

The framers were conscious of the empirical literature on the labor-supply effects of unconditional provision. The literature, drawing on the various pilot studies in Finland, Kenya, the United States, Canada, and elsewhere, finds the labor-supply effects to be modest in aggregate, with the principal observed adjustments being persons leaving exploitative employment for better employment, persons reducing hours to take on care responsibilities, persons returning to education and training, and persons starting small enterprises. The framers regard each of these adjustments as desirable. The architecture is, in this sense, indifferent to the question of whether the provision causes some recipients to work less in conventional employment, because the architecture’s understanding of “work” is not coextensive with conventional employment.

The dignity of unwaged work

The architecture takes seriously the proposition, sometimes treated as rhetorical but here treated as substantive, that much of the human work of central importance to a civilization is performed without wage. The work of caring for children, of caring for the aged and the ill, of maintaining households, of sustaining communities, of cultivating local democracy, of preserving cultural and linguistic traditions, of mentoring the young, of organizing voluntary associations, of producing the unpaid intellectual and creative work on which the paid creative economy ultimately depends — none of this work is captured by conventional measures of employment, and yet, on any honest accounting, the civilization could not function without it.

The architecture acknowledges this work in three operational ways. First, the floor of dignity does not condition the provision on participation in waged employment, with the consequence that persons engaged principally in unwaged work are not, by reason of the form of their work, deprived of subsistence. Second, the recognition of caring labor in the eligibility for retirement provision, in the credit toward credentials, and in the protection of working-time discretion gives unwaged caring work a formal standing in the institutions of the architecture. Third, the public-revenue support for the institutions of community, civic, and cultural participation — libraries, public broadcasters, civic associations, local arts institutions — finances the public-good components of unwaged work, on the principle that these are public goods and ought to be financed as such.

What is recovered

The work that is recovered, on this architecture, is not the work of the post-war factory or the post-war office. It is, more generally, the work in which a human being, given the opportunity to apply her capacities to ends she has had a hand in choosing, can construct a life she experiences as worthwhile. The conditions of such work are not, in any society’s record, automatic; they are the product of deliberate institutional choice. The chapter that has just ended states the institutional choices the federation makes. The choices are, in their detail, modest; in their combination, they reverse a half-century of structural drift in the relation of the person to her own productive activity. The reversal is, in the architecture’s account, the practical content of the principle of dignity as it applies to working life.

Chapter Twenty-Two describes the harmonization of law: how the federation’s substantive commitments are translated into a coherent framework of legal practice across the legal traditions of its members.

Chapter Twenty-Two

Harmonized Law

The federation’s commitment to harmonized law is not a commitment to a single legal code. The legal traditions of its members — the various civil-law families descended from Roman law, the common-law families descended from England, the Islamic legal traditions, the Confucian-inflected legal traditions of East Asia, the customary legal traditions of indigenous and traditional communities — are inheritances of long maturation, each adapted to the particular societies that produced them, and the federation’s architecture does not undertake their replacement. What it undertakes, instead, is the construction of an interface across these traditions sufficient to permit persons and enterprises to move across the federation, to conduct transactions across borders, and to seek remedy across legal systems, without the disabling friction that the absence of harmonization would otherwise produce.

The principle of harmonization without consolidation

The architectural choice rests on a principle that has, in the long history of cross-jurisdictional law, repeatedly been validated. The principle is that legal coordination across jurisdictions is most effective when it operates at the level of the principles to be applied and the procedures by which conflicts are resolved, rather than at the level of the substantive rules themselves. The European Union’s experience over seven decades of harmonization, the Hague Conference on Private International Law’s longer experience, the UN Commission on International Trade Law’s specialized successes, and the various regional integration projects in Africa and the Americas all converge on the same conclusion: the substantive law remains national or sub-national, the procedural framework for cross-border interaction is harmonized, and the boundary between the two is patrolled with care.

The architecture proposed here adopts the principle and applies it to the specific functions of the federation. The federation’s harmonized law operates at four levels: the constitutional commitments of Article IV, which apply to all persons within the federation regardless of which member polity’s substantive law governs their other relations; the procedural commitments described in Chapters Sixteen through Eighteen, which govern the operation of the federation’s institutions; the conflict-of-laws framework, which determines which member polity’s substantive law applies to cross-border transactions and disputes; and a small set of substantive rules that the constitutional principles require to be uniform across the federation.

The substantive uniformities

The set of substantive uniformities is small. It comprises:

The constitutional rights of Article IV. The non-derogable prohibitions on torture, on enslavement, on arbitrary deprivation of life, and on punishment without trial apply uniformly across the federation; the qualified rights are protected at a uniform substantive minimum, with member polities free to extend stronger protection within their own jurisdictions.

The floor of dignity. The threshold below which no person may fall is uniform in its substantive content (the six entitlements of Article V), although the operational implementation is calibrated to local conditions.

The custody regime for intelligent systems. The Custody Authority operates a single regulatory framework across the federation, on the grounds that the cross-border nature of intelligent systems makes a fragmented regulatory framework structurally inadequate.

The data-protection commitments. The substantive content of personal data protection is uniform across the federation, on the same cross-border grounds.

The treatment of planetary commons. The atmospheric, oceanic, orbital, lunar, deep-seabed, and electromagnetic commons are governed by uniform federation rules, on the obvious ground that fragmentation produces a tragedy of the commons that the architecture is constructed to prevent (Hardin 1968; Ostrom 1990).

The procedural minimums. The standards of due process, of judicial independence, of access to counsel, of public trial, and of effective remedy that Article IV requires are uniform across the federation, with member polities permitted to provide greater protection but not less.

Beyond these, the substantive law of contract, of property, of family, of inheritance, of tort, of crime (above the absolute prohibitions), of administrative procedure, of taxation (above the planetary minimum), and of regulation remains in the member polities. The federation does not legislate in these areas, does not interpret them, and does not adjudicate them except as they intersect with its enumerated competence.

The conflict-of-laws framework

The framework for determining which member polity’s substantive law applies to cross-border transactions is among the federation’s most operationally important contributions. It draws on the substantial body of work produced by the Hague Conference on Private International Law over the past century and a half, with adjustments for planetary scope and for the digital character of much contemporary cross-border activity.

The framework operates by a small set of connecting factors: the place of habitual residence of the person, the place of incorporation of the enterprise, the place of performance of the contract, the place of the harm in tort, the place of the property in matters of property, and so on. The connecting factors are familiar from existing private international law; the federation’s contribution is to provide a single uniform set of factors across the federation, replacing the patchwork of conflicts rules that currently produces uncertainty in cross-border transactions.

The framework includes a public-policy override: a member polity is not required to apply foreign law that would, in its application, violate the constitutional principles of the federation or the fundamental principles of the polity’s own legal order. The override is narrowly drawn — it does not entitle a polity to refuse foreign law merely because the polity’s own law differs — but it is real, and is the procedural mechanism by which the constitutional commitments are enforced against the conflicts process itself.

The Constitutional Court and the courts of members

The relationship between the Constitutional Court and the courts of member polities is, in the architecture, modeled on the relationship that has, over decades, been worked out between the European Court of Justice and the courts of EU member states, with adjustments. The Constitutional Court has exclusive jurisdiction over the interpretation of the federation’s constitution and of the organic statutes; it has appellate jurisdiction in defined classes of cases on the basis of certified questions referred by member-polity courts; it has original jurisdiction in disputes between member polities and in defined categories of constitutional rights cases. The courts of member polities have exclusive jurisdiction over their own substantive law, are bound by the Constitutional Court’s interpretation of the federation’s law, and are required to refer to the Constitutional Court questions of federation law that arise in the course of their proceedings.

The framework requires a substantial culture of judicial cooperation, which cannot be legislated but which the institutions of the federation work to cultivate. Judicial training, judicial exchanges, joint research, and the routine consultation among judges across the federation are funded by the federation’s institutions, on the basis that the harmonization of law is a long process whose principal investment is in the persons who operate the legal systems rather than in the texts that govern them.

Recognition and enforcement

The architecture establishes the mutual recognition of judgments across the federation: a final judgment of a court of competent jurisdiction in one member polity is enforceable in any other member polity, subject to the public-policy override and to defined procedural exceptions (default judgments, judgments obtained by fraud, judgments inconsistent with prior judgments). The recognition framework draws on the Hague Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Judgments, with extensions appropriate to the federation’s deeper integration.

The framework permits the federation’s substantive commitments to be enforced effectively across borders without the long delays and uncertainties that characterize the contemporary practice of recognition outside specialized regimes. A person who has obtained a judgment in one member polity for an injury caused by an enterprise operating across the federation can have that judgment enforced wherever the enterprise’s assets are located, on the basis of a streamlined registration procedure, with substantive review limited to the narrow grounds set out in the recognition statute.

The international plane

The federation’s relationship to the existing international legal order requires careful management. The federation does not, on its founding, replace the United Nations system, the International Court of Justice, the International Criminal Court, the World Trade Organization, the International Labour Organisation, the World Health Organization, or any of the other multilateral institutions through which the international order has, with mixed results, been organized over the past three-quarters of a century. The federation joins them, in its own institutional capacity, on terms compatible with their existing legal frameworks.

The compatibility is, in many respects, straightforward. The federation’s commitments to human rights are coextensive with, and in some respects exceed, the commitments of the International Bill of Rights and the Geneva Conventions. The federation’s environmental commitments are coextensive with, and in some respects exceed, the commitments of the Paris Agreement and its successor instruments (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change 2015). The federation’s commitments to the prosecution of grave crimes are coextensive with the obligations of states parties to the Rome Statute (United Nations Diplomatic Conference 1998). The federation accedes to these instruments in its own capacity and operates within their frameworks.

In other respects the compatibility is less straightforward, and the federation’s relationship to existing institutions will, over time, require negotiation. The principal questions are likely to be the relationship to the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank in matters of economic governance, the relationship to the World Trade Organization in matters of trade, and the relationship to the various regional organizations whose competence overlaps with the federation’s. The framers expected that these relationships would be worked out by treaty negotiation over the years following founding, and that the federation, operating from a strong constitutional base, would be a constructive participant in the broader reform of the international order rather than a substitute for it.

The horizon of integration

The architecture of harmonized law is, like the federation as a whole, modest in its initial scope and capable of deepening over time. The framers were conscious that the European Union’s harmonization, which began with a customs union of six countries in 1958 and now extends to many areas of substantive law across twenty-seven countries, was the work of decades, with periods of advance, retreat, and consolidation. The federation’s harmonization is unlikely to proceed at greater speed; the framers regard the prospect of deepening over decades, in response to demonstrated need and demonstrated capacity, as preferable to the alternative of premature consolidation that subsequent generations would resent.

The texture of the deepening, where it occurs, will be shaped by the particular failures and opportunities of subsequent decades, which the present generation cannot fully foresee. The architecture’s contribution is to establish the institutional framework within which the deepening, when it is needed, can proceed by negotiation among legitimate authorities rather than by the unilateral action of dominant powers. The framework is the contribution; the substantive direction of legal integration is left to those who will, in their time, weigh the considerations that are not yet visible.

Chapter Twenty-Three describes honest enterprise: how the federation establishes the structural conditions under which productive economic activity proceeds within bounds the principles of the architecture require.

Chapter Twenty-Three

Honest Enterprise

The architecture’s relationship to enterprise — to the productive activity through which goods and services are made, distributed, and exchanged — is among its most carefully drawn. The federation does not establish an economic philosophy; it does not require, encourage, or forbid any particular form of productive organization; it does not undertake to replace, supplement, or compete with the enterprises that operate within its jurisdiction. What it does undertake is the establishment of the structural conditions under which honest enterprise is possible — conditions under which producers compete on the merits of what they produce rather than on their capacity to externalize costs, capture regulators, or evade the legitimate claims of the persons their activities affect. The chapter that follows describes those conditions.

What honest enterprise is

The architecture’s working definition of honest enterprise has four components, drawn from a long tradition of political-economic thought running from Adam Smith through John Stuart Mill, John Maynard Keynes, Joseph Schumpeter, the institutional economists of the mid-twentieth century, and the contemporary work on inclusive institutions (Smith 1776; Schumpeter 1942; Acemoglu and Robinson 2012).

The first component is full cost-bearing: an enterprise’s prices reflect the costs the enterprise actually imposes on the world, including the costs imposed on third parties (the externalities) and on the future (the long-horizon costs). An enterprise that produces electricity from coal but does not pay for the climate damage of its emissions, or that mines minerals but does not pay for the ecological degradation of its tailings, or that manufactures chemicals but does not pay for the medical costs its products impose on downstream consumers, is operating on a price structure that misrepresents the activity’s actual cost. The misrepresentation is not, on the empirical record, a small matter; for several substantial industries, the unpriced externalities approach or exceed the operating margins, with the consequence that the entire industry is, in economic terms, value-subtracting rather than value-adding when correctly accounted for.

The second component is genuine competition: enterprises compete on the merits of their products, services, and operations, rather than on their capacity to capture rents from regulatory privilege, network monopoly, or barriers to entry that have no productive justification. The competitive condition has, in the late industrial age, been substantially eroded in many sectors, with the consequence that the productive efficiency the textbook treatments of the market economy attribute to it has been correspondingly degraded.

The third component is truthful disclosure: enterprises produce, in their public communications and their dealings with persons and authorities, accurate and complete information about the activities they conduct, the products they sell, and the conditions under which they operate. Truthful disclosure is the precondition of competitive markets, of effective regulation, and of meaningful consumer choice; its erosion, through systematic obfuscation, advertising claims that mislead, and disclosure burdens calibrated to defeat rather than serve the public interest, has been a substantial component of the contemporary failure of accountability.

The fourth component is respect for the standing of others: enterprises proceed in their activities with explicit recognition of the legitimate interests of the workers they employ, the consumers they serve, the communities they affect, the environment in which they operate, and the future generations whose conditions their activities help to shape. The standing is not, on this architecture, a matter of voluntary corporate responsibility — a category whose contribution has, on the empirical record, been modest where it has not been actively counterproductive — but a matter of structural requirement, enforced by the institutions described in the chapters of Part Four.

Full cost-bearing as architectural requirement

The economic instruments described in Chapter Twenty operationalize the first component. The greenhouse-gas charge prices climate externalities at the social cost of carbon. The resource-rent charges price the depletion of planetary commons. The financial-transactions charge prices the systemic externalities of certain classes of speculation. The architecture extends the principle to other domains in which substantial externalities have been documented: charges on certain pollutants, on certain land-use conversions, on certain practices of resource depletion, on the operations of intelligent systems whose deployment imposes costs on parties beyond the system’s principal.

The framers were conscious that pricing externalities is not the only available policy instrument; in some domains, direct regulation is more effective, and in others a combination of pricing and regulation is appropriate. The architecture does not insist on the price instrument in domains where it is inappropriate; it insists, more generally, that the externality be brought within the cost structure of the activity that produces it, by whatever combination of price and regulation is most effective.

The principle has substantial implications for the structure of contemporary industries. Sectors whose contemporary profitability depends on unpriced externalities — fossil-fuel extraction, certain forms of factory livestock production, certain extractive mining operations, certain components of the automotive industry, parts of the data-economy as currently structured — will, under full cost-bearing, become substantially less profitable. Sectors whose practices internalize their costs more thoroughly will become correspondingly more competitive. The structural shift is the point: the architecture relies on a price signal that, by faithfully representing actual costs, allows productive activity to flow toward the activities that are, in fact, productive of net value, and away from the activities that have, by externalization, only appeared to be.

Restoration of genuine competition

The architecture’s commitment to competitive markets requires more than the restoration of conventional antitrust enforcement, although that restoration is part of it. The contemporary failure of competition has structural sources that ordinary antitrust law was not designed to address: the network effects that make some platforms naturally winner-take-all; the data accumulations that make incumbents in data-rich sectors structurally advantaged against entrants; the patent and copyright extensions that have enclosed substantial portions of human knowledge for limited terms whose expiration has been repeatedly delayed; the regulatory complexity that small firms cannot afford to navigate but that established firms have learned to use as a barrier to entry.

The architecture addresses these structural sources by several specific commitments. Platforms above defined thresholds are required to provide interoperability with competing platforms, on terms that permit users to migrate their data and their connections to alternative services. Data accumulations subject to network effects are required to be available, on fair terms, to entrants whose use of the data does not violate the data-protection rights of the persons concerned. Patent and copyright terms are restored to the durations originally contemplated by the constitutional drafters of the modern intellectual-property regimes, with extensions limited to defined exceptional cases. Regulatory complexity is reviewed continuously by the federation’s regulatory authorities for its competitive effects, with simplification required where it does not serve the substantive purposes of the regulation.

The framers were conscious that the contemporary configuration of major platforms has been built up over decades and represents substantial investments by their operators and substantial value to their users. The architecture does not undertake the dismantling of the platforms; it undertakes the establishment of the structural conditions under which the platforms compete with possible alternatives on terms the alternatives have a fair chance of matching. The interoperability requirement, in particular, is designed to permit competition without disruption: users keep using the existing platforms if they prefer, but acquire the capacity to leave for alternatives if they choose, with their data and their connections intact.

Truthful disclosure

The disclosure architecture combines four operational components. First, financial reporting standards are aligned across the federation, with common rules on the recognition of revenue, the valuation of assets, the disclosure of contingent liabilities, and the treatment of intangible capital. The standards draw on the work of the International Accounting Standards Board and the International Sustainability Standards Board, with the federation’s contribution being the binding application of the standards across its jurisdiction rather than the voluntary adoption that has characterized the contemporary regime.

Second, sustainability and human-rights reporting are required of enterprises above defined thresholds, on the same legal footing as financial reporting. The reporting covers the substantive matters that the federation’s constitutional commitments make material: greenhouse-gas emissions across the value chain, water and other ecological footprints, labor practices, supply-chain conditions, and the operation of intelligent systems within the enterprise. The reporting is auditable, on the same standards as financial reporting; misrepresentation has the same consequences.

Third, advertising and marketing communications are subject to truthfulness standards enforced across the federation. The standards are substantive: claims must be supportable by evidence at the time they are made; comparative claims must reflect a reasonable comparison; claims about a product’s effects on health, the environment, or other matters of public concern must meet evidentiary standards proportionate to the claim’s significance. Enforcement is by both public authority and by private right of action, with damages available on terms that make enforcement viable in cases of small per-person harm but large aggregate harm.

Fourth, the operations of intelligent systems used by enterprises in their dealings with persons are subject to the disclosure requirements of the Custody architecture: the person interacting with the system has a right to know that an intelligent system is involved, what data it uses, what decisions it makes, and how those decisions can be reviewed by a human party.

The corporate form and its custodians

The architecture’s treatment of the corporate form is conservative. The corporation, as an institution permitting the assembly of capital and labor in productive enterprise, with limited liability for shareholders, has been one of the principal organizational achievements of the past several centuries, and the architecture preserves it. What the architecture modifies is the configuration of the corporation’s accountability — the duties of its directors, the rights of its stakeholders, the obligations of its disclosures, the constraints on its political activity, and the conditions under which its limited liability operates.

The directors of corporations above defined thresholds owe duties not only to shareholders but to the constituencies whose welfare the corporation’s activities materially affect: workers, consumers, communities, the environment, and future generations. The duties are concretized by the disclosure requirements above, by the standing of the affected constituencies in defined matters, and by the operation of the procedural protections of administrative law against corporate decisions of substantial public consequence.

The political activity of corporations is restricted to the activities legitimately associated with their productive functions: trade-association membership, technical comment on regulations affecting their operations, and contributions to civic life through philanthropic activity disclosed in detail. Direct political contributions to candidates, parties, or referendum campaigns are restricted on terms specified in the organic statute; the lobbying of legislators is regulated, registered, and recorded; the use of corporate resources for political purposes is disclosed in such manner as to permit shareholders, workers, and the public to assess and respond.

The framers were conscious that the contemporary configuration of corporate political activity has, in many jurisdictions, become a structural feature of legislative and regulatory processes. The architecture’s response is incremental rather than revolutionary: clear rules, transparent disclosure, and the institutional standing of competing interests in the political process, rather than the prohibition of corporate participation in public life.

Closing of Part Four

The eight chapters of Part Four — the public ledger, the governance layer, intelligence under custody, sovereign identity, economic instruments, the restoration of work, harmonized law, and honest enterprise — describe the operational architecture through which the federation’s constitutional commitments become real. The architecture is not, in any of its components, technologically novel in the sense that nothing like it has previously existed; it is novel in its combination, in the integration of components that have, in existing institutions, been pursued separately and at smaller scale, and in the deliberate alignment of the components to the principles stated in Part Two.

The architecture is incomplete in important respects. It does not specify, in detail, the implementation of every component, leaving substantial work to the founding convention and to the organic statutes; it does not anticipate every interaction among components, leaving substantial work to the institutions that will operate them; it does not foreclose the development of further components as conditions and capacities evolve. The architecture is, in this sense, a starting structure rather than a finished design. The starting structure is, however, sufficient: it gives the federation a working machinery for its first generation of operation, and it gives subsequent generations the institutional and conceptual base from which to refine the architecture in light of operational experience.

The remaining parts of the book address the transition to the federation from the conditions of 2026, and the longer horizon of what becomes possible after the federation is operating.

Part Five describes the transition: the work of the founding generation, from the initial convocation of polities through the first decade of operation.

Part Five

The Transition

No constitution begins on the first day of the world. The federation, when it founds itself, will found itself in conditions it did not choose, with materials that have already been used, in a world whose existing arrangements will not yield by argument alone. The three chapters that follow describe how a transition that does not begin in catastrophe might be made to work.

Chapter Twenty-Four

Year One — The Founding

A federation of the kind described in Parts Three and Four cannot be brought into existence by a single act, on a single day, by a single party. It will require, at minimum, an originating convocation of consenting polities; a draft constitutional text on which the convocation can deliberate; the ratification of that text by the polities and, in many of them, by their populations; the establishment of the institutions named in the text; the convening of the institutions; the enactment of the founding organic statutes; and the operational launching of the architectural subsystems. The chapter that follows offers a working sketch of how the first year of this work might be conducted, on the assumption that political conditions sufficient to begin the work are obtained. The sketch is not a prediction; it is a planning document, the kind that any serious institutional founding requires, written before the political conditions are clear so that, when they become clear, the work has somewhere to begin.

The originating convocation

The convocation is the moment at which a defined set of consenting polities, on the basis of an articulated common will, undertake to establish a federation of the kind here described. The convocation has historical analogues: the Philadelphia Convention of 1787, the Paris Conference of 1919, the San Francisco Conference of 1945, the Maastricht negotiations of 1991-92. None of the analogues is fully apposite to the present case; each provides instructive features.

The Philadelphia precedent suggests that a convocation is most effective when its mandate is broad, its participation is restricted to the polities directly engaged, and its proceedings are conducted with sufficient privacy to permit honest exchange but with sufficient eventual transparency to permit informed ratification. The architectural choice in the present case is to follow the precedent: the convocation deliberates in working session over the course of an extended period (in the planning sketch, six to twelve months), with participants drawn from the consenting polities at a level of authority sufficient to commit their governments to the convocation’s product, with daily proceedings recorded but not published until the convocation has concluded, and with the final product committed to ratification by the procedures of each consenting polity.

The convocation’s product is not, in this sketch, a finished constitution. It is, more modestly, a draft constitutional text, a draft schedule of organic statutes, a draft schedule for the institutional founding, and a draft set of accession terms for polities that wish to join the federation after the founding. The draft is committed to ratification; the ratification process is itself a substantial and time-consuming undertaking, involving in most polities a combination of parliamentary action and, in some, popular referendum.

The architectural commitment to legitimacy through ratification is firm. The federation does not begin by the simple announcement of its founding; it begins by the ratification of its constitution by polities representing a substantial share of the planetary population, in such number and distribution as the convocation specifies. The threshold for entry into force is, deliberately, set at a level high enough to ensure that the founding is more than an act of a small coalition but low enough that the founding is not held hostage to the consent of every polity in the world. The framers expected, on the basis of comparable historical precedents, that the threshold would be reached on the consent of polities representing somewhere in the range of 60 to 75 percent of the planetary population, with the remainder acceding over the following years and decades on terms agreed during the founding.

The work of the first year

The first year of operation is, in the planning sketch, organized around the constitution and seating of the federation’s institutions and the operational launch of the planetary basic provision in pilot regions.

The Council of Polities is convoked within thirty days of entry into force. Its first task is the appointment of the institutions of the federation: the judges of the Constitutional Court, the leadership of the Custody Authority, the leadership of the Standing Scientific Authority, the Tribune of Future Generations, the Auditor of the Federation, and the operational leadership of the public ledger. The appointments are made on the basis of the procedures specified in the founding organic statute and require, for each, the concurrence of the Citizens’ Assembly. Because the Citizens’ Assembly cannot itself be constituted before its members have been selected by the polities under the procedures of the organic statute, an interim Assembly is provided for in the founding schedule, drawn by random selection from the populations of the founding member polities, to perform the founding-period concurrence functions while the permanent Assembly is constituted.

The Constitutional Court is sworn in within ninety days. Its first cases, by historical pattern, are likely to be challenges to the founding actions themselves: petitions from non-member polities or from interests within member polities arguing that the founding is procedurally defective. The Court’s response to these initial challenges, on the historical record of comparable foundings, is decisive for the federation’s subsequent legitimacy. The framers expected that the Court would address the challenges with substantive engagement and, where the challenges are without merit, with timely dismissal supported by detailed reasons.

The public ledger is launched on a schedule timed to permit substantial technical preparation in advance of the political founding. The framers expected that the ledger’s technical infrastructure would be developed, tested, and stress-tested over the period between the convocation’s product and the entry into force, so that the ledger is operationally ready on the first day of operation. The launch involves the migration of operational records from the convocation itself onto the ledger, the deployment of the public-query infrastructure, and the activation of the signing infrastructure for the federation’s officers.

The planetary basic provision is launched in pilot regions during the first year, on a defined schedule. The pilots are selected to represent a range of operational conditions: regions of substantial poverty in different climates, regions of relative prosperity in which the floor is operative for marginal populations, regions of recent conflict in which the institutional infrastructure is fragile. The pilot operation provides operational experience for the planetary tier and for the polity-level partner institutions, identifies the operational problems that an architectural sketch cannot anticipate, and demonstrates, to populations not yet covered, the practical content of the federation’s commitment to dignity. The full extension to the population of the federation is scheduled for years two and three, on the basis of the operational learning of year one.

The political environment

The federation’s founding will not be a quiet event. The framers were conscious that powerful interests, in nearly every polity, have substantial reason to oppose the founding’s success: the enterprises whose business models depend on the unpriced externalities the architecture corrects, the political actors whose authority depends on the absence of the cross-border institutions the architecture establishes, the populations whose understanding of national identity does not extend to a planetary tier, the foreign powers (the term is used in the planning sketch without specification) whose international position depends on the absence of a planetary institution capable of constraining their conduct.

The opposition will operate on multiple fronts. It will argue that the founding is illegitimate, that its institutions are unaccountable, that its commitments are utopian, that its operations are corrupt, that its purposes are concealed. Some of the arguments will be substantive critiques offered in good faith and worthy of engagement. Others will be opportunistic. The framers’ working assumption is that the federation responds to substantive critique with substantive answer and to opportunism with the patient and demonstrable performance of the principles to which the founding has committed itself.

The principal political asset of the federation, in this period, is the credibility of the public ledger. A founding institution that operates in real-time public view, whose acts are signed and traceable, whose proceedings are recorded, and whose performance against its commitments is continuously measurable, is harder to caricature than an opaque institution. The framers were, in this respect, optimistic: the architectural commitment to legibility is, among other things, a defense against political opposition that depends on misrepresentation.

The economic environment

The federation’s founding occurs in an economic environment whose response cannot be fully predicted. The principal sources of revenue uncertainty are the corporate-tax minimum (which depends on the global pattern of corporate response, including the relocation of activities and the restructuring of accounting), the greenhouse-gas charge (which depends on the response of energy markets, of governments operating energy-related fiscal instruments, and of the industries directly affected), and the resource-rent charges (which depend on extraction patterns that may be partially within the federation’s control and partially not).

The framers’ working assumption is that revenues in the first three years will be in the range of 60 to 80 percent of the long-run estimates, with the lower end reflecting substantial transitional adjustment by the affected industries and the higher end reflecting more rapid stabilization than is historically typical. The planetary basic provision is, accordingly, scaled to the lower end of the revenue range during the transition, with the floor’s threshold maintained at the substantive level required by Article V and the additional planetary functions phased in as revenues stabilize.

The framers were conscious that, in the founding period, the federation may be perceived as economically fragile. The perception is, in significant part, a self-fulfilling matter: a founding that performs against its commitments stabilizes the expectations on which the underlying revenues depend; a founding that struggles produces the conditions under which the struggle continues. The architectural response is to perform what is committed, as visibly as the legibility commitment requires, with the result that fragility narratives, where they appear, can be met with the demonstrable performance of operations.

The international environment

The federation’s relationships with non-member states, with international institutions, and with the existing economic and security order will require, in the founding period, deliberate management. The framers’ working assumption is that some non-member states will, in the founding period, regard the federation with hostility — particularly where the federation’s commitments to human rights or to economic transparency are inconsistent with those states’ domestic arrangements — and that some will regard the federation with cautious cooperation, with the alignment in many cases shifting over the founding period as the federation’s actual operations become clearer.

The architectural disposition toward non-member states is consistent: the federation does not extend its substantive jurisdiction beyond its members, does not undertake to coerce non-members into accession, does not interfere in the domestic affairs of non-members beyond the protections that international law generally allows, and does cooperate with non-member states on matters of common interest where the cooperation does not require the federation to compromise its constitutional commitments. The disposition is, in its outline, the disposition of any federation toward states outside its boundaries; the architectural specification is its application to a federation whose ambitions are planetary.

Closing of the year

The first year ends, in this sketch, with the institutions of the federation seated, the public ledger operating, the planetary basic provision launched in pilot regions, the founding organic statutes enacted, and the principal operational subsystems either operating or in defined launch sequences. The federation is not, at the end of the first year, fully operational; it is operating, against the schedule of the founding, on the substantial work of subsequent years.

The principal achievement of the year is, less the operations launched than the political fact established: a federation of polities representing a substantial share of the planetary population has, by the deliberate ratification of a constitutional commitment, undertaken to govern certain planetary matters by deliberate institutional means. The fact, once established, alters the political environment in which all subsequent decisions are taken. The succeeding chapters describe what is done with the altered environment.

Chapter Twenty-Five describes the convergence of the second through fifth years: the period in which operations stabilize, accession of additional polities proceeds, and the architectural subsystems extend to their full operational scope.

Chapter Twenty-Five

Years Two through Five — Convergence

The second through fifth years of the federation are, in the planning sketch, the period of convergence: the period during which the operations launched in the first year stabilize, the architectural subsystems extend to their full operational scope, additional polities accede on terms negotiated with the founding members, and the federation’s commitments are tested against the operational realities the founding sketch could not fully anticipate. The chapter that follows describes the principal lines of work of this period and the failure modes against which the architecture provides defense.

Stabilization of operations

The principal operational task of years two through five is the stabilization of the institutions launched in the first year. The Council of Polities and the Citizens’ Assembly develop their procedural cultures: the rhythms of session, the conventions of deliberation, the relationships among the standing committees, the working relationship between the chambers, the response to the routine and the exceptional matters that come before them. The Constitutional Court accumulates its first body of operative jurisprudence, on the constitutional questions that arise in the founding period and on the increasingly substantive matters that arise as operations expand. The Custody Authority moves from registry-establishment to active supervision: the first audits, the first restrictions, the first revocations, the first incidents reported through the safety-reporting infrastructure.

The framers’ working assumption is that, by the conclusion of the fifth year, the institutions are operating with the routine reliability that comparable institutions in the late industrial age have achieved. The reliability is the practical condition of the federation’s deepening: institutions whose operations are reliable can be entrusted with additional responsibilities; institutions whose operations are unreliable cannot. The architectural commitment to disciplined operation in the founding period is, on this account, a long-term investment in the federation’s later capacity to deepen.

Extension of the planetary basic provision

The provision moves, during years two through five, from pilot operation to full coverage of the federation’s population. The extension proceeds on a defined schedule, with priority given to regions of substantial poverty and operational readiness, and with deliberate attention to the operational and political learning of the pilots. The framers’ working assumption is that, by the end of the fourth year, the substantive coverage of the floor is established for the population of the federation, with the operational refinements continuing through the fifth year and into the years following.

The economic effects of the provision become visible during this period. The empirical literature on cash-transfer programs in pilot conditions has consistently shown effects in the directions the architecture anticipates: reductions in absolute poverty, improvements in indicators of child welfare and educational attainment, modest reductions in conventional employment offset by increases in entrepreneurship, education, and care work, improvements in mental-health and stress-related health indicators, and reductions in the violence and instability that severe deprivation produces (United Nations Development Programme 2024; Standing 2011b). The full-coverage extension, on the architectural sketch, produces these effects at planetary scale.

The political effects are not negligible. A population that has experienced, during the founding period, the practical content of the federation’s commitment to dignity is, on the empirical record of comparable transitions, more disposed to support the federation’s other commitments, more disposed to participate in the federation’s institutions, and more resistant to political opposition that proceeds by misrepresenting the federation’s actual operations. The political effect is, like the economic effect, the deliberate consequence of the architectural choice; it is the practical content of the proposition that legitimacy is produced by performance.

Stabilization of the revenue instruments

The greenhouse-gas charge, the corporate-tax minimum, and the other revenue instruments are, during years two through five, brought to their full operational scope. The instruments require, in their early years, substantial administrative learning: the determination of liability, the procedures of audit, the resolution of disputes over computation, the handling of avoidance strategies, the coordination with member-polity tax authorities. The administrative learning is necessary work, and the framers’ working assumption is that the revenue yield in years two through five rises through this period from the lower-end estimates of year one to the long-run estimates by year four or five.

The economic adjustments to the revenue instruments — the responses of affected industries, the consumption-pattern shifts caused by the price signals, the investment-flow shifts toward activities the price signals favor — proceed through this period at a pace that is partially within the federation’s control and partially not. The architectural commitment is to maintain the price signals at the levels the principles require, while supporting the affected workers and communities through the transition support funded from the revenues themselves.

The framers were conscious that the early years of substantial price adjustment will produce political pressure to soften the price signals. The pressure has, in comparable adjustments in member polities (the early years of European emissions trading, the early years of various national carbon prices), been substantial. The architectural response is to maintain the substantive commitment, while addressing the legitimate distributive concerns that the pressure expresses, by mechanisms that do not undermine the substantive commitment. The transition-support component of the revenues, the protected fund’s investments in affected regions, and the operation of the planetary basic provision in those regions are, jointly, the architectural answer to the distributive concern.

Accession of additional polities

The first five years are, on the planning sketch, also the period of substantial accession by polities not among the founding members. The framers’ working assumption, based on the historical pattern of comparable institutional foundings, is that a substantial fraction of the polities outside the founding membership will, on observing the federation in operation, undertake the procedures of accession during this period.
