The accession is on terms negotiated between the acceding polity and the federation, with the substantive constitutional commitments non-negotiable and the operational arrangements (transition periods, contributions, representation in the Council of Polities) subject to the negotiation.

The accession process is, in this sketch, deliberate and unhurried. The federation does not seek the accession of polities whose ratification of the constitutional commitments would be merely formal and whose subsequent compliance would require continuous coercion. The framers’ working assumption is that some polities, particularly those whose domestic political arrangements are inconsistent with the constitutional commitments, will not seek accession in the founding period, and that the federation’s relationship with these polities is best conducted on the international plane rather than through coerced membership.

The accession of polities whose populations represent significant additions to the federation’s membership produces operational adjustments: the planetary basic provision is extended to the new populations on the schedule appropriate to the local conditions; the institutional representation in the Council is recomputed; the operational infrastructure of the public ledger and the other federation systems is extended to cover the new jurisdictions. The framers’ working assumption is that, by the conclusion of the fifth year, the federation’s membership has stabilized at the level it will hold for the medium term, with subsequent accessions occurring at a slower pace as the remaining unaccounted populations consider their positions.

The first encounter with serious failure

No institutional founding of substantial scope proceeds without serious failure during its first years. The framers were conscious of the pattern. The federation’s first failure, when it occurs, will reveal something the founding had not adequately addressed, and the response to the failure will, more than the original founding commitments, determine the federation’s long-term character.

The candidates for the first serious failure include: a substantive bias or harmful operation by an intelligent system that the Custody Authority had registered but had not adequately supervised; an operational failure of the public ledger that produces, even briefly, a loss of legibility on consequential matters; a corruption case affecting senior federation officials, whose handling will set the precedent for subsequent comparable cases; a constitutional crisis between the chambers or between the federation and a member polity, requiring the Constitutional Court to operate on a question of substantial significance with limited prior precedent; an external shock — a regional conflict, a financial crisis, a natural disaster of substantial scope — testing the federation’s response capacity beyond what its founding institutions were sized for.

The architectural response to such failures is, in each case, the substantive performance of the principles. Failures are addressed openly, on the public ledger, with substantive engagement rather than minimization; responsible parties are held to account by the procedures of accountability; the institutional arrangements that produced the failure are revised in such manner as to reduce the probability of recurrence; the substantive commitments are maintained, even when the political pressure to soften them is substantial. The framers were conscious that the response to early failure is, more than any other operational matter, the principal source of the federation’s long-term legitimacy or its long-term degeneration. The architectural commitment to the principled response, including in cases where the principled response is politically costly, is the practical content of the federation’s seriousness about its own commitments.

The international environment in this period

The federation’s relationships with non-member states and with the existing international institutions evolve through this period. The principal lines of work include: the negotiation of cooperative arrangements with non-member states on matters of common interest (climate, trade, security, public health, cross-border crime); the harmonization of the federation’s substantive commitments with the obligations of member polities under prior international agreements; the participation of the federation, in its own institutional capacity, in the existing multilateral institutions on terms compatible with its constitutional structure; and the management of relationships with the substantial powers whose interests are most directly affected by the federation’s operations.

The framers’ working assumption is that the international environment in years two through five is, on balance, neither uniformly hostile nor uniformly cooperative, and that the federation’s diplomacy is, accordingly, calibrated to the specific conditions of each external relationship. The architectural disposition is consistent: cooperation where it serves the federation’s commitments, defense where the federation’s commitments are challenged, and the patient construction of the legitimate international order in which the federation can operate as one institution among others rather than as a competing claim to comprehensive authority.

The character of the period

Years two through five are, in the planning sketch, the period during which the federation moves from being a constitutional commitment to being an operating reality. The transition is hard work; the work is mostly invisible; its outcome is, by the conclusion of the period, a federation whose operations are reliable enough that subsequent decisions can be taken on the basis of operational fact rather than institutional aspiration. The shift from aspiration to fact is, in the framers’ account, the principal architectural achievement of the founding generation.

The achievement is not, in itself, transformative; the conditions diagnosed in Part One do not, by virtue of the federation’s stabilization, immediately resolve. They begin, however, to be addressed by institutional means that are adequate to their scale, by procedures whose legitimacy is established by their disciplined operation, by commitments whose substantive content is demonstrated rather than merely declared. The transformation, where it occurs, is the work of the period after — the steady-state operation described in the chapter that follows.

Chapter Twenty-Six describes the federation a decade out: the steady-state operation in which the architecture’s commitments have produced their characteristic effects on the conditions of human life on Earth.

Chapter Twenty-Six

A Decade Out — Steady State

Ten years after entry into force, the federation, on the architectural sketch, has reached a steady state of operation. The institutions are routine; the substantive commitments are operating at their designed scale; the founding generation has, in some part, concluded its founding work and turned over operational responsibility to the second generation of officeholders. The chapter that follows describes the conditions of human life under this steady state, with the qualifications appropriate to a sketch written before the fact.

What is different in the conditions of life

The principal differences, by ten years out, are concentrated in the conditions described by the diagnostic chapters of Part One.

The trust deficit, on the architectural sketch, is partially repaired. The institutions whose operations are visible in real time, whose decisions are signed and traceable, and whose commitments have been demonstrated by performance over a decade enjoy levels of public trust that the institutions of the late industrial age, on the empirical record of the past several decades, no longer enjoyed. The repair is partial: trust takes longer to rebuild than to lose, and the inheritance of mistrust from the institutions of the previous era persists in attenuated form for at least a generation. The trajectory, however, is the reversal of the long secular decline diagnosed in Chapter Two (OECD 2024; Pew Research Center 2024).

The distributional pattern is altered. The unconditional floor of dignity has, by ten years out, materially eliminated absolute poverty within the federation, with the additional consequences in child welfare, educational attainment, and public health that the empirical literature anticipated. The contemporary inequality at the upper tail of the distribution has been moderated, less by direct redistribution than by the structural commitments of the architecture: the planetary minimum on corporate income, the elimination of the principal tax-avoidance routes that had produced the substantial below-statutory effective rates of the late industrial age, the pricing of externalities that had been captured as profit, the disclosure requirements that had eliminated the structural advantages of secrecy. The Gini coefficient at planetary scale, on the architectural sketch, has declined from its level at the founding by a substantive but not transformative margin; the absolute share of the population in deprivation has declined more sharply, in line with the priority the architecture gives to the floor (Piketty 2014; Saez and Zucman 2019).

The work conditions described in Chapter Four are altered. The portability of benefits, the restoration of countervailing voice, the lifelong retraining infrastructure, and the working-time discretion have, by ten years out, produced labor markets in which transitions are less ruinous, the bargaining position of workers is more equal, the recognition of unwaged labor is more substantive, and the connection between productivity gains and worker compensation has been at least partially restored. The shift is not, in any sense, a return to the post-war pattern; it is, more accurately, the construction of conditions appropriate to the contemporary economy that approximate the conditions the post-war pattern provided in the conditions of its time.

The legal architecture is operating. The harmonized framework permits cross-border activity within the federation on terms approaching the friction levels of intra-national activity; the constitutional rights are enforced uniformly; the procedural protections are available to persons regardless of their location within the federation. The institutions of the federation, of the member polities, and of the lower tiers operate in coordination rather than competition, with the boundary disputes resolved by the procedures the architecture provides.

The intelligence-systems landscape is governed. The Custody Authority operates active supervision over the systems within its scope; the registration regime has produced a documented record of the systems in operation, the principals responsible for them, and the conditions of their deployment; the audit regime has identified and corrected substantial classes of failure that, in the absence of supervision, would have proceeded unaddressed; the capability ceilings have constrained the accumulation of capability under any single principal. The technology has continued to advance; the governance has, in distinction from the late industrial age, kept up.

The environmental trajectory is altered. The greenhouse-gas charge, operating at the social cost of carbon, has produced the structural economic adjustments the architects anticipated: substantial decline in fossil-fuel-intensive activities, substantial expansion of low-emission alternatives, substantial investment in adaptation infrastructure where the long-horizon climate damage cannot be avoided. The decade does not, by itself, resolve the climate problem; the climate problem is multi-decadal in nature, and ten years of disciplined response is the early portion of a much longer course of work. The trajectory, however, is consistent with the IPCC pathways toward the temperature targets the international community had nominally committed to during the late industrial age, in distinction from the trajectory the late industrial age was actually producing (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 2023; International Energy Agency 2024).

What is not different

It is, equally important, to be clear about what the architectural sketch does not claim is different by ten years out.

The federation has not eliminated, and cannot eliminate, the structural conflict of interests among its members. Polities continue to disagree, on substantial matters, about the appropriate use of the federation’s authority, the appropriate scope of cooperation, the appropriate response to particular crises. The disagreements are addressed by the procedures of the federation; they are not eliminated by them. The federation is, in this respect, an arena for the conduct of disagreement rather than the abolition of disagreement, and the framers regarded the distinction as important.

The federation has not eliminated, and cannot eliminate, the residual political pathologies that any large institution accumulates. Cases of corruption, of abuse of authority, of capture by particular interests, of underperformance in particular functions, occur during the decade and continue to occur in the conditions of steady-state operation. The architectural response is the institutional capacity to identify these failures, to address them by the procedures of accountability, and to learn from them in the design of subsequent operations. The federation does not claim to be free of failure; it claims to maintain the institutional conditions under which failure can be detected and addressed without bringing down the institution as a whole.

The federation has not, by ten years out, achieved the deeper cultural transformations that some early supporters of comparable projects have anticipated. The peoples of the federation continue to differ in their cultural commitments, their religious traditions, their linguistic identities, their political preferences, their economic structures. The federation has not undertaken the transformation of any of these; the architectural commitment to subsidiarity reserves these matters to the lower tiers and to the persons themselves. The transformation, where it occurs, is the work of those who undertake it locally, on terms appropriate to local conditions, with the federation’s role limited to the maintenance of the conditions under which local life can proceed without external coercion.

The federation has not abolished the international plane. Non-member states continue to operate on the international plane on terms only partially affected by the federation’s existence; substantial fractions of the planetary population live in jurisdictions whose accession to the federation is not, on the ten-year horizon, in prospect; the matters that affect the planet as a whole — climate, the oceans, the atmosphere, the orbital commons — are, in domains where the federation cooperates with non-member states, addressed by negotiation rather than by federation legislation. The federation is, in this respect, one institution among others on the international plane, with substantial competence in defined domains and with the disposition toward cooperation on the matters where its competence is partial.

The character of the steady state

The steady state, on the architectural sketch, is not a destination at which the federation has arrived and from which no further development is required. It is, more modestly, the conditions under which the federation’s operations have stabilized at the scale appropriate to its founding commitments, with the further development — the deepening of integration, the extension of competence, the response to new conditions — proceeding by the ordinary procedures the architecture provides.

The succeeding generations will, on this sketch, face their own decisions about the further development of the federation. They will face, in particular, decisions about: the extension of the constitutional commitments in light of conditions and capabilities that the founding generation could not anticipate; the deepening of integration in domains where the founding compromises proved inadequate; the response to the technological developments that, if past patterns continue, will continue to produce capabilities and risks that the founding institutions did not anticipate; the management of the relationship between the federation and the non-member states whose accession or continued non-membership will, over the decades, take various forms.

The framers’ working assumption is that these subsequent decisions are best taken by the generations that face them, on the basis of conditions the framers cannot fully foresee, by the institutional procedures the architecture provides. The architecture’s contribution is the institutional framework within which the decisions can be taken legitimately and operationally; the substance of the decisions is properly the work of the parties who will make them.

The federation, ten years out, is, in this sense, less an end than a beginning. It is the conclusion of the founding work and the beginning of the operational work, and it is the operational work — the substantive performance of the commitments, the patient deepening of legitimate institutions, the resolution of the inevitable failures — that the architecture has been constructed to support. The conditions of human life under the federation, ten years in, are, on the architectural sketch, materially better than the conditions under which the founding generation began their work. The improvement is not transformation; it is the steady, disciplined, and sustained operation of institutions adequate to their tasks. The transformation, if any, is the work of subsequent generations, on terms they will set.

Part Six follows. It asks what becomes possible after, on the assumption that the architecture is operating: what new horizons of human work, of human knowledge, and of human presence beyond the planet are opened by the institutional foundations the federation provides.

Part Six

What Becomes Possible

If the architecture is operating, the conditions of the present are no longer the conditions that constrain what comes next. The two chapters that follow ask, with deliberate restraint, what becomes possible on Earth and what becomes possible beyond it, on the assumption that the foundations have been laid.

Chapter Twenty-Seven

What Becomes Possible on Earth

The architectural sketch of Part Five concluded with a federation in steady state, ten years after entry into force. The conditions described there are not the destination of the work the architecture undertakes; they are the foundation. What becomes possible after that foundation is laid is the substantive question of this chapter. The discussion is, deliberately, restrained: the chapter does not promise transformations whose attainment depends on factors outside the architecture’s control, does not predict the cultural shifts whose course no architecture can determine, and does not guarantee outcomes that depend on the substantive choices of the generations that will inherit the institutional framework. What it does describe are the substantive possibilities the foundation makes available, on the disciplined assumption that the foundation continues to operate.

A stabilized climate

The principal, and most consequential, possibility opened by the operating federation is the stabilization of the climate at temperature levels consistent with the persistence of stable agricultural patterns, of stable coastal infrastructure, of stable ecological communities, and of the stable social conditions that depend on these. The architecture’s price signals on greenhouse-gas emissions, operating at the social cost of carbon, produce, on the empirical record of comparable price interventions and on the modeling of the IPCC pathways, the rate of decarbonization required to hold the temperature increase to levels in the lower portion of the range the international community had nominally committed to during the late industrial age (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change 2023; IPCC 2022; Stern 2007).

The stabilization is, on this account, not utopia. It does not undo the warming that has already occurred; it does not restore the species and ecosystems that have already been lost; it does not eliminate the climate damage that the next several decades will produce regardless of the actions taken now. What it does is to halt, by a defined date in the second half of the century, the further accumulation of warming, and to begin, on a longer horizon, the gradual return of atmospheric concentrations to levels consistent with stable climate conditions.

The condition of human life under stabilized climate is materially different from the condition of human life under continued warming. The agricultural systems on which the food supply depends operate within climate envelopes for which their varieties, infrastructure, and labor patterns are calibrated. The coastal cities on which a substantial fraction of the planetary population lives operate without the persistent and accelerating threat of inundation. The ecosystems on which the planetary biosphere depends operate within their adaptive ranges. The migration pressures that climate displacement would otherwise produce are reduced to manageable levels, with the substantial benefits to international stability that the reduction implies.

The stabilization is, in summary, the condition under which the substantive achievements of human civilization can be sustained for the long horizon. It is, in this sense, the precondition of every other possibility this chapter describes.

The repair of ecosystems

The price signals on resource depletion, the protected fund’s investments in ecological restoration, the harmonized regulation of cross-border environmental harms, and the planetary commons regime over the atmospheric, oceanic, and biological commons together produce conditions under which the active repair of damaged ecosystems becomes a substantial line of work. The work has, on the empirical record of contemporary restoration efforts at smaller scale, demonstrated capacity to recover degraded landscapes, to restore depleted fisheries, to recover endangered species populations, and to rebuild watershed function over decadal timescales (Raworth 2017).

The principal architectural contribution to the work is the funding model: the protected fund, drawing on a defined share of the planetary revenues and committed to long-horizon investments under the Tribune’s oversight, is the kind of patient capital ecological restoration requires. Restoration projects whose payoff is a generation away, whose benefits are diffuse and non-extractable, and whose appearance on a quarterly accounting is unimpressive have not, on the empirical record, been competently funded by the institutions of the late industrial age. The architecture provides them with funding adequate to their nature.

The work is unspectacular and slow. It does not produce, on a yearly basis, the kind of headline outcomes the political institutions of the late industrial age were calibrated to favor. It produces, over decades, the gradual return of damaged systems to functional operation, with the substantive benefits — the carbon sequestration, the biodiversity preservation, the watershed services, the soil stability, the resilience to climate stress — accumulating on the long horizon. The architecture’s commitment to the long horizon is the practical condition under which the work proceeds.

The end of absolute poverty

The unconditional floor of dignity has, by ten years out, materially eliminated absolute poverty within the federation. The condition is not, on the architectural sketch, marginal. The number of persons globally living at or below the threshold of absolute deprivation has, in the late industrial age, varied between several hundred million and several billion depending on the threshold and the year; the elimination of this condition by direct provision is, in itself, the most consequential achievement of the architecture for the persons whose lives are immediately affected (United Nations Development Programme 2024; World Bank 2024).

The elimination is not, in itself, the achievement of human flourishing. Persons lifted out of absolute deprivation have not, by that fact, achieved comprehensive welfare; the floor is, by design, a floor and not a ceiling. The achievement is, more accurately, the elimination of a condition in which substantive flourishing is impossible. With absolute deprivation eliminated, the further work of human flourishing — the development of capabilities, the construction of meaningful work, the participation in cultural and civic life, the formation of substantive communities — can proceed on a foundation that does not, daily, require the energy of those undertaking it to be devoted to mere survival.

The substantive consequences, on the architectural sketch, are large. Educational attainment, on the empirical record of comparable interventions at smaller scale, rises substantially in populations whose food security and material stability are secured. Health outcomes, on the same record, improve along multiple dimensions, with the most consequential improvements in child mortality, maternal mortality, and the chronic conditions of stress-related illness. The labor-force capacities of populations released from absolute deprivation, on the same record, rise substantially, with consequences for the productive capacity of the economies in which they operate (Sen 1999; Nussbaum 2011).

Restored work

The conditions of work under the architecture — the portable benefits, the restored countervailing voice, the lifelong retraining, the recognition of unwaged labor, the working-time discretion — produce, by ten years out, labor markets in which work is, for substantial fractions of the population, the source of meaning and identity that the philosophical tradition has identified as among the principal human goods. The shift is gradual, and is not, in any sense, a return to a previous configuration; it is the construction, in contemporary conditions, of work that affords the substantive goods of work (Graeber 2018).

The shift is, in particular, the result of the structural commitments of the architecture rather than of any direct policy on the content of work. The portability of benefits removes the structural pressure toward employment forms that maximize the security of the benefits at the cost of the meaningfulness of the work. The retraining infrastructure removes the structural pressure to remain in obsolete occupations for fear of the costs of transition. The recognition of unwaged labor removes the structural pressure to undertake paid work that is, on substantive grounds, less valuable than the unwaged work it displaces. The countervailing voice provides the substantive condition under which workers can shape the content and conditions of their work, rather than accepting whatever the structural pressure of the labor market produces.

Functional democracy

The conditions of public life under the architecture — the legibility of institutional operation, the protection of substantive media independence, the procedural protections against the capture of regulatory and legislative processes, the regulation of intelligent systems used in political communication, the structural commitments to honest enterprise — produce, by ten years out, conditions of public life in which substantive democratic deliberation is possible at scale. The condition is not, on the architectural sketch, perfect; the political pathologies of the late industrial age are, on the empirical record of comparable transitions, persistent in attenuated forms for at least a generation.

The substantive change is not the elimination of political conflict; political conflict is the proper content of democratic life. The substantive change is the operation of political conflict on terms that are themselves legible: the parties to the conflict are visible, their funding is disclosed, their claims are subject to truthfulness standards, the institutions through which the conflict is processed are operating with verifiable independence, and the outcomes of the conflict are recorded on terms that permit subsequent generations to learn from them (Habermas 1989a; Putnam 2000).

The functional democracy is, in this account, less an achievement than a maintenance task. The conditions under which it operates require continuous defense against the structural tendencies — the concentrations of wealth, the erosions of independent media, the captures of regulatory institutions — that, in the late industrial age, had progressively degraded its operation. The architecture provides the institutional means of the defense; the defense itself is the substantive work of the persons who operate the architecture’s institutions.

Long-horizon thinking

The architectural commitment to longevity, operationalized through the Tribune of Future Generations, the protected fund, and the procedural protections of long-horizon decisions, produces, by ten years out, public deliberation in which the long horizon is a substantive consideration in the contemporary work. The shift is, on the empirical record of comparable institutional commitments at smaller scale (the Welsh Future Generations legislation, the Hungarian Ombudsman, the Finnish Committee for the Future), gradual but real. The long horizon, when it is institutionally represented, is considered. The long horizon, when it is not, is, in the routine pressure of contemporary politics, systematically discounted.

The substantive content of long-horizon consideration is the consideration of the conditions under which the persons of subsequent generations will live. The consideration includes, but is not limited to: the climate trajectory, the resource trajectories, the technological trajectories, the demographic trajectories, the institutional trajectories. The consideration is not predictive; the conditions of the long horizon cannot be precisely predicted. The consideration is, more modestly, the substantive recognition that the choices of the contemporary generation produce conditions for which subsequent generations will bear the consequences, and that the substantive interests of subsequent generations have a legitimate place in the consideration.

The character of the possible

The possibilities described in this chapter are, in their substantive content, large. They are also, in their character, modest: they describe a planet whose climate is stabilized, whose ecosystems are gradually repaired, whose persons are not in absolute deprivation, whose work affords the goods of work, whose public life is functionally democratic, whose long horizons are institutionally considered. They do not describe a planet from which conflict has been eliminated, from which suffering has been removed, from which all the conditions of the human predicament have been resolved. The framers were conscious of the distinction.

The possibilities described are, in this account, the possibilities of normal life on a planet whose institutional foundations are adequate to its conditions. They are not the possibilities of any final achievement; they are, more accurately, the possibilities of the ordinary life of substantial human civilization, sustained over the long horizon by institutions that are themselves sustainable. The achievement, where it is achieved, is the achievement of normalcy at planetary scale — a normalcy that the late industrial age, on the empirical record of its accumulating crises, was demonstrably unable to maintain.

Chapter Twenty-Eight asks what becomes possible beyond the planet — the long horizon of human presence in the orbital, lunar, and asteroidal commons, on the institutional foundations the federation provides.

Chapter Twenty-Eight

What Becomes Possible Beyond Earth

Among the substantive consequences of an operating federation is the institutional foundation it provides for human activity beyond the surface of the planet. The activity has, in the late industrial age, advanced from the experimental stage of the mid-twentieth century to the operational stage of the early twenty-first, with substantial commercial activity in low Earth orbit, the resumption of crewed lunar exploration on the schedule of multiple national programs and private operators, the planning of asteroid prospecting missions whose commercial viability depends on the possibility of substantial extraction, and the longer-horizon prospect of permanent human settlement beyond Earth. The conditions under which this activity proceeds — the legal, economic, and political conditions — are not, in the late industrial age, settled; they are, on the empirical record, governed by a thin and aging body of treaty law from the 1960s and 1970s, supplemented by national legislation that varies substantially in its substantive commitments. The chapter that follows describes how the federation’s architectural commitments apply to the conditions of activity beyond Earth, with the same restraint that has governed the previous chapters.

The orbital commons

The orbital regime around Earth — the volume of space within which satellites operate, the radio-frequency spectrum used for communication with and among them, the navigational and scientific resources orbital infrastructure provides — is, in the architectural sketch, treated as a planetary commons. The treatment follows from the principles of Article XII: the resource is finite (orbital slots in the more crowded altitude bands are, on the empirical record of contemporary congestion, materially scarce), the resource is non-excludable (the operations of one party in orbit affect the operations of all parties), the resource cannot be sustainably allocated by the unilateral action of individual states.

The architectural commitments produce, in operation, several specific consequences. The federation’s planetary commons regime allocates orbital capacity by procedures that account for the legitimate uses of the commons by all parties, including parties whose contemporary capacity to operate in orbit is limited but whose interest in the persistence of the commons is substantive. The commercial users of the commons pay charges to the federation calibrated to the resource value, with the revenues credited to the planetary basic provision and to the protected fund’s long-horizon investments in space infrastructure. The accumulation of debris in orbit, which has, on the empirical record of recent decades, become a substantial threat to the persistence of the commons itself, is regulated by enforceable rules with cleanup obligations on the parties responsible for the debris (Ostrom 1990).

The framers were conscious that the orbital commons regime is, of all the components of the architecture, the most directly dependent on the cooperation of the principal contemporary spacefaring powers. The federation’s authority over the commons is, accordingly, exercised through cooperative arrangements with non-member states whose space programs are substantial, with the federation’s contribution being the institutional capacity to coordinate the cooperation rather than the unilateral assertion of authority over parties not subject to its jurisdiction. The disposition is consistent with the architecture’s general approach to the international plane.

The lunar surface

The lunar surface, and the resources of the Moon, are, on the architectural sketch, governed by an extension of the planetary commons regime. The principal substantive commitments are: the persistence of the lunar surface as a common heritage, in which the activity of any party is conducted with consideration for the legitimate interests of all; the regulation of resource extraction by procedures calibrated to the long-horizon sustainability of the resource and to the equitable participation of parties whose contemporary capacity to operate is limited; the protection of scientific sites, sites of historical significance, and sites of unusual geological character, by procedures comparable to those that govern the protection of comparable sites on Earth; the regulation of permanent human presence on the Moon by procedures that protect both the persons present and the lunar environment.

The framers were conscious that the lunar surface, unlike orbital space, is, on the empirical record of contemporary plans, becoming a site of permanent human presence on the multi-decade horizon. The presence will require infrastructure: living quarters, life-support systems, energy systems, transportation infrastructure, communications infrastructure. The infrastructure is, in part, the proper subject of commercial activity by enterprises whose investments are at risk; in part, it is the proper subject of public investment by the federation and by member polities whose space programs operate. The architectural commitments balance the commercial and the public, with the principal commitments — to the lunar commons, to scientific access, to the protection of the environment — taking precedence over the commercial.

The framers regarded the lunar regime as one of the architectural domains where the long horizon is most substantively at issue. The decisions of the contemporary generation about how the Moon is used will, on the multi-century horizon, shape the conditions under which subsequent generations encounter the lunar environment. The Tribune’s standing, on lunar policy as on other long-horizon matters, is the institutional means by which the long horizon is represented in the contemporary deliberation.

Asteroid resources

The substantial resources of the asteroid belt — the metals, the volatiles, the rare materials whose extraction on Earth produces substantial environmental damage — are, on the architectural sketch, governed as a common-pool resource under procedures calibrated to the resource’s character. The procedures permit substantial commercial activity, on the basis that the resource is, by Earth standards, effectively limitless, while protecting the legitimate interests in equitable participation, in the long-horizon stewardship of the resource, and in the prevention of the concentrations of capability that the asteroid economy might otherwise produce.

The principal architectural commitments are: the licensing of extraction by the federation’s planetary commons regime, with charges calibrated to the substantive value of the resource and credited to the planetary basic provision; the equitable participation in the commercial activity by enterprises whose home polities are members of the federation, on terms that prevent the concentration of the resource under any single principal; the long-horizon protection of the asteroid environment, including the protection of certain bodies designated for scientific study or for cultural significance; the regulation of the use of asteroid resources to ensure that the consequences of their use — including the consequences for the orbital and lunar environments and for the climate of Earth, when the resources are used in ways that affect it — are accounted for in the regulatory framework.

The framers were conscious that the asteroid economy is, of all the prospects beyond Earth, the one with the most substantial potential commercial scale and, accordingly, the one most likely to produce the kinds of structural concentrations the architecture is designed to prevent. The architectural attention to the prevention is, accordingly, substantial. The principal architectural defense is, as on Earth, the structural commitment to competition: the licensing of extraction is conducted on terms that prevent the accumulation of dominant positions, the entry of new participants is structurally facilitated, the substantive commitments of the architecture to the prevention of monopoly extend to the asteroid economy as they extend to the terrestrial economy.

The longer horizon

The longer horizon of human presence beyond Earth — the prospect of permanent settlement on Mars, the prospect of presence in the outer solar system, the longer prospect of presence beyond the solar system on timescales that are, by the standards of contemporary technology, speculative — is not, in this chapter, addressed in detail. The framers’ working assumption is that the conditions of the longer horizon will be substantially different from the conditions of the contemporary period, that the institutional arrangements appropriate to the longer horizon will be properly the work of the generations that face them, and that the architecture’s contribution to the longer horizon is the institutional foundation rather than the substantive specification.

The principal architectural commitment relevant to the longer horizon is the commitment to the long horizon itself. The protected fund’s investments, on the multi-decade and multi-century horizons, include the substantial public investment in the scientific and technological foundations on which the longer horizon will rest. The Tribune’s standing, on long-horizon policy, includes the standing to advocate for the legitimate interests of the persons of subsequent centuries in the conditions of human presence beyond Earth. The federation’s institutional capacity, sustained across generations, is the institutional foundation on which the longer-horizon decisions can be taken legitimately and operationally.

The framers regarded the longer horizon as substantively important. The persistence of human civilization, on geological timescales, is materially advanced by the existence of human communities beyond Earth. The diversification of the substrate on which human civilization operates is, against the substantive risks the contemporary generation has identified, a substantive contribution to the resilience of the civilization as a whole. The architectural commitments do not, in themselves, achieve the diversification; they provide the institutional conditions under which the work of diversification, conducted by the parties who undertake it, can proceed on terms compatible with the legitimate interests of all parties on Earth and beyond.

The character of the work beyond Earth

The work beyond Earth is, on the architectural sketch, the work of substantial generations on substantial timescales, with the contemporary generation contributing the institutional foundations and the early operational steps. The work is not, on this account, the principal achievement of the architecture; the principal achievement, as Chapter Twenty-Seven described, is the stabilization of the conditions of human life on Earth. The work beyond Earth is, more accurately, an extension of the architecture’s commitments to a domain in which the same principles — legibility, subsidiarity, reversibility, accountability, dignity, custody, longevity — apply with appropriate adjustments.

The framers regarded the extension as substantive but not transformative. The work beyond Earth, conducted on the basis of the architectural commitments, becomes, over the decades and centuries, a substantial component of the human enterprise. It does not replace the work on Earth; the work on Earth remains, on every substantial measure, the principal site of human civilization. It complements the work on Earth, with the substantive benefits — the scientific knowledge, the resource access, the resilience to substantive risks, the long-horizon viability of the civilization — accumulating over the long horizon.

The architecture’s contribution, in this domain as in all the others it addresses, is the institutional foundation. The substantive content of the work beyond Earth, like the substantive content of the work on Earth, is the work of the persons and institutions that undertake it, on terms they will set, within the conditions the architecture provides. The conditions are the contribution; the work, beyond Earth and on it, is what the conditions make possible.

An epilogue follows, returning the voice of the work to its author and addressing, in the first person, what has been undertaken in writing this book and what is asked of those who read it.

Epilogue

A Note from the Author

The voice of the preceding chapters has, by deliberate choice, been the impersonal voice of the architect. The voice was a discipline: a writer who proposes constitutional commitments at planetary scale must, if the proposal is to be taken seriously, suppress the small accidents of his own life and address the reader on the substantive merits of the proposal. The discipline has been, on balance, the right one for the work. In this concluding section, however, I depart from it briefly, on grounds that I believe the reader is owed a small accounting of the person who has written what they have read.

I am Mircea S. Rusu. I am, by formation, a builder of systems — software, infrastructure, the kind of operational architectures that make ordinary digital life function. I have spent the working years of my adulthood close enough to the institutional and technical arrangements of the present age to see, as a practitioner sees, the points at which they fail and the directions in which the failures compound. I am not a constitutional lawyer; I am not a political scientist; I am not an economist by training. I claim no specialized authority beyond what the substantive content of the argument carries on its own.

The book has been written, more than for any other reason, because the conditions it describes seemed to me to be drifting toward consequences that no responsible adult could observe in silence. The trust failures, the distributional failures, the failures of long-horizon thinking, the emerging governance failures around intelligent systems, the persistent inability of the international order to address matters that are, by their nature, planetary — these are not abstractions to me. I see them, in their daily operation, in the technical and institutional work I do. The book is the response of a builder who, having seen the failures, has tried to specify, with the discipline of a working architecture, what would have to be different.

I want to be clear about what I have not claimed. I have not claimed that the architecture proposed here is correct in every detail. The detail of any constitutional architecture is, on the historical record of comparable foundings, inevitably revised by the practical experience of operation. I have claimed, more modestly, that the architecture is sufficient to begin: that a federation founded on these commitments, operating these institutions, exercising these competences, would be in a position to address, by legitimate institutional means, the conditions that the present arrangements have proven unable to address. The sufficiency to begin is, in any constitutional founding, the substantive question. The subsequent refinement is the work of those who operate what has been begun.

I have not claimed that the architecture is achievable on any specific schedule. The political conditions under which a founding of this kind could begin are not, on the empirical record of the present moment, in immediate prospect. They may be, on a horizon of years or decades, brought into prospect by the accumulation of conditions that the present order cannot address. They may, on a longer horizon, be brought into prospect by deliberate political work. They may not be brought into prospect at all, and the conditions diagnosed in Part One may continue to compound until they produce the catastrophic ruptures that the architecture is constructed to prevent. I do not predict which of these will occur. I have written, instead, a planning document that is available, to those who undertake the political work, as one of the materials with which the work can begin.

I have not claimed, in any sense, that I myself will lead, organize, or otherwise undertake the political work. I am not a politician; I have no organizational platform for this kind of work; I have no constituency beyond the persons who, having read the book, find in its substance reason to take its proposals seriously. The book is an offering to whoever, of the political actors of subsequent years, finds use for it. If it is useful to none, the offering will have failed; if it is useful to some, the offering will have served its purpose.

I am conscious that the book asks something of its readers. It asks them to consider, with substantive attention, conditions that are easier to look away from. It asks them to weigh, against the daily pressures of ordinary life, the substantive proposals for addressing those conditions. It asks them, where the proposals seem to them sound, to do what citizens of any constitutional order do: to advocate, to organize, to support the political actors who undertake the work, to participate in the deliberation by which substantive change becomes possible. The asking is the asking of any serious work of constitutional thought, addressed to the public on whom the substantive choices ultimately depend.

I close with the proposition that the work undertaken in this book is the work of an ordinary citizen, addressed to ordinary citizens, on the conditions of ordinary life on a planet whose institutional foundations are inadequate to the contemporary conditions. The proposal for adequate foundations is, on the historical record of comparable proposals, neither inevitable nor impossible. It is, more modestly, available — available to those who, having considered the substantive merits, find in them reason to undertake the work.

I thank the reader for the time given to the argument. I welcome correction on any point on which I am wrong. I welcome continuation by any party who finds use for the materials. The work is offered, in the substantive sense, to the reader; what is done with it is the reader’s to decide.

Mircea S. Rusu
2026

Glossary

Defined Terms

The terms below are used with specific meanings throughout this work. Where a term appears with a meaning different from its ordinary use, the definition here governs.

Architecture. The integrated set of constitutional commitments, institutional structures, procedural rules, and operational subsystems described in this book. Used in distinction from the substantive policies the architecture enables, which remain the choice of the parties who operate the architecture.

Citizens’ Assembly. The chamber of the federation’s bicameral legislature representing persons directly, with members selected by random draw stratified by demographic and geographic factors, serving non-renewable terms of three years.

Constitutional Court. The federation’s highest tribunal, with exclusive jurisdiction over the interpretation of the constitution and the organic statutes, and defined original and appellate jurisdiction in cases involving the federation’s law.

Council of Polities. The chamber of the federation’s bicameral legislature representing member polities, with weighted voting calibrated to population and to a minimum representational floor.

Custody Authority. The federation’s regulatory authority over intelligent systems above defined capability thresholds, established by Article VIII of the constitution and operating under the principles of Chapter Eighteen.

Federation. The constitutional federation of polities described in this work, exercising defined competence in matters that are, by their nature, planetary, while reserving all other competence to the polities.

Floor of Dignity. The unconditional substantive minimum below which no person within the federation may fall, comprising the six entitlements of Article V: nutrition adequate for health, shelter adequate for safety, clothing adequate for climate, sanitary water and waste disposal, basic medical care, and basic education.

Intelligent System. An artificial computational system whose operations include capabilities of perception, reasoning, decision, or action that, in human persons, would be regarded as intelligent. Used as a generic term, without commitment to any particular technical architecture.

Member Polity. A constitutionally constituted political community that has ratified the federation’s constitution and acceded to membership on terms negotiated with the federation.

Organic Statute. A federation statute, enacted by the procedures of Article XIII, that operationalizes the constitutional commitments. Organic statutes have constitutional standing within their domains and may be amended only by the procedures specified in the constitution itself.

Planetary Basic Provision. The unconditional cash transfer described in Article V, operationally calibrated to the floor of dignity, funded from the planetary revenues of the federation, and disbursed to persons within the federation regardless of employment, conduct, or other condition.

Planetary Commons. The atmospheric, oceanic, orbital, lunar, deep-seabed, electromagnetic, and biological commons, the use of which by any party affects the use available to all parties, governed by the federation under Article XII.

Polity. A constitutionally constituted political community within the federation. Used in preference to “state” to emphasize that the federation’s members may be of various constitutional forms (national states, regional confederations, indigenous polities) and that the architectural relationship is to the constituted political community, regardless of its specific form.

Principal. The person, enterprise, or institution that operates an intelligent system and bears the constitutional and statutory responsibilities for its operation under the custody regime.

Protected Fund. The federation’s long-horizon fund, drawing on a defined share of planetary revenues, committed to investments whose returns accumulate on multi-decade and multi-generational horizons, under the supervision of the Tribune of Future Generations.

Public Ledger. The federation’s cryptographically signed, append-only, public record of institutional acts, described in Chapter Sixteen and operating as the foundation of the legibility commitment.

Reversibility. The architectural principle, articulated in Chapter Nine, that decisions of substantial public consequence are taken on terms that permit revision in light of subsequent learning, with deliberate institutional preference for reversible options where reversible and irreversible options are otherwise comparable.

Subsidiarity. The architectural principle, articulated in Chapter Eight, that authority is exercised at the lowest tier capable of effective exercise, with higher tiers acting only on matters whose proper handling exceeds the capacity of lower tiers.

Tribune of Future Generations. The federation officer with standing to advocate, in defined matters, for the legitimate interests of the persons of subsequent generations. Established by Article XI and operating under the principles of Chapter Thirteen.

Acknowledgments

A book of this scope is, in any honest accounting, the product of more minds than the one whose name appears on the cover. The argument presented here draws on a long tradition of constitutional, economic, philosophical, and institutional thought, much of it represented in the bibliography that follows. Where the argument is sound, the credit belongs in substantial part to the writers, scholars, and practitioners on whose work it builds. Where the argument is wrong, the responsibility is mine.

I am grateful to the many writers, working over centuries, whose substantive contributions made the argument of this book possible: the constitutional theorists from Aristotle through Rawls, the economists from Smith through Ostrom, the institutional scholars whose patient work documented the conditions under which institutions succeed and fail, the historians whose long view provided the context within which contemporary conditions can be properly understood. The argument here is, in substantial part, the synthesis of the substantive insights they produced.

I am grateful to the practitioners whose operational work, in the various domains the architecture addresses, gave me the substantive understanding of how institutions actually function: the engineers who build and maintain the technical infrastructures of contemporary life, the public servants whose patient work makes the institutions of the present age operate to the extent they do, the academic scholars whose continuing study of contemporary conditions provided the empirical foundation on which the architectural sketch could be built.

I am grateful to those who, in the development of the argument over the years preceding the writing, served as substantive interlocutors: the friends, colleagues, and correspondents who, by patient engagement with the propositions as they developed, helped me to refine them, to identify their weaknesses, to consider the objections they would face, to address the objections that were substantive, and to set aside the propositions that, on closer examination, did not survive substantive engagement.

I am grateful, finally, to the readers of this book. The work of constitutional thought is a public work, addressed to a public whose substantive engagement is the condition of its having any substantive consequence. To those who have read the work, considered its propositions, and weighed them against the conditions of their own substantive understanding: my thanks. The continuation of the work, in whatever form it takes, is in your hands.

About the Author

Mircea S. Rusu is a Romanian-born systems architect and writer working at the intersection of computational infrastructure, applied artificial intelligence, and institutional design. His professional work has spanned the development of digital marketplaces, the engineering of inference systems for large-scale machine intelligence, and the design of operational architectures for distributed computation. The substantive concern that runs through his technical work — the construction of systems that operate reliably at scale under conditions of substantial complexity — is the same substantive concern that motivates this work of institutional thought.

The author writes about civilization, governance, technology, and the long horizon. The Architect is his treatise on the constitutional foundations of a planetary federation adequate to the conditions of the contemporary age. Subsequent works are in preparation.

The author can be reached through the publisher of this volume, on terms appropriate to substantive correspondence about the work.

Source Code & Open Repository

The architectural sketch described in this volume is, in its institutional substance, a public proposal: a working draft offered to the deliberation of any party who finds it useful. Consistent with the architecture’s own commitment to legibility, the manuscript sources of this book — the Markdown source files, the bibliographic database, the typographic templates, and the build scripts that produce the printed and electronic editions — are maintained as an open repository under a permissive license.

The repository is intended to support three purposes. First, it permits independent verification of the citations, the figures, and the substantive claims of the text against the sources from which they are drawn. Second, it permits translation, adaptation, and study of the work by parties whose access to the conventional publishing channels is limited. Third, it permits the constitutional and statutory drafts in the appendices to be used, in whole or in part and with appropriate attribution, by drafters of subsequent institutional work for whom the present text serves as a starting point.

Repository location

github.com/mirceasilviurusu/the-architect

The repository contains the complete manuscript in English and Romanian, the bibliographic database, the constitutional and organic-statute drafts, and the typographic and build infrastructure. Errata, reader correspondence accepted for public reply, and subsequent editions are issued through the same repository.

License and attribution

The text of this volume is offered under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike license (CC BY-SA 4.0). Readers, translators, adaptors, and republishers are free to use the work, to produce derivative works from it, and to distribute it commercially or non-commercially, provided that the author is credited, that derivative works are offered under the same license, and that the substantive integrity of the constitutional and statutory drafts is preserved when they are reproduced.

The build infrastructure (typographic templates, citation styling, automation scripts) is offered separately under the MIT License.

A note to publishers and platforms

This volume is made available through several publishing channels in addition to the open repository: print and electronic editions through trade and on-demand publishers, electronic editions through retail platforms, and academic preprint archives. The author and publishers regard the presence of the work in multiple channels as consistent with the architecture’s commitment to legibility; readers who acquire the work through any channel are welcome to consult the open repository for the sources from which the substantive material is drawn.

— the architecture is the contribution; the work is what the conditions make possible —
